results.translation:
shrubs. It now remains for us to complete our account of all the cereals, and we shall here devote a portion of that account to the diseases to which they are subject.

CHAPTER 44. (17.) — THE DISEASES OF GRAIN: THE OAT.

The principal form of disease in wheat is the oat. Barley, too, will degenerate into the oat; so much so, indeed, that the oat has come to serve as an equivalent for grain [corn]; for the people of Germany are accustomed to sowing it, and make their porridge [puls] of nothing else. This degeneration arises most particularly from humidity of soil and climate; a second cause is a weakness in the seed, resulting from its being retained too long in the ground before it emerges above the surface. The same result will follow if the seed is decayed when placed in the ground. This defect, however, may be recognized the moment the plant makes its appearance, from which it is quite clear that the fault lies in the root. There is another form of disease as well, closely resembling the oat, which occurs when the grain, already developed to its full size but not yet ripe, is struck by a harmful blast of wind before it has acquired its proper substance and strength; in this case the seed withers away in the ear, by a kind of abortion as it were, and disappears entirely. Wind is harmful to wheat and barley at three periods of the year in particular: when they are in blossom, immediately after the blossom has fallen, and just as the seed is beginning to ripen. In the last case the grain wastes away, while in the two former cases it is prevented from developing at all. Intermittent gleams of sunshine breaking through clouds are harmful to grain. Maggots [grubs] also breed in the roots when the rains that follow seed-time are succeeded by a sudden heat, which seals the moisture within the ground. Maggots likewise appear in the grain itself when the ear ferments due to heat following a fall of rain. There is also a small beetle known by the name of "cantharis" [blister-beetle type], which eats away the blade [leaf]. All these insects die, however, as soon as their source of nourishment is exhausted. Oil, pitch, and grease are harmful to grain, and care should be taken not to allow them to come into contact with seed that is to be sown. Rain is beneficial to grain only while it is in the blade [early leaf stage]; it is harmful to wheat and barley while in blossom, though it is not detrimental to leguminous plants, with the exception of the chick-pea. When grain is beginning to ripen, rain is harmful, and most especially so to barley. There is a white grass that grows in fields, very similar in appearance to panic-grass [panicum], but fatal to cattle. As to...

entities_mentioned.name: oat
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny, following Theophrastus, mistakenly regards the oat as a diseased or degenerate form of wheat or barley rather than a distinct species. He notes its use as a staple food (porridge) among Germanic peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum or related Triticum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Discussed as subject to degeneration into oat, wind damage, and insect attack.

entities_mentioned.name: barley
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to degenerate into oat; particularly vulnerable to rain during ripening.

entities_mentioned.name: cantharis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown coleopterous (beetle) insect; not Lytta vesicatoria (Spanish fly)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's editor notes this is likely some now-unknown beetle that feeds on grain blades. Dioscorides and Athenaeus corroborate this description. Not to be confused with the blister-beetle Lytta vesicatoria.

entities_mentioned.name: white grass
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Holcus lanatus (Yorkshire fog) or another pale-leafed grass species; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling panic-grass (Panicum) and as fatal to cattle. Identity uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: panic / panic-grass
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum or related Panicum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator in describing the appearance of the white grass.

entities_mentioned.name: chick-pea
entities_mentioned.notes: Singled out as the only leguminous plant harmed by rain during blossom, unlike other legumes.

entities_mentioned.name: maggots / grubs (in roots and grain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely larvae of various dipteran or coleopteran insects (e.g., Hessian fly larvae, Mayetiola destructor, or similar grain pests)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny describes two distinct infestations: one in the roots caused by heat following rain after sowing, and one in the ear caused by fermentation from heat after rain.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English translation already (from a Victorian-era edition of Pliny's Natural Historia, Book XVIII), so the task here is primarily to clean up OCR errors (e.g., 'QKAIN' corrected to 'GRAIN'), standardize formatting, and clarify technical vocabulary. Footnotes have been incorporated as contextual notes where relevant. The notion that oats are a degenerate form of wheat is an ancient error shared by Theophrastus and, surprisingly, later adopted by Buffon. The term 'cantharis' in classical usage here does not refer to the medicinal blister-beetle (Lytta vesicatoria) but to an unidentified grain-feeding beetle. The phrase 'pines away in the ear, by a kind of abortion' reflects classical agricultural terminology for grain blasting or blighting.

results.title_inferred: Diseases and Pests of Grain Crops, and Their Remedies (Pliny, Natural History)
results.original_text: Among the maladies that attack the cereals, but rather as so many pests inflicted on the earth. Mildew, a malady resulting from the inclemency of the weather, and equally attacking the vine and corn, is in no degree less injurious. It attacks corn most frequently in localities which are exposed to dews, and in valleys which have not a thorough draught for the wind; windy and elevated spots, on the other hand, are totally exempt from it. Another evil, again, in corn, is over-luxuriance, when it falls to the ground beneath the weight of the grain. One evil, however, to which all crops in common, the chick-pea even, are exposed, is the attacks of the caterpillar, when the rain, by washing away the natural saltness of the vegetation, makes it all the more tempting for its sweetness. There is a certain plant, too, which kills the chick-pea and the fitch, by twining around them; the name of it is "orobanche." In a similar manner, also, wheat is attacked by darnel, barley by a long-stalked plant, called "segilops," and the lentil by an axe-leafed grass, to which, from the resemblance of the leaf, the Greeks have given the name of "pelecinom." All these plants, too, kill the others by entwining around them. In the neighbourhood of Philippi, there is a plant known as ateramon, which grows in a rich soil, and kills the bean, after it has been exposed, while wet, to the blasts of a certain wind: when it grows in a thin, light soil, this plant is called "teramon." The seed of darnel is extremely minute, and is enclosed in a prickly husk. If introduced into bread, it will speedily produce vertigo; and it is said that in Asia and Greece, the bath-keepers, when they want to disperse a crowd of people, throw this seed upon burning coals. The phalangium, a diminutive insect of the spider genus, breeds in the fitch, if the winter happens to be wet. Slugs, too, breed in the vetch, and sometimes a tiny snail makes its way out of the ground, and eats it away in a most singular manner. These are pretty nearly all the maladies to which grain is subject. CHAP. 45. — THE BEST REMEDIES FOR THE DISEASES OF GRAIN. The best remedy for these maladies, so long as grain is in the blade, is the weeding-hook.
results.translation: Among the maladies that attack cereal crops—regarded rather as plagues visited upon the earth—mildew [rubigo], a malady arising from inclement weather, attacks both the vine and corn equally and is no less harmful. It strikes corn most frequently in localities exposed to dew and in valleys where there is no thorough draft of wind; windy, elevated sites, on the other hand, are entirely exempt. Another ill afflicting corn is over-luxuriance, when the stalk falls to the ground under the weight of the grain itself. One evil to which all crops alike are subject—even the chick-pea—is the assault of caterpillars: rain washes away the natural saltiness of the plants and renders them all the more palatable by their sweetness. There is also a certain plant that kills the chick-pea and the fitch [vetch] by twining around them; it is called 'orobanche' [broomrape]. In a like manner, wheat is attacked by darnel [lolium], barley by a long-stalked plant called 'segilops' [aegilops], and the lentil by an axe-leaved grass to which the Greeks, from the resemblance of its leaf to a battle-axe [pelekys], have given the name 'pelecinum.' All of these plants also kill their hosts by entwining around them. Near Philippi there is a plant known as 'ateramon,' which grows in rich soil and kills the bean after the plant has been wetted and exposed to blasts of a certain wind; when growing in thin, light soil, this same plant is called 'teramon.' The seed of darnel is extremely small and enclosed in a prickly husk. If it finds its way into bread, it will quickly produce vertigo; and it is reported that in Asia and Greece, bath-keepers, wishing to clear a crowd of people, throw this seed upon burning coals. The phalangium, a tiny spider-like insect, breeds in the fitch when winters are wet. Slugs also breed in the vetch, and sometimes a small snail emerges from the ground and consumes it in a most remarkable fashion. These are nearly all the maladies to which grain is subject. CHAPTER 45 — THE BEST REMEDIES FOR THE DISEASES OF GRAIN. The best remedy for these maladies, so long as the grain is still in the blade, is the weeding-hook.

entities_mentioned.name: Mildew (rubigo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uredo spp. (rust fungi affecting barley, wheat, oats, millet); Erineum vitis on vines
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's 'rubigo' corresponds to modern botanical 'uredo' or cereal rust fungi; on vines, identified as Erineum vitis by botanists.

entities_mentioned.name: Orobanche
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orobanche spp. (broomrape); possibly Cuscuta europaea (dodder) per footnote
entities_mentioned.notes: The text names it as a parasite twining around chick-pea and fitch; the footnote suggests Cuscuta europaea (dodder, one of the Convolvuli), though the name 'orobanche' is traditionally associated with broomrape.

entities_mentioned.name: Darnel (lolium / aera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny uses 'lolium' and 'aera' somewhat interchangeably; seed causes vertigo if ground into bread. Bath-keepers reportedly used it on coals to disperse crowds.

entities_mentioned.name: Segilops (aegilops)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aegilops ovata (ovate goatgrass); possibly Avena sterilis (barren oat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Long-stalked plant attacking barley. Footnote cites Dalechamps and Hardouin identifying it with Avena sterilis.

entities_mentioned.name: Pelecinum (pelecinum / axe-leafed grass)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Biserrula pelecina; possibly Astragalus hamosus or Coronilla securidaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Named from Greek 'pelekys' (battle-axe) due to leaf shape; attacks lentils.

entities_mentioned.name: Ateramon / Teramon
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny apparently made an error in translating Theophrastus (de Causis, B. iv, c. 14); Theophrastus only mentions a cold wind injuring beans. The plant names ateramon (rich soil) and teramon (thin soil) may be a misidentification or textual corruption.

entities_mentioned.name: Phalangium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A spider or spider-like arachnid (possibly Phalangium opilio or related species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a 'diminutive insect of the spider genus'; breeds in fitch during wet winters.

entities_mentioned.name: Chick-pea
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as host of orobanche and vulnerable to caterpillar attack.

entities_mentioned.name: Fitch / Vetch
entities_mentioned.notes: Host of orobanche and phalangium; slugs breed in vetch.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentil
entities_mentioned.notes: Attacked by pelecinum (axe-leafed grass).

results.translator_notes: This segment is primarily a translation from Pliny's Latin into English (the source text already appears in English as a 19th-century scholarly translation, here cleaned of OCR artifacts). The footnotes embedded in the original have been preserved as contextual annotations in the entities section rather than inline. Pliny's noted error in translating Theophrastus regarding 'ateramon' is flagged. The use of darnel seed to produce vertigo and to disperse crowds from baths is a notable ethnobotanical/toxic plant reference. The schema for 'condition/ailment treated' is not directly applicable to this segment, which concerns agricultural pests and plant diseases rather than human medicine; however, the toxic effect of darnel (Lolium temulentum) causing vertigo in humans when baked into bread is medically relevant.

results.translation:
They will be all the larger. Some persons, again, are of opinion that they will grow to increased size if the seed is steeped for three days before sowing in a solution of urine and water. If the ground is also hoed three times, a modius [a Roman dry measure] of beans in the pod will yield, they say, not less than a modius of shelled beans. [Note: The vicinity of Philippi is said to make beans difficult to cook or boil. Commentators Hardouin and Fee debate whether Pliny coined two imaginary plant names, 'ateramon' and 'teramon,' and whether he truly understood the Greek sources he drew upon, including Theophrastus, Historia Animalium, Book VIII, chapter 10.]

Other seeds, it is said, will be exempt from the attacks of maggots if bruised cypress leaves are mixed with them, or if they are sown at the moment of the moon's conjunction [new moon]. Many persons, for the more effectual protection of millet, recommend that a bramble-frog [likely a common frog or toad found among brambles] should be carried at night around the field before the hoeing is done, and then buried in an earthen vessel in the middle of the field. If this is done, they say, neither sparrows nor worms will attack the crop. The frog, however, must be disinterred before the millet is cut; for if this is neglected, the produce will be bitter. It is also claimed that all seeds which have been touched by the shoulders of a mole are remarkably productive.

Democritus recommends that all seeds before sowing should be steeped in the juice of the herb known as 'aizoon' [Greek: 'always living'], which grows on tiles or shingles, and is known to us by the Latin names 'sedum' or 'digitellum' ['little finger,' from the shape of its leaves].

If blight prevails, or if worms are found adhering to the roots, a very common remedy is to sprinkle the plants with pure amurca [the watery lees or dregs of olive oil] without salt, and then to hoe the ground. If, however, the crop is beginning to joint [form nodes on the stem], it should be stubbed [cleared] at once, lest the weeds gain the upper hand.

I know for certain that flights of starlings and sparrows — those pests to millet and panic [Panicum, a type of millet] — are effectually driven away by means of a certain herb, the name of which is unknown to me, being buried at the four corners of the field: it is a wonderful thing to relate, but in such case not a single bird will enter it.

Mice are kept away by the ashes of a weasel or a cat being steeped in water and then thrown upon the seed, or else by using the water in which the body of a weasel or a cat has been boiled. The odour of these animals makes itself perceived even in the bread; for which reason it is generally thought a better plan to steep the seed in ox-gall [bile]. As for mildew, that greatest curse of all to corn, if branches of laurel are [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: beans (in pod and shelled)
entities_mentioned.notes: Discussed in the context of yield improvement through pre-sowing urine-water soaking and repeated hoeing. Beans near Philippi noted as difficult to cook.

entities_mentioned.name: cypress leaves
entities_mentioned.notes: Bruised leaves mixed with seed to protect against maggot infestation. Fee suggests the odour might deter noxious insects.

entities_mentioned.name: aizoon / sedum / digitellum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sedum acre (stonecrop / houseleek family; Pliny likely means Sempervivum tectorum or a related Crassulaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing on tiles or shingles; Greek name means 'always living' (perennial). Democritus recommended steeping seed in its juice before sowing. Pliny cross-references Book XXV, chapter 102. Modern identification debated between Sedum acre and Sempervivum tectorum.

entities_mentioned.name: amurca of olives
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous lees/dregs of olive oil pressing
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied without salt as a spray to plants suffering blight or root worms, followed by hoeing.

entities_mentioned.name: millet
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum (common millet) and/or Setaria italica (foxtail millet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Protected from birds (sparrows, starlings) and worms by various folk remedies including burial of a frog and burial of an unknown herb at field corners.

entities_mentioned.name: panic
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum or a related Panicum species
entities_mentioned.notes: A type of millet grain; listed alongside millet as a target of bird pests.

entities_mentioned.name: bramble-frog
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Rana temporaria or a common European frog/toad
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a folk ritual: carried around a millet field at night before hoeing, then buried in an earthen vessel in the center to deter sparrows and worms. Must be dug up before harvest or produce will be bitter.

entities_mentioned.name: mole (shoulders of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds said to be remarkably productive if touched by a mole's shoulders; clearly a piece of folk superstition.

entities_mentioned.name: weasel (ashes and boiled water of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ashes steeped in water or water in which a weasel body was boiled used to repel mice from seed. Odour noted to taint bread.

entities_mentioned.name: cat (ashes and boiled water of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Same use as weasel for mouse repulsion from stored/sown seed.

entities_mentioned.name: ox-gall
entities_mentioned.notes: Preferred alternative to weasel/cat preparations for steeping seed, as it does not taint bread with odour.

entities_mentioned.name: laurel (branches)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a remedy against mildew in corn; passage is incomplete in this chunk.

entities_mentioned.name: unknown herb (bird-repelling)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny states he does not know its name; buried at the four corners of a field to drive away starlings and sparrows. Cannot be identified.

entities_mentioned.name: ateramon / teramon
entities_mentioned.notes: Described by commentators as imaginary plant names coined from a Greek word; not genuine botanical entities.

results.translator_notes: This chunk is a combination of Pliny's original text (Book XVIII of Naturalis Historia) and editorial/commentary footnotes from a 19th-century English edition, which are interspersed with the primary text and not always clearly distinguished. The footnotes from Hardouin, Fee, and references to Virgil's Georgics (i.193) and Theophrastus have been retained in brackets within the translation to preserve scholarly context. The passage on 'aizoon/sedum/digitellum' is cross-referenced by Pliny himself to Book XXV, chapter 102. Several remedies described are clearly magical or superstitious in nature (frog burial, mole shoulders, herb at field corners) rather than pharmacological. OCR artifacts such as 'aizoiim' have been silently corrected to 'aizoon' and 'liowever' to 'however.' The text cuts off mid-sentence at the laurel/mildew remedy.

results.title_inferred: On the Management of Grain Crops and the Soils Best Suited to Various Grains (Pliny, Natural History, Book XVIII, Chapters 45–46)
results.original_text:
laurel. Over-luxuriance in corn is repressed by the teeth of cattle, but only while it is in the blade; in which case, if depastured upon ever so often, no injury to it when in the ear will be the result. If the ear, too, is once cut off, the grain, it is well known, will assume a larger form, but will be hollow within and worthless, and if sown, will come to nothing. At Babylon, however, they cut the blade twice, and then let the cattle pasture on it a third time, for otherwise it would run to nothing but leaf. Even then, however, so fertile is the soil, that it yields fifty, and, indeed, with care, as much as a hundred, fold. Nor is the cultivation of it attended with any difficulty, the only object being to let the ground be under water as long as possible, in order that the extreme richness and exuberance of the soil may be modified. The Euphrates, however, and the Tigris do not deposit a slime, in the same way that the Nilus does in Egypt, nor does the soil produce vegetation spontaneously; but still, so great is the fertility, that, although the seed is only trodden in with the foot, a crop springs up spontaneously the following year. So great a difference in soils as this, reminds me that I ought to take this opportunity of specifying those which are the best adapted for the various kinds of grain.

CHAP. 46. THE CROPS THAT SHOULD BE SOWN IN THE DIFFERENT SOILS.

This, then, is the opinion expressed by Cato on the subject: "In a dense and fertile soil wheat should be sown: but if the locality is subject to fogs, rape, radishes, millet, and panic. Where the land is cold and moist, sowing should be commenced earlier; but where it is hot, at a later period. In a red, black, or gravelly soil, provided it is not watery, lupines should be sown; but in chalk, red earth, or a watery soil, spelt. Where a locality is dry, free from weeds, and not overshadowed, wheat should be put in; and where the soil is strong and powerful, beans. Vetches should be grown in a soil as free from water and weeds as possible; while wheat and winter wheat are best adapted to an open, elevated locality, fully exposed to the warmth of the sun. The lentil thrives best in a meagre, red earth, free from weeds. Barley is equally suited for fallow land and for a soil that is not intended to be fallow, and three-month wheat, for a soil upon which a crop of ordinary wheat would never ripen, but strong enough to bear." The following, too, is sound advice: Those plants should be sown in a thin soil which do not stand in need of much nutriment, the cytisus, for instance, and such of the leguminous plants, with the exception of the chick-pea, as are taken up by the roots and not cut. From this mode of gathering them — "legere" — the legumina derive their name. Where it is a rich earth, those plants should be grown which require a greater proportion of nutriment, coleworts for instance, wheat, winter-wheat, and flax. The result, then, will be, that a light soil will be given to barley — the root of that grain standing in need of less nutriment — while a more dense, though easily-worked soil, will be assigned [to heavier crops].
results.translation:
laurel. Over-luxuriance in grain [corn] is checked by the grazing of cattle, but only while the crop is still in the blade [i.e., before the ear has formed]; if grazed upon as many times as one likes at that stage, no harm to the ear will result. If the ear itself is once cut off, however, the grain will, as is well known, assume a larger form, but will be hollow within and worthless, and if sown will come to nothing. At Babylon, however, they cut the blade twice and then allow the cattle to graze upon it a third time, for otherwise the crop would run entirely to leaf. Even so, the soil is so fertile that it yields fifty-fold, and indeed, with careful management, as much as a hundred-fold. Nor is its cultivation attended with any great difficulty, the sole aim being to keep the ground under water as long as possible, so that the extreme richness and exuberance of the soil may be tempered. The Euphrates and the Tigris, however, do not deposit a silt [slime] in the way that the Nile does in Egypt, nor does the soil there produce vegetation of its own accord; yet so great is the fertility that, even though the seed is merely trodden in with the foot, a crop springs up of itself the following year. So great a difference in soils calls to my mind that I ought at this point to specify those soils which are best suited to the various kinds of grain.

CHAPTER 46. THE CROPS THAT SHOULD BE SOWN IN THE DIFFERENT SOILS.

The following is the opinion expressed by Cato on the subject: "In a dense and fertile soil, wheat should be sown; but if the locality is subject to fogs, then rape, radishes, millet, and panic-grass. Where the land is cold and moist, sowing should be begun earlier; but where it is hot, at a later period. In a red, black, or gravelly soil, provided it is not waterlogged, lupines should be sown; but in chalky, red-clay, or waterlogged soil, spelt [ador]. Where a locality is dry, free from weeds, and not overshadowed, wheat should be sown; and where the soil is strong and heavy, beans. Vetches should be grown in a soil as free from excess water and weeds as possible; wheat and winter wheat are best suited to an open, elevated situation fully exposed to the warmth of the sun. The lentil thrives best in a thin, red earth free from weeds. Barley is equally suited to fallow land and to land not intended to lie fallow; and three-month wheat [trimestre] is suited to ground on which an ordinary crop of wheat would never come to full ripeness, yet which is strong enough to bear a crop." The following, too, is sound advice: those plants should be sown in a thin soil which do not require much nutriment — cytisus, for instance, and those leguminous plants, with the exception of the chick-pea, that are pulled up by the roots rather than cut. It is from this mode of gathering them — legere ["to pick up"] — that the legumina [legumes] derive their name. Where the earth is rich, those plants should be grown that require a greater share of nutriment — coleworts [brassica], for instance, wheat, winter wheat, and flax. The outcome, then, will be that a light soil is assigned to barley — since the root of that grain requires less nutriment — while a denser yet easily worked soil will be assigned [to the more demanding crops].

results.title_inferred: Methods of Ploughing, Harrowing, Hoeing, and Stubbing for Various Grains
results.original_text:
method of ploughing, which the devastations of warfare have suggested in Italy that lies beyond the Padus. The Salassi, when ravaging the territories which lay at the foot of the Alps, made an attempt to lay waste the crops of panic and millet that were just appearing above the ground. Finding, however, that Nature resisted all their endeavours, they passed the plough over the ground, the result of which was that the crops were more abundant than ever; and this it was that first taught us the method of ploughing in, expressed by the word "artrare," otherwise "aratrare," in my opinion the original form. This is done either just as the stem begins to develope itself, or else when it has put forth as many as two or three leaves. Nor must we withhold from the reader a more recent method, which was discovered the year but one before this, in the territory of the Treviri. The crops having been nipped by the extreme severity of the winter, the people sowed the land over again in the month of March, and had a most abundant harvest. We shall now proceed to a description of the peculiar methods employed in cultivating each description of grain.

CHAP. 50. (21.) — THE METHODS OF HARROWING, STUBBING, AND HOEING, EMPLOYED FOR EACH DESCRIPTION OF GRAIN. THE USE OF THE HARROW.

For winter wheat, spelt, wheat, zea, and barley, harrow, hoe and stub upon the days which will be mentioned in the sequel. A single hand per jugerum will be quite enough for any one of these kinds of grain. The operation of hoeing loosens the ground in spring when it has been hardened and saddened by the rigours of the winter, and admits the early sun to the interior. In hoeing, every care must be taken not to go beneath the roots of the corn; in the case of wheat, zea, and barley, it is best to give a couple of hoeings. Stubbing, when the crop is just beginning to joint, cleanses it of all noxious weeds, disengages the roots of the corn, and liberates the growing blade from the clods. Among the leguminous plants, the chick-pea requires the same treatment that spelt does. The bean requires no stubbing, being quite able of itself to overpower all weeds; the lupine, too, is harrowed only. Millet and panic are both harrowed and hoed; but this operation is never repeated, and they do not require stubbing. Fenugreek and the kidney-bean require harrowing only. There are some kinds of ground, the extreme fertility of which obliges the grower to comb down the crops while in the blade — this is done with a sort of harrow armed with pointed iron teeth — and even then he is obliged to depasture cattle upon them. When, however, the blade has been thus eaten down, it stands in need of hoeing to restore it to its former vigour. But in Bactria, and at Cyrenae in Africa, all this trouble has been rendered quite unnecessary by the indulgent benignity of the climate, and after the seed is in, the owner has no occasion to return to the field till the time has come for getting in the harvest. In those parts the natural dryness of the soil prevents noxious weeds from springing up, and, aided by the night dews alone, the soil supplies its nutriment to the grain.
results.translation:
This passage, from Pliny the Elder's Natural History (Book XVIII), describes agricultural methods observed in Italy beyond the Po [Padus] River and in other regions. The Salassi people, while raiding territories at the foot of the Alps, attempted to destroy emerging crops of panic-grass and millet. When they found that the plants resisted destruction, they ploughed the ground over them — only to find that the crops came up more abundantly than before. This episode, Pliny asserts, first taught the practice of 'ploughing in' [artrare or aratrare, which Pliny considers the original Latin form], performed either when the stem first begins to develop or when it has put out two or three leaves. He also records a more recent method discovered two years before the time of writing in the territory of the Treviri: after crops were killed by a severe winter, the farmers resowed the land in March and obtained an excellent harvest.

Chapter 50 then turns to the specific methods of harrowing, stubbing, and hoeing appropriate to each type of grain. For winter wheat, spelt, common wheat, zea [emmer or a related grain], and barley, the recommended operations are harrowing, hoeing, and stubbing, to be performed on auspicious days noted elsewhere. One labourer per jugerum [a Roman unit of area, roughly 0.25 hectares] suffices for any of these crops. Hoeing in spring loosens ground compacted and depressed by winter cold, and admits the early warmth of the sun to the soil's interior. Care must be taken during hoeing not to disturb the roots of the corn; for wheat, zea, and barley, two hoeings are recommended. Stubbing, carried out when the crop is just beginning to joint [form nodes], clears noxious weeds, frees the roots, and liberates the growing blade from clods of earth. Among legumes, the chick-pea requires the same treatment as spelt. The bean needs no stubbing, being vigorous enough to suppress weeds on its own; lupine likewise requires only harrowing. Millet and panic-grass are both harrowed and hoed, but only once each, and require no stubbing. Fenugreek and the kidney-bean need harrowing only. On ground of exceptional fertility, it is necessary to comb down the growing blades with an iron-toothed harrow, and even to graze cattle upon the field; after such treatment, hoeing is needed to restore the crop's vigour. In Bactria and Cyrene in Africa, the benign climate makes all these labours unnecessary: once the seed is sown, the farmer need not return until harvest. The natural dryness of the soil in those regions prevents weeds from growing, and night dews alone provide sufficient moisture for the grain.

results.translator_notes: This segment is already in English (it is a 19th-century translation of Pliny's Latin Natural History, Book XVIII, Chapters 49–50), so the 'translation' provided above is a clarified and annotated rendering rather than a translation from another language. The term 'zea' (footnote 68 in original) is a technically contested identification; Fee and other commentators differ on whether it denotes emmer, einkorn, or another ancient grain. The editorial footnotes in the original (64–71) have been retained in context. 'Jugerum' is left with a parenthetical gloss. The year 'A.U.C. 830' corresponds to approximately 77 CE, consistent with Pliny's composition of the Natural History. The word 'artrare'/'aratrare' may reflect a genuine Latin hapax or a scribal/OCR variant; it is preserved and annotated.

results.title_inferred: On Seed Quality, Sowing Practices, and Quantities of Grain Required per Jugerum
results.original_text:
white within is of inferior quality. It is a well-known fact that some lands require more seed than others, from which circumstance first arose a superstition that exists among the peasantry; it is their belief that when the ground demands the seed with greater avidity than usual, it is famished, and devours the grain. It is consistent with reason to put in the seed where the soil is humid sooner than elsewhere, to prevent the grain from rotting in the rain: on dry spots it should be sown later, and just before the fall of a shower, so that it may not have to lie long without germinating and so come to nothing. When the seed is put in early it should be sown thick, as it is a considerable time before it germinates; but when it is put in later, it should be sown thinly, to prevent it from being suffocated. There is a certain degree of skill, too, required in scattering the seed evenly; to ensure this, the hand must keep time with the step, moving always with the right foot. There are certain persons, also, who have a secret method of their own, having been born with a happy hand which imparts fruitfulness to the grain. Care should be taken not to sow seed in a warm locality which has been grown in a cold one, nor should the produce of an early soil be sown in a late one. Those who give advice to the contrary have quite misapplied their pains.

CHAP. 55. WHAT QUANTITY OF EACH KIND OF GRAIN IS REQUISITE FOR SOWING A JUGERUM.

In a soil of middling quality, the proper proportion of seed is five modii of wheat or winter wheat to the jugerum, ten of spelt or of seed-wheat — that being the name which we have mentioned as being given to one kind of wheat — six of barley, one-fifth more of beans than of wheat, twelve of vetches, three of chick-pease, chicheling vetches, and pease, ten of lupines, three of lentils — (these last, however, it is said, must be sown with dry manure) — six of fitches, six of fenugreek, four of kidney-beans, twenty of hay grass, and four sextarii of millet and panic. Where the soil is rich, the proportion must be greater, where it is thin, less. There is another distinction, too, to be made; where the soil is dense, cretaceous, or moist, there should be six modii of wheat or winter-wheat to the jugerum, but where the land is loose, dry, and prolific, four will be enough. A meagre soil, too, if the crop is not very thinly sown, will produce a diminutive, empty ear. Rich lands give a number of stalks to each grain, and yield a thick crop from only a light sowing. The result, then, is, that from four to six modii must be sown, according to the nature of the soil.
results.translation:
Grain that is white within is of inferior quality. It is a well-known fact that some lands require more seed than others, from which circumstance first arose a superstition common among the peasantry: they believe that when the ground demands seed with greater avidity than usual, it is famished and is devouring the grain. It is reasonable to sow seed in humid soil sooner than elsewhere, to prevent the grain from rotting in the rain; on dry spots it should be sown later, and just before a shower falls, so that it may not lie long without germinating and thus come to nothing. When seed is put in early it should be sown thickly, as it takes a considerable time to germinate; but when put in later, it should be sown thinly, lest it be suffocated. A certain degree of skill is also required in scattering seed evenly; to ensure this, the hand must keep time with the step, always moving with the right foot. There are certain persons, moreover, who employ a secret method of their own, having been born with a 'happy hand' [sors genialis atque fecunda] that imparts fruitfulness to the grain. Care should be taken not to sow, in a warm locality, seed that was grown in a cold one; nor should the produce of an early-ripening soil be sown in a late-ripening one. Those who advise to the contrary have quite misapplied their efforts.

CHAPTER 55. WHAT QUANTITY OF EACH KIND OF GRAIN IS REQUISITE FOR SOWING A JUGERUM.

In soil of middling quality, the proper proportion of seed is five modii [a Roman dry measure, roughly 8.7 litres each] of wheat or winter wheat per jugerum [a Roman unit of area, roughly 0.25 hectares], ten modii of spelt or seed-wheat — that being the name we have already mentioned as applied to one variety of wheat — six of barley, one-fifth more of beans than of wheat, twelve of vetches, three of chick-peas, chicheling vetches [grass peas], and peas, ten of lupines, three of lentils (these last, it is said, must be sown together with dry manure), six of fitches [bitter vetch], six of fenugreek, four of kidney-beans, twenty of hay grass, and four sextarii [a smaller Roman liquid/dry measure] of millet and panic grass. Where the soil is rich the proportion must be greater; where it is thin, less. A further distinction must be made: where the soil is dense, chalky [cretaceous], or moist, six modii of wheat or winter wheat should be used per jugerum; but where the land is loose, dry, and naturally productive, four will suffice. A meagre soil, if not sown very thinly, will produce a diminutive and empty ear. Rich lands yield numerous stalks per grain and produce a thick crop from only a light sowing. The conclusion, therefore, is that between four and six modii must be sown, according to the nature of the soil.

entities_mentioned.name: wheat (triticum / winter wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny distinguishes ordinary wheat and 'winter wheat' [siligo]; both likely belong to the genus Triticum.

entities_mentioned.name: spelt

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (Natural History), Book XVIII, dealing with agriculture. The text is already in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock & Riley) rather than Latin; the task here has been to clean, clarify, and annotate it rather than translate from Latin. Key technical terms preserved: jugerum (Roman unit of area, c. 0.25 ha), modii (plural of modius, Roman dry measure, c. 8.7 litres), sextarii (smaller measure). Footnotes present in the OCR source (numbered 86–96) have been silently integrated or noted. The phrase 'sors genialis atque fecunda est' (a fortunate and fruitful birth-lot) is preserved in brackets as it underlies the concept of the 'happy hand.' The medical/agricultural relevance is primarily agronomy; no direct therapeutic preparations are described in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On Sowing Times, the Vergiliae, Leaf-Fall as Agricultural Sign, and Sowing of Legumes and Poppy
results.original_text:
given us the signal for it, a term by which Cicero understands the rains that prevail in November. The true method to be adopted, however, is not to sow until the leaves begin to fall. Some persons are of opinion that this takes place at the setting of the Vergiliae, or the third day before the ides of November, as already stated, and they carefully observe it, for it is a constellation very easily remarked in the heavens, and warns us to resume our winter clothes. Hence it is, that immediately on its setting, the approach of winter is expected, and care is taken by those who are on their guard against the exorbitant charges of the shop-keepers, to provide themselves with an appropriate dress. If the Vergiliae set with cloudy weather, it forebodes a rainy winter, and the prices of cloaks immediately rise; but if, on the other hand, the weather is clear at that period, a sharp winter is to be expected, and then the price of garments of other descriptions is sure to go up. But as to the husbandman, unacquainted as he is with the phenomena of the heavens, his brambles are to him in place of constellations, and if he looks at the ground he sees it covered with their leaves. This fall of the leaves, earlier in one place and later in another, is a sure criterion of the temperature of the weather; for there is a great affinity between the effects produced by the weather in this respect, and the nature of the soil and climate. There is this peculiar advantage, too, in the careful observation of these effects, that they are sure to be perceptible throughout the whole earth, while at the same time they have certain features which are peculiar to each individual locality. A person may perhaps be surprised at this, who does not bear in mind that the herb pennyroyal, which is hung up in our larders, always blossoms on the day of the winter solstice; so firmly resolved is Nature that nothing shall remain concealed from us, and in that spirit has given us the fall of the leaf as the signal for sowing. Such is the true method of interpreting all these phenomena, granted to us by Nature as a manifestation of her will. It is in this way that she warns us to prepare the ground, makes us a promise of a manure, as it were, in the fall of the leaves, announces to us that the earth and the productions thereof are thus protected by her against the cold, and warns us to hasten the operations of agriculture.

CHAP. 61. WHEN TO SOW THE LEGUMINOUS PLANTS AND THE POPPY.

results.translation:
This passage gives us the signal for sowing, a term by which Cicero understands the rains that prevail in November. The true method to be adopted, however, is not to sow until the leaves begin to fall. Some persons are of opinion that this takes place at the setting of the Vergiliae [the Pleiades], or the third day before the ides of November, as already stated, and they carefully observe it, for it is a constellation very easily remarked in the heavens, and warns us to put on our winter clothing. Hence it is that, immediately upon its setting, the approach of winter is expected, and those who are on their guard against the exorbitant charges of shop-keepers take care to provide themselves with an appropriate dress. If the Vergiliae set amid cloudy weather, it forebodes a rainy winter, and the prices of cloaks [lacernae] immediately rise; but if, on the other hand, the weather is clear at that time, a sharp winter is to be expected, and then the price of garments of other kinds is sure to go up. As for the husbandman, unacquainted as he is with the phenomena of the heavens, his brambles serve him in place of constellations, and when he looks at the ground he sees it covered with their fallen leaves. This fall of the leaves, occurring earlier in one place and later in another, is a sure indicator of the temperature of the weather; for there is a great affinity between the effects produced by the weather in this respect and the nature of the soil and climate. There is also this peculiar advantage in the careful observation of these effects, that they are sure to be perceptible throughout the whole earth, while at the same time they have certain features peculiar to each individual locality. A person may perhaps be surprised at this, who does not bear in mind that the herb pennyroyal [puleium], which is hung up in our larders, always blossoms on the day of the winter solstice; so firmly resolved is Nature that nothing shall remain hidden from us, and in that spirit she has given us the fall of the leaf as the signal for sowing. Such is the true method of interpreting all these phenomena, granted to us by Nature as a manifestation of her will. It is in this way that she warns us to prepare the ground, offers us a kind of promise of manure in the fall of the leaves, announces to us that the earth and its productions are thus protected against the cold, and urges us to hasten the operations of agriculture.

Chapter 61. When to Sow the Leguminous Plants and the Poppy.

Varro has given no sign other than this for our guidance in sowing the bean. Some persons are of opinion that it should be sown at full moon, the lentil between the twenty-fifth and thirtieth day of the moon, and [the text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Vergiliae
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny uses the setting of the Vergiliae (circa November 11, three days before the ides of November) as an agricultural and meteorological sign marking the onset of winter and the appropriate time to sow.

entities_mentioned.name: Pennyroyal (puleium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as being hung up in larders and noted to blossom on the day of the winter solstice. This is cited as an example of Nature's reliable signs. The Latin 'puleium' is glossed in the translator's footnote as referenced in Book II, c. 41.

entities_mentioned.name: Bean
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba (broad bean) or Phaseolus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Varro cited as authority for sowing time; some recommend sowing at full moon. Likely Vicia faba given the Roman agricultural context.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentil
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended to be sown between the twenty-fifth and thirtieth day of the moon.

entities_mentioned.name: Brambles
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by husbandmen as a natural phenological indicator of the season in lieu of astronomical observation.

entities_mentioned.name: Cloak (lacerna)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Roman outer garment; mentioned in the context of rising prices correlated with weather forecasting from the setting of the Vergiliae.

results.title_inferred: Agricultural Calendar: Winter Sowing, Monthly Farm Tasks, and Winter Solstice Practices
results.original_text:
of Nature, with reference to which we will give the words employed by Cicero himself: "The lentisk, ever green and ever bent Beneath its fruits, affords a threefold crop: Thrice teeming, thrice it warns us when to plough." One of the periods here alluded to, is the same that is now under consideration, being the appropriate time also for sowing flax and the poppy. With reference to this last, Cato gives the following advice: "Burn, upon land where corn has been grown, the twigs and branches which are of no use to you, and when that is done, sow the poppy there." The wild poppy, which is of an utility that is quite marvellous, is boiled in honey as a remedy for diseases in the throat, while the cultivated kind is a powerful narcotic. Thus much in reference to winter sowing.

CHAP. 62. — WORK TO BE DONE IN THE COUNTRY IN EACH MONTH RESPECTIVELY.

And now, in order to complete what we may call in some measure an abridgment of the operations of agriculture, it is as well to add that it will be a good plan at the same period to manure the roots of trees, and to mould up the vines — a single hand being sufficient for one jugerum. Where, too, the nature of the locality will allow it, the vines, and the trees upon which they are trained, should be lopped, and the soil turned up with the mattock for seed plots; trenches, too, should be opened out, and the water drained from off the fields, and the presses should be well washed and put away. Never put eggs beneath the hen between the calends of November and the winter solstice: during all the summer and up to the calends of November, you may put thirteen under the hen; but the number must be smaller in winter, not less than nine, however. Democritus is of opinion, that the winter will turn out of the same character as the weather on the day of the winter solstice and the three succeeding days; the same too with the summer and the weather at the summer solstice. About the winter solstice, for about twice seven days mostly, while the halcyon is sitting, the winds are lulled, and the weather serene; but in this case, as in all others, the influence of the stars must only be judged of by the result, and we must not expect the changes of the weather, as if out upon their recognizances, to make their appearance exactly on certain predetermined days.

CHAP. 63. WORK TO BE DONE AT THE WINTER SOLSTICE.

Be careful never to touch the vine at the winter solstice. Hyginus recommends us to strain and even rack-off wine at the seventh day after the winter solstice, provided the moon is seven days old. About this period, also, the cherry-tree, he says, should be planted. Acorns, too, should now be put in soak for the oxen, a modius for each pair. If given in larger quantities, this food will prove injurious to their health; and whenever it is given, if they are fed with it for less than thirty days in succession, an attack of scab in the spring, it is said, will be sure to make you repent. This, too, is the period that we have already assigned for cutting timber — other kinds of work, again, may be found for the hours of the
results.translation:
Concerning Nature, for which we shall cite the very words of Cicero himself: "The lentisk, ever green and ever bowed beneath its fruit, yields a threefold harvest: thrice ripening, thrice it signals when to plough." One of the periods referred to here is the same as that now under discussion, being likewise the proper time for sowing flax and the poppy. Regarding the latter, Cato offers the following advice: "On land where grain has been grown, burn the twigs and branches that are of no use to you, and when that is done, sow the poppy there." The wild poppy, which is of truly marvellous usefulness, is boiled in honey as a remedy for diseases of the throat, while the cultivated variety is a powerful narcotic. So much concerning winter sowing.

CHAPTER 62. — WORK TO BE DONE IN THE COUNTRYSIDE MONTH BY MONTH.

Now, in order to complete what may be called a summary of agricultural operations, it should be added that it is good practice at this same period to manure the roots of trees and to earth up [mound the soil around] the vines — a single worker being sufficient for one jugerum [a Roman unit of land area, approximately 0.25 hectares]. Where the nature of the locality permits, the vines and the trees upon which they are trained should be pruned, and the soil turned up with the mattock for seed-plots; trenches should also be dug out, water drained from the fields, and the presses thoroughly washed and stored away. Never place eggs under a hen between the Calends of November [1 November] and the winter solstice: throughout the summer and up until the Calends of November you may place thirteen eggs under a hen, but the number must be smaller in winter — no fewer than nine, however. Democritus holds the opinion that the character of the winter will correspond to the weather on the day of the winter solstice and the three days following; the same applies to the summer and the weather at the summer solstice. Around the winter solstice, for approximately fourteen days in most years, while the halcyon [kingfisher] is brooding, the winds fall quiet and the weather is calm [the so-called "halcyon days"]; but in this as in all other matters, the influence of the stars must be judged only by actual results, and we must not expect changes in the weather to appear as though bound by obligation on certain fixed and predetermined days.

CHAPTER 63. WORK TO BE DONE AT THE WINTER SOLSTICE.

Take care never to touch the vine at the winter solstice. Hyginus advises us to strain and even rack off wine on the seventh day after the winter solstice, provided the moon is seven days old. Around this period also, he says, the cherry-tree should be planted. Acorns, too, should now be soaked for the oxen — one modius [a Roman dry measure, approximately 8.7 litres] per pair. If given in larger quantities, this feed will prove harmful to their health; and whenever it is fed to them, if they receive it for fewer than thirty consecutive days, an outbreak of scab [mange/skin disease] in the spring will, it is said, inevitably follow to your regret. This is likewise the period we have already assigned for cutting timber — other kinds of work may be found for the remaining hours of the [text breaks off]

results.title_inferred: Agricultural Calendar: Tasks Following the First West Winds of Spring
results.original_text: occupations, in reference to which, however, he is extremely liable to be deceived. He is summoned to the commencement of these labours, not upon the day on which the west winds ought to begin, but upon the day on which they really do begin, to blow. This moment then must be looked for with the most careful attention, as it is a signal which the Deity has vouchsafed us in this month, attended with no doubts or equivocations, if only looked for with scrupulous care. We have already stated in the Second Book the quarter in which this wind blows, and the exact point from which it comes, and before long we shall have occasion to speak of it again still more in detail. In the mean time, however, setting out from the day, whatever it may happen to be, on which the west winds begin to prevail (for it is not always on the seventh before the ides of February that they do begin), whether, in fact, they begin to blow before the usual time, as is the case with an early spring, or whether after, which generally happens when the winter is prolonged — there are subjects innumerable to engage the attention of the agriculturist, and those, of course, should be the first attended to, which will admit of no delay. Three month wheat must now be sown, the vine pruned in the way we have already described, the olive carefully attended to, fruit-trees put in and grafted, vineyards cleaned and hoed, seedlings laid out, and replaced in the nursery by others, the reed, the willow, and the broom planted and lopped, and the elm, the poplar, and the plane planted in manner already mentioned. At this period, also, the crops of corn ought to be weeded, and the winter kinds, spelt more particularly, well hoed. In doing this, there is a certain rule to be observed, the proper moment being when four blades have made their appearance, and with the bean this should never be done until three leaves have appeared above ground; even then, however, it is a better plan to clean them only with a slight hoeing, in preference to digging up the ground — but in no case should they ever be touched the first fifteen days of their blossom. Barley must never be hoed except when it is quite dry: take care, too, to have all the pruning done by the vernal equinox. Four men will be sufficient for pruning a jugerum of vineyard, and each hand will be able to train fifteen vines to their trees. At this period, too, attention should be paid to the gardens and rose-beds, subjects which will be separately treated of in succeeding Books; due care should be given to ornamental gardening as well. It is now, too, the very best time for making ditches. The ground should now be opened for future purposes, as we find recommended by Virgil in particular, in order that the sun may thoroughly warm the clods. It is a piece of even more sound advice, which recommends us to plough no lands in the middle of spring but those of middling quality.
results.translation: occupations, in reference to which, however, he is extremely liable to be deceived. He is summoned to the commencement of these labours, not upon the day on which the west winds ought to begin, but upon the day on which they really do begin to blow. This moment must therefore be watched for with the most careful attention, as it is a signal which the Deity has granted us in this month, free from doubt or ambiguity, if only sought with scrupulous care. We have already stated in the Second Book the quarter from which this wind blows and the exact point of its origin, and before long we shall have occasion to speak of it again in still greater detail. In the meantime, however, reckoning from whatever day it may happen to be on which the west winds begin to prevail — for it is not always on the seventh day before the Ides of February [i.e., the 7th of February] that they begin — whether in fact they begin to blow earlier than usual, as occurs in an early spring, or later, as generally happens when winter is prolonged: there are innumerable tasks to engage the attention of the agriculturist, and those which admit of no delay should naturally be attended to first. Three-month wheat [a fast-ripening variety] must now be sown; the vine pruned in the manner already described; the olive carefully tended; fruit-trees planted and grafted; vineyards cleaned and hoed; seedlings set out and replaced in the nursery by new ones; the reed, the willow, and the broom planted and lopped; and the elm, the poplar, and the plane planted in the manner already mentioned. At this period also the standing crops of corn ought to be weeded, and the winter varieties — spelt in particular — thoroughly hoed. In this operation a certain rule must be observed: the proper moment is when four blades have appeared, and in the case of the bean this should never be done until three leaves have come above ground. Even then, however, it is better practice to clean them with only a light hoeing rather than to dig up the ground — but on no account should they ever be touched during the first fifteen days of their flowering. Barley must never be hoed except when perfectly dry. Care should also be taken to have all pruning completed by the vernal equinox. Four men will suffice to prune a jugerum [a Roman unit of land area, roughly 0.25 hectares] of vineyard, and each worker will be able to train fifteen vines to their supporting trees. Attention should be paid at this time as well to the gardens and rose-beds, subjects which will be treated separately in later Books; due care should likewise be given to ornamental gardening. This is also the very best season for digging ditches. The ground should now be broken up for future use, as Virgil in particular recommends, so that the sun may thoroughly warm the clods. There is yet sounder advice, which recommends that in the middle of spring only lands of middling quality should be ploughed.

results.title_inferred: Agricultural Calendar: Spring Ploughing Signs, Vernal Equinox, and Stellar Observations
results.original_text:
a day, probably. 76 Georg. i. 63. 86 FLINT'S NATURAL HISTORY. [Book XVIII.] other hand, it is a thin, meagre land, as soon as the heat comes on, it will be dried up, and so lose all the moisture which should be reserved to nourish the seed when sown. It is a much better plan, beyond a doubt, to plough such soils as these in autumn. Cato lays down the following rules for the operations of spring. "Ditches," he says, "should be dug in the seed-plots, vines should be grafted, and the elm, the fig, the olive, and other fruit-trees planted in dense and humid soils. Such meadows as are not irrigated, must be manured in a dry moon, protected from the western blasts, and carefully cleaned; noxious weeds must be rooted up, fig-trees cleared, new seed-plots made, and the old ones dressed: all this should be done before you begin to hoe the vineyard. When the pear is in blossom, too, you should begin to plough, where it is a meagre, gravelly soil. When you have done all this, you may plough the more heavy, watery soils, doing this the last of all." The proper time for ploughing, then, is denoted by these two signs, the earliest fruit of the lentisk making its appearance, and the blossoming of the pear. There is a third sign, however, as well, the flowering of the squill among the bulbous plants, and of the narcissus among the garland plants. For both the squill and the narcissus, as well as the lentisk, flower three times, denoting by their first flowering the first period for ploughing, by the second flowering the second, and by the third flowering the last; in this way it is that one thing affords hints for another. There is one precaution, too, that is by no means the least important among them all, not to let ivy touch the bean while in blossom; for at this period the ivy is noxious to it, and most baneful in its effects. Some plants, again, afford certain signs which bear reference more particularly to themselves, the fig for instance; when a few leaves only are found shooting from the summit, like a cup in shape, then it is more particularly that the fig-tree should be planted.

CHAP. 66. WORK TO BE DONE AFTER THE VERNAL EQUINOX.

The vernal equinox appears to end on the eighth day before the calends of April. Between the equinox and the morning rising of the Vergiliae, the calends of April announce, according to Caesar, [stormy weather]. Upon the third before the nones of April, the Vergiliae set in the evening in Attica, and the day after in Boeotia, but according to Caesar and the Chaldaeans, upon the nones. In Egypt, at this time, Orion and his Sword begin to set. According to Caesar, the setting of Libra on the sixth before the ides of April announces rain. On the fourteenth before the calends of May, the Suculae set to the people of Egypt in the evening, a stormy constellation, and significant of tempests both by land and sea. This constellation sets on the sixteenth in Attica, and on the fifteenth, according to Caesar, announcing four days of bad weather in succession: in Assyria it sets upon the twelfth before the calends of May. This constellation has ordinarily the name of Parilicium, from the circumstance that the eleventh before [the calends of May coincides with the Parilia festival].
results.translation:
On the other hand, if it is thin, meagre land, as soon as the heat comes on it will dry out and lose all the moisture that should be preserved to nourish the seed once sown. It is beyond doubt a much better plan to plough such soils in autumn. Cato [De Re Rustica, c. 40] lays down the following rules for the operations of spring: "Ditches," he says, "should be dug in the seed-plots; vines should be grafted; and the elm, the fig, the olive, and other fruit-trees should be planted in dense and humid soils. Meadows that are not irrigated must be manured during a dry moon, protected from the western blasts, and carefully cleaned; noxious weeds must be rooted up, fig-trees cleared, new seed-plots made, and the old ones dressed — all this should be done before you begin to hoe the vineyard. When the pear is in blossom, you should begin to plough where the soil is meagre and gravelly. When all this is done, you may plough the heavier, waterlogged soils, doing this last of all." The proper time for ploughing, then, is indicated by two signs: the earliest fruit of the lentisk [mastich, Pistacia lentiscus] making its appearance, and the blossoming of the pear. There is a third sign as well: the flowering of the squill among bulbous plants, and of the narcissus among garland plants. For both the squill and the narcissus, as well as the lentisk, flower three times — indicating by their first flowering the first period for ploughing, by the second flowering the second period, and by the third flowering the last; in this way one thing provides guidance for another. There is also a precaution that is by no means the least important: ivy must not be allowed to touch the bean while it is in blossom, for at this period the ivy is harmful to it and most baneful in its effects. Some plants, again, provide signs that refer more particularly to themselves — the fig, for instance: when only a few leaves are found shooting from the summit, shaped like a cup, that is precisely the time to plant the fig-tree.

CHAPTER 66. WORK TO BE DONE AFTER THE VERNAL EQUINOX.

The vernal equinox appears to end on the eighth day before the Calends of April [25 March]. Between the equinox and the morning rising of the Vergiliae [Pleiades], the Calends of April announce, according to Caesar, [stormy weather]. On the third day before the Nones of April, the Vergiliae set in the evening in Attica, and the following day in Boeotia; but according to Caesar and the Chaldaeans, they set on the Nones [5 April]. In Egypt at this time, Orion and his Sword begin to set. According to Caesar, the setting of Libra on the sixth day before the Ides of April announces rain. On the fourteenth day before the Calends of May, the Suculae [Hyades] set in the evening for the people of Egypt — a stormy constellation, portending tempests both by land and sea. This constellation sets on the sixteenth in Attica, and on the fifteenth according to Caesar, announcing four successive days of bad weather; in Assyria it sets on the twelfth before the Calends of May. This constellation is commonly called the Parilicium, from the fact that the eleventh day before [the Calends of May coincides with the Parilia, a Roman festival].

entities_mentioned.name: lentisk (lentiscus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called mastich; used here as a phenological indicator for ploughing seasons. Its fruit and flowering cycles mark three successive ploughing periods.

entities_mentioned.name: squill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (sea squill, formerly Urginea maritima / Scilla maritima)
entities_mentioned.notes: A bulbous plant whose three flowering periods serve as a calendar indicator for ploughing.

entities_mentioned.name: narcissus
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a 'garland plant' that flowers three times, serving alongside the squill as a ploughing calendar indicator.

entities_mentioned.name: fig (ficus)

entities_mentioned.name: ivy
entities_mentioned.notes: Stated to be harmful to the bean while the bean is in blossom. Pliny acknowledges uncertainty about the origin of this belief (footnote 82).

entities_mentioned.name: bean
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba (broad bean) or Phaseolus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The specific legume is not identified; most likely Vicia faba in the Roman agricultural context.

entities_mentioned.name: pear
entities_mentioned.notes: Blossoming of the pear is one of two primary phenological signs for the start of spring ploughing.

entities_mentioned.name: elm
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among trees to be planted in dense and humid soils in spring, per Cato.

entities_mentioned.name: olive
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed for spring planting in dense and humid soils, per Cato.

entities_mentioned.name: Vergiliae (Pleiades)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an astronomical calendar marker for weather forecasting and agricultural timing.

entities_mentioned.name: Suculae (Hyades)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called Parilicium. Described as a stormy constellation whose setting portends tempests by land and sea. Setting dates vary by region: Egypt (14 before Calends of May), Attica (16th), Caesar (15th, announcing 4 days of bad weather), Assyria (12th).

entities_mentioned.name: Orion and his Sword
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orion constellation and the Orion's Sword asterism

entities_mentioned.name: Libra
entities_mentioned.notes: According to Caesar, its setting on the sixth before the Ides of April announces rain.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny's Natural History, Book XVIII, as rendered in an early English translation (likely Bostock and Riley, or an earlier version referenced as 'Flint's Natural History'). The text incorporates Cato's De Re Rustica (c. 40) on spring agricultural operations. Footnote references in the original (numerals 76–92) have been retained in context where they appear in the cleaned source text. The annotation that 'it is not known whence he derived this unfounded notion' (footnote 82) refers to the claim about ivy being harmful to beans in blossom. The vernal equinox is equated with 25 March (footnote 83), consistent with the Roman Julian calendar. The name 'Parilicium' for the Hyades (Suculae) derives from the Parilia festival (21 April), though the text appears to reference the eleventh before the Calends of May — the connection is noted as incomplete due to apparent truncation at the end of the segment. The term 'dry moon' [luna sicca] in Cato's instructions refers to a waning or dry-weather moon phase considered auspicious for manuring meadows.

results.title_inferred: Pliny's Natural History: Agricultural Calendar, Stars, and Seasonal Signs (Book XVIII)
results.original_text: Behold now! I scatter stars amid the grass for your service, and I reveal them to you in the evening, as you return from your work; and that you may not disregard them, I call your attention to this marvel. Do you not see how the wings of this insect cover a body bright and shining like fire, and how that body gives out light in the hours of the night even? I have given you plants to point out to you the hours, and, that you may not have to turn your eyes from the earth, even to view the sun, the heliotropium and the lupine have been made by me to move with his movements. Why then still look upwards, and scan the face of heaven? Behold, here before your very feet are your Vergiliae; upon a certain day do they make their appearance, and for a certain time do they stay. Equally certain, too, it is that of that constellation they are the offspring. Whoever, then, shall put in his summer seeds before they have made their appearance, will infallibly find himself in the wrong. It is in this interval, too, that the little bee comes forth, and announces that the bean is about to blossom; for it is the bean in flower that summons it forth. We will here give another sign, which tells us when the cold is gone; as soon as ever you see the mulberry in bud, you have no occasion to fear any injury from the rigour of the weather. It is the time, now, to put in cuttings of the olive, to clear away between the olive-trees, and, in the earlier days of the equinox, to irrigate the meadows. As soon, however, as the grass puts forth a stem, you must shut off the water from the fields. You must now lop the leafy branches of the vine, it being the rule that this should be done as soon as the branches have attained four fingers in length; one labourer will be sufficient for a jugerum. The crops of corn, too, should be hoed over again, an operation which lasts twenty days. It is generally thought, however, that it is injurious to both vine and corn to begin hoeing directly after the equinox. This is the proper time, too, for washing sheep. After the rising of the Vergiliae the more remarkable signs are, according to Caesar, the morning rising of Arcturus, which takes place on the following day; and the rising of the Lyre on the third before the ides of May. The She-goat sets in the evening of the twelfth before the calends of June, and in Attica the Dog. On the eleventh before the calends of June, according to Caesar, Orion's Sword begins to appear; and, according to the same writer, on the fourth before the nones of June the Eagle rises in the evening, and in Assyria as well. On the seventh before the ides of June Arcturus sets in the morning to the people of Italy, and on the fourth before the ides the Dolphin rises in the evening. On the seventeenth before the calends of July Orion's Sword rises in Italy, and, four days later, in Egypt. On the eleventh before the calends of July, according to Caesar's reckoning, Orion's Sword begins to set; and the eighth before the calends of July, the longest day in the year, with the shortest night, brings us to the summer solstice. In this interval of time the vine should be cleared of its superfluous branches, and care taken to give an old vine one turning up at the roots, a young tree two. Sheep, too, are sheared at this period.

In this interval of time the vine should be cleared of its superfluous branches, and care taken to give an old vine one turning up at the roots, a young tree two. Sheep, too, are sheared at this period.
