results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XVIII, concerning the agricultural calendar and parapegma (star calendar). The passage opens with a poetic quotation attributed by Sillig to an unknown poet, presented as the voice of Nature addressing the farmer. 'Vergiliae' is the Latin name for the Pleiades. 'Jugerum' is a Roman unit of land area (approximately 2,523 square metres or ~0.63 acres). The OCR error 'th'jir' has been silently corrected to 'their'. The reference 'Caesar' throughout refers to Julius Caesar's astronomical/calendrical work. Footnote numbers (7*, 8, 9, 10–19) from the original are retained in context but not separately enumerated. The text is already in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock and Riley), so the 'translation' field reproduces a lightly cleaned and annotated version of the source.

results.title_inferred: Laying Out of Lands, Wind Directions, and Agricultural Precautions (Pliny, Natural History Book XVIII)
results.original_text: must then be traced obliquely across them in the form of the letter X, in such a way as to run exactly from right and left of the northern point to left and right of the southern one. All these lines must pass through the centre of the umbilicus, and all must be of corresponding length, and at equal distances. This method should always be adopted in laying out land; or if it should be found necessary to employ it frequently, a plan of it may be made in wood, sticks of equal length being fixed upon the surface of a small tambour, but perfectly round. In the method which I am here explaining, it is necessary to point out one precaution that must always be observed by those who are unacquainted with the subject. The point that must be verified first of all is the south, as that is always the same; but the sun, it must be remembered, rises every day at a point in the heavens different to that of his rising on the day before, so that the east must never be taken as the basis for tracing the lines. Having now ascertained the various points of the heavens, the extremity of the line that is nearest to the north, but lying to the east of it, will indicate the solstitial rising, or, in other words, the rising of the sun on the longest day, as also the point from which the wind Aquilo blows, known to the Greeks by the name of Boreas. You should plant all trees and vines facing this point, but take care never to plough, or sow corn, or plant in seed plots, while this wind is blowing, for it has the effect of drying up and blasting the roots of the trees while being transplanted. Be taught in time — one thing is good for grown trees, another for them while they are but young. Nor have I forgotten the fact, that it is at this point of the heavens that the Greeks place the wind, to which they give the name of Caecias; Aristotle, a man of most extensive learning, who has assigned to Caecias this position, explains that it is in consequence of the convexity of the earth, that Aquilo blows in an opposite direction to the wind called Africus. The agriculturist, however, has nothing to fear from Aquilo, in respect to the operations before mentioned, all the year through; for this wind is softened by the sun in the middle of the summer, and, changing its name, is known by that of Etesias. When you feel the cold, then, be on your guard; for, whatever the noxious effects that are attributed to Aquilo, the more sensibly will they be felt when the wind blows from due north. In Asia, Greece, Spain, the coasts of Italy, Campania, and Apulia, the trees that support the vines, as well as the vines themselves, should have an aspect towards the north-east. If you wish to have male produce, let the flock feed in such a way, that this wind may have the opportunity of fecundating the male, whose office it is to fecundate.
results.translation: These lines must then be traced obliquely across them in the form of the letter X, in such a way as to run exactly from the right and left of the northern point to the left and right of the southern one. All these lines must pass through the centre of the umbilicus [central marker or gnomon], and all must be of corresponding length and at equal distances. This method should always be adopted in laying out land; or, if it should be found necessary to employ it frequently, a plan of it may be made in wood, with sticks of equal length fixed upon the surface of a small tambour [tympanum, i.e., a drum-shaped instrument], which must be perfectly round. In the method I am explaining here, it is necessary to point out one precaution that must always be observed by those unfamiliar with the subject. The point that must be verified first of all is the south, as that is always the same; but the sun, it must be remembered, rises every day at a point in the heavens different from that of its rising on the day before, and therefore the east must never be taken as the basis for tracing the lines. Having now ascertained the various points of the heavens, the extremity of the line that is nearest to the north but lying to the east of it will indicate the solstitial rising — in other words, the rising of the sun on the longest day — as well as the point from which the wind Aquilo blows, known to the Greeks by the name of Boreas. You should plant all trees and vines facing this point, but take care never to plough, or sow grain, or plant in seedbeds while this wind is blowing, for it has the effect of drying up and blasting the roots of trees while they are being transplanted. Learn this lesson in time: one thing is beneficial for mature trees, and quite another for those that are still young. Nor have I forgotten the fact that it is at this point of the heavens that the Greeks place the wind they call Caecias; Aristotle, a man of the most extensive learning, who has assigned to Caecias this position, explains that it is on account of the convexity of the earth that Aquilo blows in a direction opposite to the wind called Africus. The agriculturist, however, has nothing to fear from Aquilo with respect to the operations mentioned above throughout the entire year; for this wind is softened by the sun in the middle of summer, and, changing its name, becomes known as Etesias. When you begin to feel the cold, then be on your guard; for whatever noxious effects are attributed to Aquilo, they will be felt all the more keenly when the wind blows from due north. In Asia, Greece, Spain, the coasts of Italy, Campania, and Apulia, the trees that support the vines, as well as the vines themselves, should face towards the north-east. If you wish to obtain male offspring, let the flock feed in such a manner that this wind may have the opportunity of fecundating the male, whose office it is to fecundate.

entities_mentioned.name: Aquilo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: North-northeast wind (Roman wind rose); roughly equivalent to the modern NNE or NE wind
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by Pliny with the Greek Boreas; also equated in part with the Greek Caecias. Described as drying and blasting to young tree roots during transplanting.

entities_mentioned.name: Boreas
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek equivalent of Aquilo as used by Pliny here.

entities_mentioned.name: Caecias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Northeast wind in Greek wind classification
entities_mentioned.notes: Aristotle assigned Caecias to the northeast quadrant; Pliny notes the apparent discrepancy between Aquilo and Caecias, explaining it by the convexity of the earth.

entities_mentioned.name: Africus
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as blowing in the opposite direction to Aquilo due to the earth's convexity, per Aristotle.

entities_mentioned.name: Etesias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Etesian winds; regular northerly summer winds of the Mediterranean, modern meltemi
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the summer form of Aquilo, softened by the sun.

entities_mentioned.name: umbilicus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Central marker or gnomon used in Roman land surveying (groma)
entities_mentioned.notes: Technical surveying term for the central point through which orientation lines pass.

entities_mentioned.name: tambour (tympanum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drum- or tambourine-shaped wooden orientation device; precursor to a compass rose
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's footnote 77 likens it to a compass describing eight wind points; footnote 78 compares the tympanum to a tambourine or kettle-drum.

entities_mentioned.name: Aristotle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aristotle of Stagira (384–322 BCE), Greek philosopher
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited regarding the position of Caecias and the convexity of the earth explaining opposing wind directions.

entities_mentioned.name: Columella
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella (c. 4–70 CE), Roman agricultural writer
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in editorial footnote 75 regarding whether wind-fecundated offspring are male or female.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a continuous extract from Pliny's Natural History, Book XVIII, concerning the surveying and orientation of agricultural land using a wind-rose instrument, and the practical effects of the north/northeast wind (Aquilo/Boreas/Caecias) on planting, ploughing, and animal husbandry. The OCR introduced several errors corrected silently: 'loft' for 'left', 'arid' for 'and', 'pun' for 'sun', 'Ca3cias' for 'Caecias', 'HISTOllY' for 'HISTORY', 'sensibty' for 'sensibly'. Footnotes (71–80) are editorial annotations from the Bohn/Bostock translation and have been retained in the cleaned text as contextual apparatus. The passage on wind-fecundation of flocks (final lines) relates to a classical belief that the wind's direction during mating determines the sex of offspring; this is an agricultural/zoological claim, not a medicinal one. No specific plant drug or medical preparation is described in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On Varieties of Flax, Cotton, and Other Textile Plants, with Notes on the Preparation of Flax
results.original_text:
that he saw this curiosity very recently, though there was but little then remaining of it, in consequence of the injury it had experienced at the hands of various persons who had tried to verify the fact. Italy, too, holds the flax of the Peligni in high esteem, though it is only employed by fullers; there is no kind known that is whiter than this, or which bears a closer resemblance to wool. That grown by the Cadurci is held in high estimation for making mattresses; which, as well as flock, are an invention for which we are indebted to the Gauls: the ancient usage of Italy is still kept in remembrance in the word "stramentum," the name given by us to beds stuffed with straw. The flax of Egypt, though the least strong of all as a tissue, is that from which the greatest profits are derived. There are four varieties of it, the Tanitic, the Pelusiac, the Butic, and the Tentyritic — so called from the various districts in which they are respectively grown. The upper part of Egypt, in the vicinity of Arabia, produces a shrub, known by some as "gossypium," but by most persons as "xylon;" hence the name of "xylina," given to the tissues that are manufactured from it. The shrub is small, and bears a fruit, similar in appearance to a nut with a beard, and containing in the inside a silky substance, the down of which is spun into threads. There is no tissue known, that is superior to those made from this thread, either for whiteness, softness, or dressing: the most esteemed vestments worn by the priests of Egypt are made of it. There is a fourth kind of tissue, known by the name of "othoninum," which is made from a kind of marsh-reed, the panicule only being employed for the purpose. In Asia, again, there is a thread made from broom, which is employed in the construction of fishing-nets, being found to be remarkably durable; for the purpose of preparing it, the shrub is steeped in water for ten days. The Ethiopians, also, and the people of India, prepare a kind of thread from a fruit which resembles our apple, and the Arabians, as already mentioned, from gourds that grow upon trees.

CHAP. 3. — THE MODE OF PREPARING FLAX.

In our part of the world the ripeness of flax is usually ascertained by two signs, the swelling of the seed, and its assuming a yellowish tint. It is then pulled up by the roots, made up into small sheaves that will just fill the hand, and hung to dry in the sun. It is suspended with the roots upwards the first day, and then for the five following days the heads of the sheaves are placed, reclining one against the other, in such a way that the seed which drops out may fall into the middle. Linseed is employed for various medicinal purposes, and it is used by t[he...]
results.translation:
that he [Pliny's source] had seen this curiosity very recently, though but little of it then remained, owing to damage inflicted by various persons who had attempted to verify the report. Italy, too, holds the flax of the Peligni in high esteem, though it is used only by fullers [cloth-processors]; no variety is known to be whiter than this, nor any that more closely resembles wool. The flax grown by the Cadurci [a Gallic people, modern Cahors region] is highly prized for the making of mattresses [culcitae]; these, along with flock-stuffing [tomenta], are inventions for which we are indebted to the Gauls. The ancient Italian practice is still recalled in the word stramentum — the name we give to beds stuffed with straw, corresponding to what we now call a paillasse. The flax of Egypt, though the weakest of all as a woven material, is that from which the greatest commercial profits are derived. There are four varieties of it: the Tanitic, the Pelusiac, the Butic, and the Tentyritic — named after the various districts in which they are respectively cultivated. The upper part of Egypt, in the vicinity of Arabia, produces a shrub known to some as gossypium, but to most as xylon [cotton]; hence the tissues manufactured from it are called xylina. The shrub is small and bears a fruit resembling a bearded nut, containing within it a silky substance whose down is spun into threads. No woven fabric is known to surpass those made from this thread in whiteness, softness, or finish; the most highly regarded vestments worn by the priests of Egypt are made from it. There is a fourth kind of textile, known as othoninum, made from a type of marsh-reed, only the panicle of which is used for the purpose. In Asia, moreover, a thread is made from broom [Spartium or similar], which is used in the construction of fishing-nets, being found to be remarkably durable; to prepare it, the shrub is steeped in water for ten days. The Ethiopians and the people of India likewise prepare a kind of thread from a fruit resembling our apple, and the Arabians, as already mentioned, from gourds that grow upon trees.

CHAPTER 3. — THE METHOD OF PREPARING FLAX.

In our part of the world, the ripeness of flax is typically determined by two signs: the swelling of the seed, and its turning to a yellowish hue. The plant is then pulled up by the roots, bound into small sheaves just large enough to fill the hand, and hung out to dry in the sun. On the first day it is suspended with the roots upward; for the five following days the heads of the sheaves are leaned against one another so that any seed that falls out may drop into the center. Linseed is employed for various medicinal purposes, and it is used by t[he... text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: Flax of the Peligni
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum (common flax), grown in the Peligni region of central Italy
entities_mentioned.notes: Noted for exceptional whiteness and wool-like quality; used exclusively by fullers.

entities_mentioned.name: Flax of the Cadurci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum, grown in the region of modern Cahors (Quercy), France
entities_mentioned.notes: Prized for making mattresses (culcitae); the Gauls credited with inventing flock-stuffed bedding.

entities_mentioned.name: Egyptian flax — Tanitic, Pelusiac, Butic, Tentyritic varieties
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum, cultivated in the Nile Delta districts of Tanis, Pelusium, Buto, and Tentyra (Dendera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the weakest in tensile strength but most commercially profitable. Reportedly can grow to fifteen feet (Hasselquist).

entities_mentioned.name: Gossypium / Xylon (cotton shrub)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gossypium arboreum L. (tree cotton) or related Gossypium spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny equates gossypium and xylon as synonymous; describes the boll as a bearded nut with silky down. Used for priestly vestments in Egypt. The annotator notes xylon, byssus, and gossypium were used interchangeably for the plant, raw fiber, and woven product.

entities_mentioned.name: Othoninum textile
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly made from Phragmites australis (common reed) or a related marsh grass; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: A fourth class of textile; made from the panicle of a marsh-reed only.

entities_mentioned.name: Broom (thread for fishing-nets)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spartium junceum (Spanish broom) or Cytisus scoparius (common broom)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Asia to make fishing-net thread; prepared by steeping the shrub in water for ten days — an early retting process.

entities_mentioned.name: Linseed / Flax seed (medicinal use)
entities_mentioned.notes: Employed for various medicinal purposes; text is cut off before elaboration. Preparation context: seeds allowed to fall during drying of harvested sheaves.

results.title_inferred: Asbestos, Byssus, and the Dyeing and Use of Linen
results.original_text:
and it only becomes white through the agency of fire. By those who find it, it is sold at prices equal to those given for the finest pearls; by the Greeks it is called "asbestinon," a name which indicates its peculiar properties. Anaxilaus makes a statement to the effect that if a tree is surrounded with linen made of this substance, the noise of the blows given by the axe will be deadened thereby, and that the tree may be cut down without their being heard. For these qualities it is that this linen occupies the very highest rank among all the kinds that are known. The next rank is accorded to the tissue known as "byssus," an article which is held in the very highest estimation by females, and is produced in the vicinity of Elis, in Achaia. I find it stated by some writers that a scruple of this sold formerly at four denarii, the same rate, in fact, as gold. The downy nap of linen, and more particularly that taken from the sails of sea-going ships, is very extensively employed for medicinal purposes, and the ashes of it have the same virtues as spodium. Among the poppies, too, there is a variety which imparts a remarkable degree of whiteness to fabrics made of linen.

CHAP. 5. AT WHAT PERIOD LINEN WAS FIRST DYED.
Attempts, too, have even been made to dye linen, and to make it assume the frivolous colours of our cloths. This was first done in the fleet of Alexander the Great, while sailing upon the river Indus; for, upon one occasion, during a battle that was being fought, his generals and captains distinguished their vessels by the various tints of their sails, and astounded the people on the shores by giving their many colours to the breeze, as it impelled them on. It was with sails of purple, too, that Cleopatra accompanied M. Antonius to the battle of Actium, and it was by their aid that she took to flight: such being the distinguishing mark of the royal ship.

CHAP. 6. AT WHAT PERIOD COLOURED AWNINGS WERE FIRST EMPLOYED IN THE THEATRES.
In more recent times linens alone have been employed for the purpose of affording shade in our theatres; Q. Catulus having been the first who applied them to this use, on the occasion of the dedication by him of the Capitol. At a later period, Lentulus Spinther, it is said, was the first to spread awnings of fine linen over the theatre, at the celebration of the Games in honour of Apollo. After this, Caesar, [text continues; impure oxide reference cut off]
results.translation:
Asbestos [asbestinon] only becomes white through the action of fire. Those who find it sell it at prices equal to those commanded by the finest pearls; the Greeks call it 'asbestinon' [ἀσβέστινον], a name that denotes its peculiar property of being inextinguishable. Anaxilaus asserts that if a tree is encircled with cloth woven from this material, the sound of axe-blows will be muffled by it, and the tree may be felled without the noise being heard. It is on account of these qualities that this linen holds the very highest rank among all known types of cloth. The next rank belongs to the fabric known as 'byssus' [byssus], which is held in the highest esteem by women and is produced in the neighbourhood of Elis in Achaia. Some writers record that a scruple [scripulum] of it once sold for four denarii—the same rate, in fact, as gold. The downy nap of linen, and especially that stripped from the sails of sea-going ships, is very widely employed for medicinal purposes, and its ash possesses the same virtues as spodium [impure zinc oxide]. Among the poppies, moreover, there is a variety that imparts a remarkable whiteness to linen fabrics.

CHAPTER 5. AT WHAT PERIOD LINEN WAS FIRST DYED.
Efforts have even been made to dye linen and to make it take on the showy colours [frivolous colours] of our woollen cloths. This practice was first introduced in the fleet of Alexander the Great while he was sailing on the river Indus; for on one occasion, during a naval engagement, his generals and captains distinguished their vessels by the various hues of their sails, and astonished the people on the banks by displaying their many colours to the breeze that drove them on. It was also with purple sails that Cleopatra accompanied Mark Antony to the battle of Actium, and it was under cover of those sails that she took to flight—such being the distinguishing mark of the royal ship.

CHAPTER 6. AT WHAT PERIOD COLOURED AWNINGS WERE FIRST EMPLOYED IN THE THEATRES.
In more recent times, linen alone has been used to provide shade in our theatres. Q. Catulus was the first to put it to this use, on the occasion of his dedication of the Capitol. At a later date, Lentulus Spinther is said to have been the first to spread awnings of fine linen over a theatre, at the celebration of the Games in honour of Apollo. After him, Caesar [passage continues; text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: asbestinon (asbestos linen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asbestos (chrysotile or actinolite fibres woven into cloth)
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek term ἀσβέστινον, meaning 'inextinguishable.' Pliny notes it whitens only through fire and commands prices equal to the finest pearls. A funerary cloth of asbestos was reportedly found near Rome's Naevian Gate in 1702.

entities_mentioned.name: byssus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly sea-silk (Pinna nobilis byssus threads) or fine cotton (Gossypium sp.); ancient commentators are divided
entities_mentioned.notes: Translator's note suggests cotton fabric; Arrian, Apollonius, and Philostratus describe a tree with willow leaves and poplar shape, which does not match cotton. Some scholars favour sea-silk or a fine plant-fibre fabric from Elis in Achaia. Sold at four denarii per scruple, equalling the price of gold.

entities_mentioned.name: linen nap (from sails)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally; the ashes reportedly have virtues equivalent to spodium [impure zinc oxide or bone ash used as a desiccant/antiseptic in ancient medicine].

entities_mentioned.name: spodium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Impure zinc oxide or calcined bone/ivory ash
entities_mentioned.notes: A medicinal ash preparation used in antiquity as a desiccant and wound-healing agent; the text equates linen ash with spodium in therapeutic virtue.

entities_mentioned.name: poppy (variety that whitens linen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Papaver somniferum or a related Papaver sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny mentions a poppy variety used to bleach or whiten linen fabrics; exact species and mechanism unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Anaxilaus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited for the claim that asbestos cloth wrapped around a tree deadens the sound of axe-blows during felling.

entities_mentioned.name: Q. Catulus
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to be the first to use linen awnings for shade in Roman theatres, at the dedication of the Capitoline temple.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentulus Spinther
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to be the first to spread fine linen awnings specifically over a theatre, at the Ludi Apollinares.

OCR errors silently corrected: 'theai' → 'them'; 'wns' → 'was'; 'poppies' section reconstructed from context. 3. The medicinal application explicitly mentioned is the use of linen nap (especially from ship sails) for unspecified medicinal purposes, with its ash equated to spodium—a desiccant/wound-healing agent. No specific disease or preparation method beyond burning (for ash) is detailed. 4. The footnotes in the original are editorial additions by Bostock & Riley (19th-century translators), not by Pliny himself; they are preserved in the cleaned text as contextual scholarly annotations. 5. results.title_inferred: Spartum: Its Uses, Preparation, and Geographic Origin (Pliny, Natural History)
results.original_text:
With it they kindle their fires also, and prepare their torches; shoes also, and garments for the shepherds, are made of it. As a food for animals, it is highly injurious, with the sole exception of the tender tops of the shoots. When wanted for other uses, it is pulled up by the roots, with considerable labour; the legs of the persons so employed being protected by boots, and their hands with gloves, the plant being twisted round levers of bone or holm-oak, to get it up with the greater facility. At the present day it is gathered in the winter, even; but this work is done with the least difficulty between the ides of May and those of June, that being the period at which it is perfectly ripe.

CHAP. 8. THE MODE OF PREPARING SPARTUM.

When taken up it is made into sheaves, and laid in heaps for a couple of days, while it retains its life and freshness; on the third day the sheaves are opened out and spread in the sun to dry, after which it is again made up into sheaves, and placed under cover. It is then put to soak in sea-water, this being the best of all for the purpose, though fresh water will do in case sea-water cannot be procured: this done, it is again dried in the sun, and then moistened afresh. If it is wanted for immediate use, it is put in a tub and steeped in warm water, after which it is placed in an upright position to dry: this being universally admitted to be the most expeditious method of preparing it. To make it ready for use, it requires to be beaten out. Articles made of it are proof, more particularly, against the action of fresh or sea-water; but on dry land, ropes of hemp are generally preferred. Indeed, we find that spartum receives nutriment even from being under water, by way of compensation, as it were, for the thirst it has had to endure upon its native soil. By nature it is peculiarly well adapted for repairing, and however old the material may be, it unites very well with new. The person, indeed, who is desirous duly to appreciate this marvellous plant, has only to consider the numerous uses to which, in all parts of the world, it is applied: from it are made, the rigging of ships, various appliances of mechanism employed in building, and numerous other articles which supply the wants of daily life. To suffice for all these requirements, we find it growing solely on a tract of ground which lies upon the sea-line of the province of New Carthage, somewhat less than thirty miles in breadth by one hundred in length. The expense precludes its being transported to any very considerable distance.

CHAP. 9. AT WHAT PERIOD SPARTUM WAS FIRST EMPLOYED.

The Greeks used formerly to employ the [text continues]
results.translation:
They also use it [spartum] to kindle fires and make torches; shoes and garments for shepherds are likewise made from it. As fodder for animals it is highly harmful, with the sole exception of the tender growing tips of the shoots. When it is needed for other purposes, it is pulled up by the roots with considerable effort; the legs of those doing this work are protected by boots and their hands by gloves, and the plant is twisted around levers made of bone or holm-oak to lift it out more easily. In the present day it is even gathered during winter, but the work is most easily done between the Ides of May [15th of May] and the Ides of June [13th of June], that being the season when the plant is fully ripe.

CHAPTER 8. THE METHOD OF PREPARING SPARTUM.

Once lifted, it is bound into sheaves and piled up for two days while it still retains its vitality and freshness. On the third day the sheaves are opened out and spread in the sun to dry, after which they are again made up into sheaves and stored under cover. The material is then steeped in sea-water—this being the most suitable liquid for the purpose, though fresh water will serve if sea-water is unavailable. After this soaking it is dried again in the sun and then re-moistened. If it is needed for immediate use, it is placed in a vat and steeped in warm water, then set upright to dry—a method universally acknowledged as the quickest means of preparation. Before use it must be beaten out. Articles made from it are especially resistant to the action of both fresh and salt water; on dry land, however, hemp ropes are generally preferred. Indeed, spartum appears to draw nourishment from immersion in water, as if in recompense for the drought it endures on its native soil. By its nature it is particularly well suited to repair work, and however old the material, it joins very readily with new. Anyone wishing to appreciate this remarkable plant need only reflect on the many uses to which it is put throughout the world: from it are manufactured the rigging of ships, various mechanical devices used in construction, and many other articles serving the needs of everyday life. Yet for all these purposes, it grows only on a strip of land along the coastline of the province of New Carthage [Hispania Tarraconensis], less than thirty miles wide and one hundred miles long. The cost of transport prevents it from being carried to very great distances.

CHAPTER 9. WHEN SPARTUM FIRST CAME INTO USE.

The Greeks formerly employed the [text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: Spartum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stipa tenacissima L. (esparto grass); possibly also Lygeum spartum (false esparto / alvarde)
entities_mentioned.notes: A tough, fibrous grass native to the western Mediterranean, especially southeastern Spain (ancient New Carthage region). Used for rope, rigging, footwear (modern 'espartenas' / 'alpargatas'), torches, and shepherds' garments. Pliny notes it is harmful as animal fodder except for tender shoot tips. Preparation involved sheaving, sun-drying, soaking in sea-water or fresh water, re-drying, and beating.

entities_mentioned.name: Hemp (cannabis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as preferred over spartum for ropes used on dry land.

entities_mentioned.name: Holm-oak
entities_mentioned.notes: Wood used to make the levers employed in uprooting spartum.

entities_mentioned.name: New Carthage (province)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Region around Cartagena, southeastern Spain (Hispania Tarraconensis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the sole source of spartum in Pliny's time; a coastal strip less than 30 miles wide and 100 miles long.

OCR errors silently corrected include: 'injurious,67' (footnote marker removed from running text), 'at the'present' → 'at the present', 'EI1IOPHOKUS' (OCR artifact for 'ERIOPHORUS' in a chapter heading appearing mid-text—retained in cleaned original as contextual noise from the printed edition's marginalia). Footnotes 62–68 appearing inline have been retained in the cleaned original but not carried into the translation body, as they are editorial additions by the 19th-century translator, not Pliny's text. The 'Bulb Eriophorus' chapter heading visible mid-text (p. 141 reference) is a page-break artifact from the printed edition and does not belong to the spartum discussion.

results.title_inferred: On the Bulb Eriophorus, Spartum, Plants Without Roots, and Truffles
results.original_text: linden-tree. It seems to me very probable, too, that it was from them that the Carthaginians borrowed the first hint for applying spartum to a similar purpose. CHAP. 10. THE BULB ERIOPHORUS. Theophrastus informs us, that there is a kind of bulb, which grows on the banks of rivers, and which encloses between the outer coat and the portion that is eaten a sort of woolly substance, of which felt socks, and other articles of dress, are made; but, in the copies, those at least which have fallen in my way, there is no mention made of the country in which it grows, or of any details in connection with it, beyond the fact that the name given to it is "eriophoron." As to spartum, he makes no mention of it whatever, although he has given the history, with the greatest exactness, of all the known plants, three hundred and ninety years before our time — a fact to which I have already alluded on other occasions: from this it would appear that spartum has come into use since his day. CHAP. 11. — PLANTS WHICH SPRING UP AND GROW WITHOUT A ROOT. PLANTS WHICH GROW, BUT CANNOT BE REPRODUCED FROM SEED. As we have here made a beginning of treating of the marvels of Nature, we shall proceed to examine them in detail; and among them the very greatest of all, beyond a doubt, is the fact that any plant should spring up and grow without a root. Such, for instance, is the vegetable production known as the truffle; surrounded on every side by earth, it is connected with it by no fibres, not so much as a single thread even, while the spot in which it grows presents neither protuberance nor cleft to the view. It is found, in fact, in no way adhering to the earth, but enclosed within an outer coat; so much so, indeed, that though we cannot exactly pronounce it to be composed of earth, we must conclude that it is nothing else but a callous concretion of the earth. Truffles generally grow in dry, sandy soils, and spots that are thickly covered with shrubs; in size they are often larger than a quince, and are found to weigh as much as a pound. There are two kinds of them, the one full of sand, and consequently injurious to the teeth, the other free from sand and all impurities. They are distinguished also by their colour, which is red or black, and white within; those of Africa are the most esteemed. Whether the truffle grows gradually, or whether this blemish of the earth — for it can be looked upon as nothing else — at once assumes the globular form and magnitude which it presents when found.
results.translation:
linden-tree. It seems to me very probable, too, that it was from them that the Carthaginians borrowed the first hint for applying spartum [esparto grass] to a similar purpose.

CHAPTER 10. THE BULB ERIOPHORUS.

Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum, Book 13, Chapter 13) informs us that there is a kind of bulb which grows on the banks of rivers, and which encloses between its outer coat and the edible portion a sort of woolly substance, from which felt socks and other articles of clothing are made. However, in the manuscript copies I have been able to consult, no mention is made of the country in which it grows, nor of any further details concerning it, beyond the fact that it is called "eriophoron" [wool-bearer]. As for spartum, Theophrastus makes no mention of it whatsoever — though he recorded, with the greatest exactness, the history of all known plants three hundred and ninety years before our time, a fact to which I have already referred on other occasions. From this it would appear that spartum only came into common use after his day.

CHAPTER 11. PLANTS WHICH SPRING UP AND GROW WITHOUT A ROOT. PLANTS WHICH GROW BUT CANNOT BE REPRODUCED FROM SEED.

Having here begun to treat of the marvels of Nature, we shall proceed to examine them in detail. Among them, beyond all doubt the very greatest is the fact that any plant should spring up and grow without a root. Such, for instance, is the vegetable production known as the truffle [tuber]. Surrounded on every side by earth, it is connected with the soil by no fibres — not so much as a single thread — while the spot in which it grows presents neither protuberance nor cleft to the view. It is found, in fact, in no way adhering to the earth, but enclosed within an outer coat [cortex]; so much so, indeed, that though we cannot exactly pronounce it to be composed of earth itself, we must conclude that it is nothing other than a callous [hardened] concretion of the earth.

Truffles generally grow in dry, sandy soils and in spots thickly covered with shrubs. In size they are often larger than a quince, and are found to weigh as much as a pound. There are two kinds: one full of sand, and consequently injurious to the teeth; the other free from sand and all impurities. They are also distinguished by their colour, which is red or black, and white within. Those of Africa are the most highly esteemed. Whether the truffle grows gradually, or whether this blemish of the earth — for it can be looked upon as nothing else — at once assumes the globular form and magnitude it presents when found [the text breaks off here].

entities_mentioned.name: spartum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stipa tenacissima (esparto grass) or Lygeum spartum
entities_mentioned.notes: A fibrous grass used for rope-making and other textile purposes. Pliny notes Theophrastus does not mention it, suggesting it came into use after Theophrastus's time; however, footnote 72 indicates Theophrastus does in fact mention it in Historia Plantarum Book 1, Chapter 8.

entities_mentioned.name: eriophoron (bulb eriophorus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a species of Eriophorum (cottongrass, Cyperaceae), though Féé doubts the identification with Eriophorum angustifolium
entities_mentioned.notes: A riverside bulbous plant producing a woolly substance between its outer coat and edible interior, used to make felt socks and garments. Theophrastus is the source. Dodonaeus considered it identical to Eriophorum angustifolium of Linnaeus; Féé disagrees.

entities_mentioned.name: truffle (tuber)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tuber melanosporum (black truffle) and possibly Tuber aestivum or related species; Linnaeus identified it as Tuber cibarium
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny describes the truffle as a rootless subterranean growth, enclosed in a cortex, with no fibrous connection to the soil. He distinguishes sandy (tooth-damaging) versus clean varieties, and red/black versus white interior types. African truffles most prized. Féé interprets the 'callous concretion' as a hymenium of vesicles.

entities_mentioned.name: Theophrastus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theophrastus of Eresus (c. 371–287 BCE), author of Historia Plantarum
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny cites him as writing approximately 390 years before Pliny's own time.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English translation (by John Bostock and H.T. Riley, standard 19th-century edition) of Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XIX, Chapters 10–11. The source text is already in English; the task here is to produce a clean, annotated scholarly version. Footnote 72 flags an important discrepancy: Pliny claims Theophrastus never mentions spartum, but in fact Theophrastus does mention it (Historia Plantarum 1.8). The entity 'eriophoron' defies confident modern identification; the woolly substance it produces is analogous to the seed-head fibers of cottongrass (Eriophorum spp.), but the 'bulb' and 'edible portion' described do not match Eriophorum angustifolium well. The description of truffles as rootless 'concretions of the earth' reflects ancient uncertainty about fungal biology. The text breaks off mid-sentence at the end of the segment.

results.title_inferred: Laserpitium, Laser, and Maspetum: On the Plant Silphium and Its Resin
results.original_text: It was the produce of this last country, probably, that afforded the juice or gum resin here mentioned as 'laser,' and so highly esteemed by the ancients, as forming a component part of their perfumes. Fee is inclined to think that the Laserpitium here spoken of was the Thapsia silphium, and to mark it as a remarkable plant, known to the Greeks by the name of 'silphion,' and originally a native of the province of Cyrenaica. The juice of this plant is called 'laser,' and it is greatly in vogue for medicinal as well as other purposes, being sold at the same rate as silver. For these many years past, however, it has not been found in Cyrenaica, as the farmers of the revenue who hold the lands there on lease, have a notion that it is more profitable to depasture flocks of sheep upon them. Within the memory of the present generation, a single stalk is all that has ever been found there, and that was sent as a curiosity to the Emperor Nero. If it so happen that one of the flock, while grazing, meets with a growing shoot of it, the fact is easily ascertained by the following signs; the sheep, after eating of it, immediately falls asleep, while the goat is seized with a fit of sneezing. For this long time past, there has been no other laser imported into this country, but that produced in either Persis, Media, or Armenia, where it grows in considerable abundance, though much inferior to that of Cyrenaica; and even then it is extensively adulterated with gum, sacopenium, or pounded beans. I ought the less then to reject the more general opinion that it is identical with the Ferula asafoetida. Pliny has probably caused some confusion by blending the description of other writers with that given by Theophrastus, each having in view a different plant. Indeed, whatever the Laserpitium or Silphium of other countries may have been, it is not improbable that the odoriferous plant of Cyrenaica was not identical with the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. The foliage of the Thapsia silphium is exactly similar to that of the Laserpitium as depicted on medals of Cyrenaica, still extant. We learn from Littré, that Dr. Guyon showed, in 1842, to the Académie des Sciences, a plant which the Arabs of Algeria employ as a purgative, and which they call bonnefa. It is the Thapsia Garganica of Desfontaines, and is considered by Guyon to be identical with the Silphium of the ancients. [Footnote 86: See B. xxii. c. 48. In the 'Rudens' of Plautus, the scene of which is near Cyrene, frequent allusion is made to the growth of laserpitium there, and the preparation and export of the resin, as forming the staple article of commerce.] [Footnote 87: Scribonius Largus, who lived in the time of Tiberius, speaks of using in a prescription laser of Cyrenaica, 'if it can be met with'; 'si poterit inveniri.'] [Footnote 88: 'In spem nascentis.'] [Footnote 89: Fee remarks that Pliny has not found this absurd story in any of the works from which he has compiled his account, but that it is entirely his own.] [Footnote 90: This was probably the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus.] [Footnote 91: See B. xx. c. 75.] —omit the facts, that in the consulship of C. Valerius and M. Herennius, there was brought to Rome, from Cyrenae, for the public service, thirty pounds' weight of laserpitium, and that the Dictator Caesar, at the beginning of the Civil War, took from out of the public treasury, besides gold and silver, no less than fifteen hundred pounds of [laserpitium].
results.translation: It was likely the produce of this last country [Cyrenaica] that furnished the juice or gum resin referred to here as 'laser,' so highly prized by the ancients as a constituent of their perfumes. [The commentator] Fee is inclined to identify the Laserpitium here described with Thapsia silphium, and to characterize it as a remarkable plant, known to the Greeks under the name 'silphion,' and originally native to the province of Cyrenaica. The juice of this plant is called 'laser,' and it was greatly in demand for medicinal as well as other purposes, being sold at the same price as silver. For many years past, however, it has not been found in Cyrenaica, since the tax-farmers who lease the lands there considered it more profitable to graze flocks of sheep upon them. Within living memory, only a single stalk has ever been found there, and that was sent as a curiosity to the Emperor Nero. Should it happen that one of the grazing flock encounters a growing shoot of the plant, the fact may be readily determined by the following signs: the sheep, having eaten of it, immediately falls asleep, while the goat is seized with a fit of sneezing. For a long time now, no laser other than that produced in Persis [Persia], Media, or Armenia has been imported into this country [Rome], where it grows in considerable abundance, though far inferior to that of Cyrenaica; and even this imported laser is extensively adulterated with gum, sacopenium [sagapenum], or ground beans. I have the less reason, therefore, to reject the more widely held opinion that it is identical with Ferula asafoetida. Pliny has probably introduced some confusion by combining the descriptions of other writers with that of Theophrastus, each of whom had a different plant in mind. Indeed, whatever the Laserpitium or Silphium of other regions may have been, it is not improbable that the odoriferous plant of Cyrenaica was not the same as the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. The foliage of Thapsia silphium is exactly similar to that of the Laserpitium as it is depicted on medals of Cyrenaica still extant. We learn from Littré that Dr. Guyon presented to the Académie des Sciences in 1842 a plant which the Arabs of Algeria use as a purgative and call bonnefa. This is the Thapsia Garganica of Desfontaines, and is considered by Guyon to be identical with the Silphium of the ancients. [Footnote: See Book xxii, chapter 48. In Plautus's 'Rudens,' the scene of which is set near Cyrene, frequent reference is made to the cultivation of laserpitium there, and to the preparation and export of the resin as the chief article of commerce. — Scribonius Largus, who lived in the time of Tiberius, mentions using laser of Cyrenaica in a prescription 'if it can be obtained' ('si poterit inveniri'). — Fee remarks that Pliny has not found this curious story about sheep and goats in any of the sources he compiled, and that it is entirely his own addition. — The inferior laser of Persia and Media was probably the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. — See Book xx, chapter 75 for sacopenium.] Not to omit the further facts: that in the consulship of C. Valerius and M. Herennius, thirty pounds' weight of laserpitium was brought to Rome from Cyrene for public use; and that the Dictator Caesar, at the outbreak of the Civil War, withdrew from the public treasury, in addition to gold and silver, no less than fifteen hundred pounds of [laserpitium].

entities_mentioned.name: Laserpitium / Silphium / Silphion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely extinct; possibly Ferula tingitana, Thapsia silphium, or a related Apiaceae species native to Cyrenaica (modern Libya)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of the Cyrenaic silphium remains unresolved; it is considered possibly extinct. The text distinguishes it from the inferior Persian/Median/Armenian laser, which is more confidently identified as Ferula asafoetida.

entities_mentioned.name: Laser
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gum resin of silphium; the Persian/Median variety likely corresponds to asafoetida (Ferula asafoetida L.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally and as a perfume ingredient. Sold at the price of silver. Subject to adulteration with gum, sagapenum, and ground beans. Cyrenaic laser was considered far superior to that from Persis, Media, or Armenia.

entities_mentioned.name: Thapsia silphium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Thapsia garganica or a related species (Apiaceae); Fee's identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Fee and Guyon both propose this identification based on the appearance of the foliage as depicted on Cyrenaican medals.

entities_mentioned.name: Thapsia Garganica (Desfontaines)
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'bonnefa' by Algerian Arabs; used as a purgative. Dr. Guyon presented it to the Académie des Sciences in 1842 as a candidate for the ancient silphium.

entities_mentioned.name: Ferula asafoetida (Linnaeus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by the editor as the likely source of the inferior laser imported from Persis, Media, and Armenia. The text notes that Pliny's conflation of sources may have led him to blur the distinction between this and the true Cyrenaic silphium.

entities_mentioned.name: Sacopenium / Sagapenum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ferula szowitsiana DC. or related species; sagapenum gum resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as one of the adulterants used to dilute imported laser. See Pliny Book xx, chapter 75.

entities_mentioned.name: Bonnefa
entities_mentioned.notes: Arab name used in Algeria for a plant employed as a purgative; identified by Guyon as the ancient silphium.

The source text is thus a 19th-century English scholarly edition with embedded footnotes and commentary (likely Holland's or Bostock and Riley's edition). The OCR garbled several words: 'LA8EKPITITJM' = 'LASERPITIUM'; 'asafcetida' = 'asafoetida'; 'Linna3us' = 'Linnaeus'; 'Littre' = 'Littré'; 'HISTOET' = 'HISTORY'. The segment is truncated at the end ('fifteen hundred pounds o'); the missing word is almost certainly 'laserpitium.' Medicinal use of laser is asserted but not described in detail in this excerpt; the text primarily concerns identification, commerce, and adulteration. The condition treated is not specified in this chunk. Preparation: the juice (gum resin) is extracted from the plant.

results.title_inferred: Laserpitium (Silphium) and Magydaris: Description, Properties, and Uses
results.original_text:
remarkable fall of rain extended, it is said, over no less than four thousand stadia of the African territory; and upon this soil laserpitium began universally to grow, a plant that is in general wild and stubborn, and which, if attempted to be cultivated, will leave the spot where it has been sown quite desolate and barren. The roots of it are numerous and thick, the stalk being like that of fennel-giant, and of similar thickness. The leaves of this plant were known as "maspetum," and bore a considerable resemblance to parsley; the seeds of it were foliaceous, and the plant shed its leaves every year. They used to feed the cattle there upon it; at first it purged them, but afterwards they would grow fat, the flesh being improved in flavour in a most surprising degree. After the fall of the leaf, the people themselves were in the habit of eating the stalk, either roasted or boiled: from the drastic effects of this diet the body was purged for the first forty days, all vicious humours being effectually removed. The juices of this plant were collected two different ways, either from the root or from the stalk; in consequence of which these two varieties of the juice were known by the distinguishing names of "rhizias" and "caulias," the last being of inferior quality to the other, and very apt to turn putrid. Upon the root there was a black bark, which was extensively employed for the purposes of adulteration. The juice of the plant was received in vessels, and mixed there with a layer of bran; after which, from time to time it was shaken, till it had reached a proper state of maturity; indeed, if this precaution was neglected, it was apt to turn putrid. The signs that it had come to maturity were its colour, its dryness, and the absorption of all humidity. There are some authors, however, who state that the root of laserpitium was more than a cubit in length, and that it presented a tuberosity above the surface of the earth. An incision, they say, was made in this tuberosity, from which a juice would flow, like milk in appearance; above the tuberosity grew a stalk, to which they give the name of "magydaris;" the leaves that grew upon this stalk were of the colour of gold, and, falling at the rising of the Dog-star, when the south winds begin to prevail, they acted as seed for the purposes of reproduction. It was from these leaves, too, they say, that laserpitium was produced, the root and the stalk attaining their full growth in the space of one year. The same writers also state, that it was the practice to turn up the ground about the plant, and that it had no such effect as purging the cattle that were fed upon it; though one result of using it as food was, that such cattle as were ailing were either cured of their distempers, or else died immediately upon eating of it, a thing, however, that but rarely happened. The first description, however, is found to agree more nearly with the silphium that comes from Persis.

CHAP. 16. MAGYDARIS.
There is another variety of this plant, known as [magydaris].
results.translation:
A remarkable fall of rain is said to have extended over no less than four thousand stadia [roughly 460 miles] of African territory; and upon this soil laserpitium [silphium] began to grow universally — a plant that is in general wild and stubborn, and which, if one attempts to cultivate it, will leave the ground where it was sown quite desolate and barren. Its roots are numerous and thick, and its stalk resembles that of giant fennel [ferula] in form and thickness. The leaves of this plant were known as "maspetum" and bore a considerable resemblance to parsley; its seeds were foliaceous [leaf-like], and the plant shed its leaves every year. In that region they used to feed cattle upon it; at first it purged them, but afterwards they would grow fat, the flesh being improved in flavour to a most surprising degree. After leaf-fall, the people themselves were accustomed to eating the stalk, either roasted or boiled: owing to the drastic effects of this diet, the body was purged for the first forty days, all corrupt humours being thoroughly expelled. The juices of this plant were collected in two different ways — either from the root or from the stalk — and accordingly these two varieties of juice were distinguished by the names "rhizias" [root-juice] and "caulias" [stalk-juice], the latter being of inferior quality to the former and very liable to putrefaction. Upon the root there was a black bark, which was widely used for purposes of adulteration. The juice of the plant was received into vessels and mixed with a layer of bran; it was then shaken periodically until it reached a proper state of maturity — for if this precaution were neglected, it was prone to turn putrid. The signs of its maturity were its colour, its dryness, and the absorption of all moisture.

Some authors, however, state that the root of laserpitium was more than a cubit in length and that it produced a tuberosity above the surface of the earth. An incision was made in this tuberosity, they say, from which a milky juice would flow; above the tuberosity grew a stalk to which they give the name "magydaris." The leaves growing upon this stalk were golden in colour and, falling at the rising of the Dog-star when the south winds begin to prevail, served as seed for reproduction. From these leaves, they say, laserpitium was also produced, the root and stalk reaching full growth within a single year. The same writers further state that it was customary to turn up the ground around the plant, and that it had no purgative effect upon the cattle fed upon it; though one consequence of its use as fodder was that ailing cattle were either cured of their ailments or died immediately upon eating it — an outcome, however, that occurred only rarely. The first description, however, is found to correspond more closely with the silphium that comes from Persia.

CHAPTER 16. MAGYDARIS.
There is another variety of this plant, known as [magydaris].

entities_mentioned.name: laserpitium (silphium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ferula tingitana, Ferula galbaniflua, or the extinct Cyrenaican silphium (possibly Ferula historica or a closely related Ferula species)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of silphium/laserpitium remains disputed and possibly represents a now-extinct taxon. Pliny distinguishes between the African (Cyrenaic) form and the Persian variety, which some scholars associate with asafoetida (Ferula assa-foetida). The leaf-name 'maspetum' and juice-names 'rhizias' and 'caulias' are technical Greek/Latin terms preserved by Pliny.

entities_mentioned.name: maspetum
entities_mentioned.notes: The name given to the leaves of laserpitium; described as resembling parsley. Not independently identified in modern botany.

entities_mentioned.name: rhizias
entities_mentioned.notes: The juice extracted from the root of laserpitium; considered the superior variety of the two juice types.

entities_mentioned.name: caulias
entities_mentioned.notes: The juice extracted from the stalk of laserpitium; considered inferior in quality and prone to putrefaction.

entities_mentioned.name: magydaris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Laser trilobum (syn. Magydaris panacifolia) or another large-umbellifer related to ferula
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Pliny as both the name of the stalk portion of laserpitium and as the name of a distinct variety of the plant described in Chapter 16. The Greek botanical tradition also uses magydaris for a separate umbelliferous taxon.

entities_mentioned.name: fennel-giant (ferula)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a reference plant for the morphological description of the stalk of laserpitium.

entities_mentioned.name: silphium from Persis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ferula assa-foetida (asafoetida) or a closely related Ferula species
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny notes that the second morphological description of laserpitium (with tuberosity and milky juice) corresponds more closely to the Persian variety, which modern scholars generally associate with asafoetida.

results.translator_notes:
1. The footnote reference to A.U.C. 661 (i.e., 93 BCE) appears to anchor a historical event mentioned just before this passage, presumably relating to the founding or rediscovery of the laserpitium territory; context is cut off at the segment boundary.
2. Footnote 94 notes that the description of the edible stalk 'could hardly apply to the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus, the stalk of it being extremely acrid, and the juice fetid in the highest degree' — this is the scholar Fée's observation, not Pliny's.
3. Footnote 95 flags that the Latin 'Vitia his omnibus' is likely a corrupt reading.
4. The chapter heading 'MAGTDATUS' in the OCR is clearly a corruption of 'MAGYDARIS' — silently corrected.
5. The phrase 'vicious humours' is retained as the period medical translation of Latin vitia (lit. 'faults, defects, corruptions'), consistent with humoral pathology vocabulary appropriate to the archive's scope.
6. 'Foliaceous seeds' likely refers to seeds with membranous or wing-like structures, characteristic of many Apiaceae/Umbelliferae.

results.title_inferred: On Laserpitium, Madder, and Radicula: Plants Used in Dyeing and Medicine
results.original_text:
The whole of this paragraph has been borrowed from Theophrastus, Hist. Plant. B. vi. c. iii. Sprengel takes this to be the Laserpitium ferulaceum of Linnaeus, but Fee thinks it is more than doubtful if the identity can be established. From Theophrastus. Dioscorides says, on the other hand, that it grows in Libya. grows also upon Mount Parnassus, in great abundance, a plant to which some persons give the name of "laserpitium:" by means of all these varieties, adulterations are effected of a production that is held in the highest esteem for its salutary qualities and its general usefulness. The chief proofs of its genuineness consist in its colour, which ought to be slightly red without, and when broken quite white and transparent within; the drops of it, too, should melt very rapidly on the application of spittle. It is extensively employed for medicinal purposes.

CHAP. 17. MADDER.
There are two other plants also, which are but little known to any but the herd of the sordid and avaricious, and this because of the large profits that are derived from them. The first of these is madder, the employment of which is necessary in dyeing wool and leather. The madder of Italy is the most esteemed, and that more particularly which is grown in the suburbs of the City; nearly all our provinces, too, produce it in great abundance. It grows spontaneously, but is capable of reproduction by sowing, much after the same manner as the fitch. The stem, however, is prickly, and articulated, with five leaves arranged round each joint: the seed is red. Its medicinal properties we shall have occasion to mention in the appropriate place.

CHAP. 18. THE RADICULA.
The plant known to us by the name of "radicula," is the second of these productions. It furnishes a juice that is extensively employed in washing wool, and it is quite wonderful how greatly it contributes to the whiteness and softness of wool. It may be produced anywhere by cultivation, but that which grows spontaneously in Asia and Syria, upon rugged, rocky sites, is more highly esteemed. That, however, which is found beyond the Euphrates has the highest repute of all. The stalk of it is ferulaceous and thin, and is sought by the inhabitants of those countries as an article of food. It is employed also for making unguents, being boiled up with the other ingredients, whatever they may happen to be. In leaf it strongly resembles the olive. The Greeks...
results.translation:
This passage is drawn in its entirety from Theophrastus, Natural History of Plants, Book VI, Chapter III. Sprengel identifies the plant in question as Laserpitium ferulaceum of Linnaeus, but Fée considers it more than doubtful that this identification can be firmly established. According to Theophrastus, while Dioscorides states that it grows in Libya, it also grows upon Mount Parnassus in great abundance — a plant to which some persons give the name 'laserpitium.' Through all these various species, adulteration is carried out of a product held in the highest esteem for its health-giving qualities and its general utility. The chief marks of its genuineness lie in its colour, which ought to be slightly reddish on the outside, and when broken, quite white and transparent within; moreover, drops of it should dissolve very rapidly upon contact with saliva. It is extensively employed for medicinal purposes.

CHAPTER 17. MADDER.
There are two further plants which are little known except to the greedy and avaricious, owing to the great profits that can be derived from them. The first of these is madder [Rubia tinctorum], the use of which is indispensable in dyeing wool and leather. The madder of Italy is the most highly regarded, and especially that grown in the environs of the City [Rome]; nearly all our provinces likewise produce it in great abundance. It grows wild of its own accord, but can also be propagated by sowing, in much the same manner as the fitch [vetch]. Its stem, however, is prickly and jointed, with five leaves arranged around each node; the seed is red. We shall have occasion to discuss its medicinal properties in the appropriate place.

CHAPTER 18. THE RADICULA.
The plant known to us by the name 'radicula' [little root] — though in reality, as Pliny notes, it possesses a large root — is the second of these productions. It yields a juice that is extensively used in washing wool, and it is quite remarkable how greatly it contributes to the whiteness and softness of that material. It may be cultivated almost anywhere, but the plant that grows wild in Asia and Syria, on rough and rocky ground, is held in higher esteem. That found beyond the Euphrates, however, enjoys the greatest reputation of all. Its stalk is ferulaceous [fennel-like] and slender, and is sought out by the inhabitants of those regions as a foodstuff. It is also employed in the preparation of unguents, being boiled together with whatever other ingredients are used. In its leaves it closely resembles the olive. The Greeks...

entities_mentioned.name: laserpitium (laserwort)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Laserpitium ferulaceum Linnaeus, or Ferula Tingitana / Ptychotis verticillata (De Candolle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity disputed by Sprengel, Fée, and Fraas. Grows on Mount Parnassus and in Libya according to different ancient sources. Used medicinally and highly valued; subject to adulteration. Authenticity tested by reddish exterior, white-transparent interior when broken, and rapid dissolution in saliva.

entities_mentioned.name: madder (rubia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for dyeing wool and leather. Italian madder most esteemed, especially from the suburbs of Rome. Stem prickly and articulated, five leaves per node, red seed. Medicinal properties discussed by Pliny in Book XXIV, Chapter 56.

entities_mentioned.name: radicula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Reseda luteola L. (dyer's weed / weld), though identification is disputed
entities_mentioned.notes: Name means 'little root,' though the root is actually large. Juice used for washing and whitening wool. Best specimens from wild plants in Asia and Syria, especially beyond the Euphrates. Stalk ferulaceous and edible locally. Also used in unguent preparation. Leaves resemble olive leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: fitch (ervilia / orobus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a comparative reference for the mode of propagation of madder. Pliny's footnote notes there is in fact no botanical resemblance between madder and the fitch.

entities_mentioned.name: Theophrastus, Hist. Plant. Book VI, Chapter III
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient source cited as the origin of the preceding paragraph on laserpitium.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 16–18, as rendered in the Bohn Classical Library English translation (likely John Bostock and H.T. Riley). The source text contains extensive editorial footnotes interspersed with the main text, which have been preserved and integrated. The footnote referencing 'Littre' and 'M. Fraas' regarding Magydaris and Laserpitium as possibly Ferula Tingitana and Ptychotis verticillata is a scholarly annotation from the 19th-century editors, not from Pliny himself. The text breaks off mid-sentence ('The Greeks...') indicating the chunk ends before completion of Chapter 18. The OCR error 'Lascrpitmrn' has been silently corrected to 'Laserpitium'; 'B. \i. c. iii.' has been corrected to 'B. vi. c. iii.' based on context.

results.title_inferred: On Gardens: Historical and Cultural Notes from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX
results.original_text: the garden, a subject recommended by its own intrinsic merits to our notice: for we find that in remote antiquity, even, there was nothing looked upon with a greater degree of admiration than the gardens of the Hesperides, those of the kings Adonis and Alcinous, and the Hanging Gardens, whether they were the work of Semiramis, or whether of Cyrus, king of Assyria, a subject of which we shall have to speak in another work. The kings of Rome cultivated their gardens with their own hands; indeed, it was from his garden that Tarquinius Superbus sent to his son that cruel and sanguinary message of his. In our laws of the Twelve Tables, we find the word 'villa,' or 'farm,' nowhere mentioned; it is the word 'hortus' that is always used with that signification, while the term 'heredium' we find employed for 'garden.' There are certain religious impressions, too, that have been attached to this species of property, and we find that it is in the garden and the Forum only that statues of satyrs are consecrated, as a protection against the evil effects of spells and sorcery; although in Plautus, we find the gardens spoken of as being under the tutelage of Venus. At the present day, under the general name of gardens, we have pleasure-grounds situate in the very heart of the City, as well as extensive fields and villas.
