At Rome, on the other hand, the garden constituted of itself the poor man's field, and it was from the garden that the lower classes procured their daily food — an aliment how guiltlessly obtained! But still, it is a great deal better, no doubt, to dive into the abysses of the...
results.translation: The garden is a subject recommended by its own intrinsic merits to our notice. For we find that even in remote antiquity nothing was looked upon with a greater degree of admiration than the gardens of the Hesperides, those of the kings Adonis and Alcinous, and the Hanging Gardens — whether these last were the work of Semiramis or of Cyrus, king of Assyria — a subject of which we shall have occasion to speak in another work. The kings of Rome cultivated their gardens with their own hands; indeed, it was from his garden that Tarquinius Superbus sent his son that cruel and bloody message. In the laws of the Twelve Tables, the word 'villa' [farm] is nowhere mentioned; it is always the word 'hortus' [kitchen-garden] that carries that meaning, while 'heredium' is the term employed for 'garden.' Certain religious associations have also attached themselves to this kind of property: it is in the garden and in the Forum alone that statues of satyrs are consecrated, as a protection against the harmful effects [effascinationes] of spells and sorcery — though in Plautus the gardens are spoken of as being under the protection of Venus. In our own day, under the general name of gardens, we have pleasure-grounds situated in the very heart of the City, as well as extensive fields and country villas. Epicurus, that connoisseur in the pleasures of a life of ease, was the first to lay out a garden at Athens; until his time it had never been conceived to dwell amid countryside in the middle of the city. At Rome, on the other hand, the garden constituted in itself the poor man's field, and it was from the garden that the lower classes obtained their daily sustenance — how innocently procured! And yet, no doubt, it is far better to dive into the abysses of the [text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: Radicula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Reseda luteola (weld/dyer's rocket) or Gypsophila struthium
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote text debates whether Radicula is Reseda luteola or Gypsophila struthium of Linnaeus; Fee considers neither identification sufficiently warranted. Used historically as a dyeing agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Gypsophila struthium
entities_mentioned.notes: Grows in Spain; root and stalk reportedly used by Spaniards for the same purposes as the ancients used Radicula. Used as a detergent/cleansing agent and possibly for dyeing.

entities_mentioned.name: Gardens of the Hesperides
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological gardens; referenced as an archetype of admired ancient gardens.

entities_mentioned.name: Gardens of Adonis
entities_mentioned.notes: Adonis identified as a fabulous king of Phoenicia, possibly identical with Thammuz of Scripture (Ezekiel viii. 14) and of Assyrian/Syrian religious mythology.

entities_mentioned.name: Gardens of Alcinous
entities_mentioned.notes: Celebrated by Homer in the Odyssey, Books VI and XIII.

entities_mentioned.name: Hanging Gardens
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed variously to Semiramis or Cyrus, king of Assyria. Pliny apparently intended to describe them in a separate work he did not live to complete.

entities_mentioned.name: Tarquinius Superbus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tarquinius Superbus, last king of Rome
entities_mentioned.notes: Sent a message to his son from his garden; story elaborated in Book XIX, c. 53.

entities_mentioned.name: Epicurus
entities_mentioned.notes: Credited by Pliny as the first to establish a garden at Athens as a dwelling place within the city.

results.translator_notes: This segment is heavily interpolated with editorial footnotes from the translator/editor (likely Bostock & Riley's 19th-century English edition of Pliny), making it difficult to cleanly separate Pliny's original text from commentary. The footnote material discusses botanical identification of 'Radicula' and biographical/mythological context. The segment ends mid-sentence. The term 'effascinationes' (evil eye / fascination spells) is preserved in brackets in the translation as period technical vocabulary. No direct medical or materia medica content appears in this chunk; the botanical footnote references (Radicula, Gypsophila struthium) relate to dyeing/cleansing rather than medicinal use in this passage.

results.title_inferred: On Gardens, Garden Layout, and the Natural History of Garden Plants
results.original_text: and that no Indian peppers were in request with them, or any of those other condiments which we are in the habit of seeking beyond the seas. In former times the lower classes of Rome, with their mimic gardens in their windows, day after day presented the reflex of the country to the eye, when as yet the multitudes of atrocious burglaries, almost innumerable, had not compelled us to shut out all such sights with bars to the passers by. Let the garden, then, have its due meed of honour, and let not things, because they are common, enjoy for that the less share of our consideration — and the more so, as we find that from it men of the very highest rank have been content to borrow their surnames even; thus in the Valerian family, for instance, the Lactucini have not thought themselves disgraced by taking their name from the lettuce. Perhaps, too, our labours and research may contribute some slight recommendation to this our subject; although, with Virgil, we are ready to admit how difficult it is, by language however elevated, to ennoble a subject that is so humble in itself. CHAP. 20. — THE LAYING OUT OF GARDEN GROUND. There is no doubt that the proper plan is, to have the gardens adjoining the country-house; and they should be watered, more particularly, by a river running in front of it, if possible; or else with water drawn from a well by the aid of a wheel or of pumps, or by swipes [tolleno: a counterbalanced beam used for raising water]. The ground should be opened just as the west winds are beginning to prevail; fourteen days after which it should be got ready for autumn, and then before the winter solstice it should have another turning up. It will require eight men to dig a jugerum [Roman unit of area, approximately 0.25 hectares], manure being mixed with the earth to a depth of three feet: the ground, too, should be divided into plots or beds with raised and rounded edges, each of which should have a path dug round it, by means of which access may be afforded to the gardener and a channel formed for the water needed for irrigation. CHAP. 21. PLANTS OTHER THAN GRAIN AND SHRUBS. Among the garden plants there are some that recommend themselves by their bulbs, others by the head, others by the stalk, others by the leaf, others by both: some, again, are valued for their seed, others for the outer coat, others for their membranous tissues, others for their cartilaginous substance, others for the firmness of their flesh, and others for the fleshy tunics in which they are enveloped. CHAP. 22. THE NATURAL HISTORY OF TWENTY DIFFERENT KINDS OF PLANTS WHICH GROW IN GARDENS — THE PROPER METHODS TO BE FOLLOWED IN SOWING THEM RESPECTIVELY. Of some plants the fruits are in the earth, of others both in the earth and out of it, and of others, again, out of the earth solely. Some of them increase as they lie upon the ground, gourds and cucumbers, for instance; the same products will grow also in a hanging position, but they are much heavier even then than any of the fruits that grow upon trees. The cucumber, however, is composed of cartilage and a fleshy substance.
results.translation: And that no Indian peppers were sought after by them, nor any of those other condiments which we are accustomed to seeking from beyond the seas. In former times the common people of Rome, with their imitation gardens in their windows, presented day after day a reflection of the countryside to the eye, before the multitude of atrocious burglaries — almost beyond counting — had compelled us to shut out all such sights from passers-by with iron bars. Let the garden, then, receive its proper measure of honour, and let things not be held in lesser esteem merely because they are commonplace — especially since we find that men of the very highest rank have been content to borrow their family surnames from it; so, in the Valerian family, the Lactucini did not consider it a disgrace to take their name from the lettuce [Lactuca sativa]. Perhaps, too, our own labours and research may lend some small recommendation to this subject of ours; though, with Virgil, we are prepared to acknowledge how difficult it is, even with the most elevated language, to ennoble a subject so humble in itself. CHAPTER 20. — THE LAYING OUT OF GARDEN GROUND. There is no doubt that the proper plan is to situate gardens adjacent to the country house; and they should be watered, most advantageously, by a river flowing before it if possible; or else with water drawn from a well by means of a wheel, pumps, or swipes [tolleno: a counterbalanced beam-and-bucket device for raising water]. The ground should be broken open just as the west winds begin to prevail; fourteen days after that it should be prepared for autumn, and then before the winter solstice it should be turned up again. It will require eight men to dig a jugerum [approximately 0.25 hectares], with manure mixed into the earth to a depth of three feet. The ground should also be divided into plots or beds with raised and rounded edges, each of which should have a path dug around it, providing the gardener with access and forming a channel for the water required for irrigation. CHAPTER 21. PLANTS OTHER THAN GRAIN AND SHRUBS. Among garden plants, some recommend themselves by their bulbs, others by the head, others by the stalk, others by the leaf, and others by both stalk and leaf. Some are valued for their seed, others for their outer coat, others for their membranous tissues, others for their cartilaginous substance, others for the firmness of their flesh, and others for the fleshy tunics in which they are enclosed. CHAPTER 22. THE NATURAL HISTORY OF TWENTY DIFFERENT KINDS OF PLANTS GROWN IN GARDENS, AND THE PROPER METHODS TO BE FOLLOWED IN SOWING EACH OF THEM. The fruits of some plants lie in the earth, those of others both in and out of the earth, and those of others entirely out of the earth. Some increase as they spread along the ground — gourds and cucumbers, for instance; these same plants will also grow in a suspended position, but even then their fruits are considerably heavier than any fruit borne on trees. The cucumber, however, is composed of cartilage and a fleshy substance.

entities_mentioned.name: tolleno (swipe / counterbalanced beam)

results.title_inferred: Pliny on Cucumbers, Pepones, and Melopepos: Varieties, Properties, and Cultivation
results.original_text: growing, may be trained to take any form that may be wished: in Italy the cucumbers are green and very small, while those grown in some of the provinces are remarkably large, and of a wax colour or black. Those of Africa, which are also remarkably prolific, are held in high esteem; the same, too, with the cucumbers of Moesia, which are by far the largest of all. When the cucumber acquires a very considerable volume, it is known to us as the 'pepo.' Cucumbers when eaten remain on the stomach till the following day, and are very difficult of digestion; still, for all that, in general they are not considered very unwholesome. By nature they have a wonderful hatred to oil, and no less affection for water, and this after they have been cut from the stem even. If water is within a moderate distance of them, they will creep towards it, while from oil, on the other hand, they will shrink away: if any obstacle, too, should happen to arrest their progress, or if they are left to hang, they will grow curved and crooked. Of these facts we may be satisfactorily convinced in a single night even, for if a vessel filled with water is placed at four fingers' distance from a cucumber, it will be found to have descended to it by the following morning; but if the same is done with oil, it will have assumed the curved form of a hook by the next day. If hung in a tube while in blossom, the cucumber will grow to a most surprising length. It is only of late, too, that a cucumber of entirely new shape has been produced in Campania, it having just the form of a quince. It was quite by accident, I am told, that the first one acquired this shape in growing, and it was from the seed of this that all the others have been reproduced. The name given to this variety is 'melopepo.' These last do not grow hanging, but assume their round shape as they lie on the ground. A thing that is very remarkable in them, in addition to their shape, colour, and smell, is the fact that, when ripe, although they do not hang from the stem, they separate from it at the stalk. Columella has given us a plan of his, by which we may have cucumbers the whole year round: the largest bramble-bush that can be procured is transplanted to a warm, sunny spot, and then cut down, about the time of the vernal equinox, to within a couple of fingers of the ground; a cucumber-seed is then inserted in the pith of the bramble, and the roots are well moulded up with fine earth and manure, to withstand the cold. According to the Greeks, there are three kinds of cucumbers, the La[conian...]

In Italy the cucumbers are green and very small, while those grown in some of the provinces are remarkably large, and of a wax colour or black. Those of Africa, which are also remarkably prolific, are held in high esteem; and likewise the cucumbers of Moesia, which are by far the largest of all. When the cucumber acquires a very considerable volume, it is known to us as the 'pepo' [pompion or pumpkin]. Cucumbers when eaten remain on the stomach until the following day and are very difficult of digestion; yet for all that, they are not in general considered very unwholesome. By nature they have a wonderful aversion to oil and no less an affinity for water, and this even after they have been cut from the stem. If water is within a moderate distance of them, they will creep towards it, while from oil they will shrink away; and if any obstacle should happen to arrest their progress, or if they are left to hang, they will grow curved and crooked. Of these facts one may be satisfactorily convinced in a single night, for if a vessel filled with water is placed four fingers' breadth from a cucumber, it will be found to have reached it by the following morning; but if the same is done with oil, it will have assumed the curved form of a hook by the next day. If hung in a tube while in blossom, the cucumber will grow to a most surprising length. It is also only recently that a cucumber of an entirely new shape has been produced in Campania, having just the form of a quince. It was quite by accident, I am told, that the first one acquired this shape in growing, and it is from the seed of this that all the others have been reproduced. The name given to this variety is 'melopepo.' These last do not grow hanging but assume their round shape as they lie on the ground. A thing that is very remarkable in them, in addition to their shape, colour, and smell, is the fact that when ripe, although they do not hang from the stem, they separate from it at the stalk. Columella has given us a method by which we may have cucumbers the whole year round: the largest bramble-bush that can be procured is transplanted to a warm, sunny spot, and then cut down, about the time of the vernal equinox, to within a couple of fingers of the ground; a cucumber-seed is then inserted in the pith of the bramble, and the roots are well mounded up with fine earth and manure to withstand the cold. According to the Greeks, there are three kinds of cucumbers—the La[conian, and others, text incomplete]...

results.translator_notes: This segment is the existing English translation of Pliny's Natural History (Book XIX), not a foreign-language source requiring fresh translation; it is the Bostock & Riley version with editorial footnotes embedded. The text has been cleaned of OCR artifacts ('PLIKY'S' corrected to 'PLINY'S', 'Mossia' corrected to 'Moesia', 'morn-ing' reunited). The text ends mid-sentence ('the La-') indicating the chunk is cut off; likely the next chunk continues with 'Laconian' cucumber varieties. Footnote numbers are retained in the cleaned original for scholarly reference. The segment contains no distinct foreign-language source text—it is already in English; translation is therefore a faithful rendering of the cleaned text. Medical/digestive claims (cucumbers difficult to digest; aversion to oil) are noted as folk beliefs critiqued even in Pliny's own footnotes. No preparation method for medicinal use is described in this segment; cucumbers are discussed primarily as food and horticultural subjects.

results.title_inferred: On Gourds and Cucumbers: Cultivation, Varieties, and Growth Habits
results.original_text: the produce, they say, will be sure to grow without seeds. CHAP. 24. — GOURDS. Gourds resemble the cucumber in nature, at least in their manner of growing; they manifest an equal aversion to the winter, too, while they require constant watering and manure. Both cucumbers and gourds are sown in holes a foot and a half deep, between the vernal equinox and the summer solstice, at the time of the Parilia or more particularly. Some persons, however, think it better to sow gourds after the calends of March, and cucumbers after the nones, and at the time of the Quinquatria. The cucumber and the gourd climb upwards in a precisely similar manner, their shoots creeping along the rough surface of the walls, even to the very roof, so great is their fondness for elevated spots. They have not sufficient strength, however, to support themselves without the aid of stays. Shooting upwards with the greatest rapidity, they soon cover with their light shade the arched roofs of the houses and the trellises on which they are trained. From this circumstance it is that we find the gourd classified into two primary kinds, the roof-gourd, and the common gourd, which creeps upon the ground. In the first kind, from a stalk of remarkable thinness is suspended a fruit of considerable weight and volume, and quite immoveable by the action of the wind. The gourd, too, as well as the cucumber, admits of being lengthened to any extent, by the aid of osier tubes more particularly. Just after the blossom has fallen off, the plant is introduced into these tubes, and as it grows it can be made to assume any form that may be wished, that of a serpent coiled up being the one that is mostly preferred; if left at liberty to grow as it hangs, it has been known before now to attain to no less than nine feet in length. The cucumber flowers gradually, blossom succeeding blossom; and it adapts itself perfectly well to a dry soil.
results.translation: The produce, they say, will be sure to grow without seeds. CHAPTER 24. — GOURDS. Gourds resemble the cucumber in nature, at least in their manner of growing; they manifest an equal aversion to winter, and similarly require constant watering and manuring. Both cucumbers and gourds are sown in holes a foot and a half deep, between the vernal equinox and the summer solstice, most particularly at the time of the Parilia [a festival celebrated on the nineteenth of April, the anniversary of the founding of Rome]. Some persons, however, think it better to sow gourds after the Calends of March [the first of March], and cucumbers after the Nones [the seventh of March], at the time of the Quinquatria. The cucumber and the gourd climb upwards in a precisely similar manner, their shoots creeping along the rough surfaces of walls, even up to the very roof, so great is their tendency toward elevated positions. They lack sufficient strength, however, to support themselves without the aid of props. Shooting upward with the greatest rapidity, they soon cover with their light shade the arched roofs of houses and the trellises on which they are trained. It is from this circumstance that the gourd is classified into two primary kinds: the roof-gourd [camerarium], and the common gourd [plebeium], which creeps upon the ground. In the first kind, from a stalk of remarkable thinness is suspended a fruit of considerable weight and volume, quite unmoved by the action of the wind. The gourd, like the cucumber, may also be made to grow to any desired length, most effectively by means of osier [willow-wicker] tubes. Just after the blossom has fallen off, the young fruit is inserted into these tubes, and as it grows it can be made to assume any form desired, the shape of a coiled serpent being the most commonly preferred; if left free to grow as it hangs, the fruit has been known to attain no less than nine feet in length. The cucumber flowers gradually, blossom succeeding blossom, and it adapts perfectly well to a dry soil.

entities_mentioned.notes: Fee identifies the 'camerarium' (roof-gourd) with the long gourd and related calabash varieties. The term 'cucurbita' in Pliny may extend to the citrul or small pumpkin as well.

entities_mentioned.name: Cucumber

results.translator_notes: This segment is the source text of Pliny's Natural History already in English (the original Latin has been previously translated), accompanied by editorial footnotes. The 'original_text' field therefore reproduces the English source as cleaned-up primary text. Key footnote identifications by Fee (a 19th-century commentator) and references to Theophrastus and Columella have been incorporated where relevant. The note that a foot-and-a-half planting depth would likely retard or prevent growth reflects modern horticultural skepticism of Pliny's recommendation. The claim that gourd fruit can reach nine feet in length is considered by Fee to refer to the fruit specifically, not the plant, which can grow considerably longer.

results.title_inferred: On Gourds, Cucumbers, Rape, and Turnips
results.original_text:
to be used in baths for jugs and pitchers, but for this long time past they have been employed as casks for keeping wine. The rind is tender while the fruit is green, but still it is always scraped off when the gourd is used for food. It admits of being eaten several ways, and forms a light and wholesome aliment, and this although it is one of those fruits that are difficult of digestion by the human stomach, and are apt to swell out those who eat of them. The seeds which lie nearest to the neck of the gourd produce fruit of remarkable length, and so do those which lie at the lower extremities, though not at all comparable with the others. Those, on the other hand, which lie in the middle, produce gourds of a round shape, and those on the sides fruit that are thick and short. The seeds are dried by being placed in the shade, and when wanted for sowing, are steeped in water first. The longer and thinner the gourd is, the more agreeable it is to the palate, and hence it is that those which have been left to grow hanging are reckoned the most wholesome: these, too, have fewer seeds than the others, the hardness of which is apt to render the fruit less agreeable for eating. Those which are intended for keeping seed, are usually not cut before the winter sets in; they are then dried in the smoke, and are extensively employed for preserving garden seeds, and for making other articles for domestic use. There has been a method discovered, also, of preserving the gourd for table, and the cucumber as well, till nearly the time when the next year's crop is ripe; this is done by putting them in brine. We are assured, too, that if put in a hole dug in a place well shaded from the sun, with a layer of sand beneath, and dry hay and earth on the top of them, they may be kept green for a very long time. We also find wild cucumbers and gourds; and, indeed, the same is the case with pretty nearly all the garden plants. These wild varieties, however, are only possessed of certain medicinal properties, and for this reason we shall defer any further mention of them till we come to the Books appropriated to that subject.

CHAP. 25. RAPE. TURNIPS.

The other plants that are of a cartilaginous nature are concealed, all of them, in the earth. In the number of these is the rape, a subject upon which it would almost appear that we have treated at sufficient length already, were it not that we think it as well to observe, that medical men call those which are round "male," while those which are larger and more elongated, are known to them as "female" rape: these last are superior in sweetness, and better for keeping, but by successive sowings they are changed into male rape. The same authors, too, have distinguished five different varieties of the turnip: the Corinthian, the Cleonaean, the Liothasian, the Boeotian, and the one which they have characterized as peculiarly the "green" turnip. The Corinthian turnip grows to a very large size,

to be used in baths as jugs and pitchers, but for a long time now they have been employed as casks [dolia] for keeping wine. The rind is tender while the fruit is green, but it is always scraped off when the gourd is used for food. It may be eaten in several ways, and forms a light and wholesome nourishment [alimentum], though it is one of those fruits difficult of digestion by the human stomach and apt to cause bloating in those who eat it. The seeds lying nearest to the neck of the gourd produce fruit of remarkable length, as do those at the lower extremities, though these are not comparable with the former. Those lying in the middle, on the other hand, produce gourds of a round shape, and those at the sides produce fruit that is thick and short. The seeds are dried in the shade, and when needed for sowing are first steeped in water. The longer and thinner the gourd, the more agreeable it is to the palate, and hence those that have been allowed to grow hanging are considered the most wholesome; these also have fewer seeds than the others, the hardness of which tends to render the fruit less pleasant for eating. Those intended for keeping seed are usually not cut before the onset of winter; they are then dried in smoke and are extensively used for preserving garden seeds and for making various domestic articles. A method has also been discovered for keeping the gourd and the cucumber at table until nearly the time when the following year's crop is ripe; this is accomplished by placing them in brine [muria]. It is also reported that if placed in a hole dug in a spot well shaded from the sun, with a layer of sand beneath and dry hay and earth placed over them, they may be kept green for a very long time. Wild cucumbers and gourds are also found; and indeed the same is true of nearly all garden plants. These wild varieties, however, possess only certain medicinal properties, and for this reason further mention of them will be deferred until the Books devoted to that subject.

CHAPTER 25. RAPE. TURNIPS.

The other plants of a cartilaginous [cartilaginosus] nature are all concealed within the earth. Among these is the rape [rapa], a subject upon which it might appear we have already treated at sufficient length, were it not useful to note that medical men call those which are round "male" [mas], while those which are larger and more elongated are known to them as "female" [femina] rape; the latter are superior in sweetness and better for keeping, though through successive sowings they revert to male rape. The same authorities have also distinguished five varieties of the turnip [napus]: the Corinthian, the Cleonaean, the Liothasian, the Boeotian, and that which they have characterized as particularly the "green" turnip. The Corinthian turnip grows to a very large size,

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny's Naturalis Historia, Book XIX (or nearby), covering cucurbits and root vegetables. Footnotes embedded in the OCR'd source text (numbered 73–82) have been silently integrated as contextual notes rather than disrupting the main text flow. The footnote text attributing botanical identifications to 'Fee' refers to Antoine Laurent Apollinaire Fée, the 19th-century French botanist who annotated Pliny. The segment ends mid-sentence ('The Corinthian turnip grows to a very large size,'), indicating a continuation in the following chunk. The word 'rape' here refers to Brassica rapa and related species, not the oilseed crop exclusively; period usage retained per rules.

results.title_inferred: On Turnips and Radishes: Varieties, Properties, and Uses
results.original_text:
stands extreme cold the best of all. Next to it, the Boeotian kind is the sweetest; it is remarkable, also, for the roundness of its shape and its shortness; while the Cleonaean turnip, on the other hand, is of an elongated form. Those, in general, which have a thin, smooth leaf, are the sweetest; while those, again, the leaf of which is rough, angular, and prickly, have a pungent taste. There is a kind of wild turnip, also, the leaves of which resemble those of rocket. At Rome, the highest rank is given to the turnips of Amiternum, and those of Nursia; after them, those grown in the neighbourhood of the City are held in the next degree of esteem. The other particulars connected with the sowing of the turnip have been already mentioned by us when speaking of the rape.

CHAP. 26. RADISHES.

Radishes are composed of an outer coat and a cartilaginous substance, and in many instances the rind is found to be thicker than the bark of some trees. This plant is remarkable for its pungency, which increases in proportion to the thickness of the rind: in some cases, too, the surface of it assumes a ligneous nature. Radishes are flatulent to a remarkable degree, and are productive of eructations; hence it is that they are looked upon as an aliment only fit for low-bred people, and this more particularly if cole worts are eaten directly after them. If, on the other hand, they are eaten with green olives, the eructations produced are not so frequent, and less offensive. In Egypt the radish is held in very high esteem, on account of the abundance of oil that is extracted from the seed. Indeed, the people of that country sow this plant in preference to any other, whenever they can get the opportunity, the profits derived from it being very considerable.
results.translation:
This variety [of turnip] withstands extreme cold best of all. Next to it, the Boeotian kind is the sweetest; it is also notable for the roundness of its shape and its shortness; while the Cleonaean turnip, on the other hand, is of an elongated form. Those varieties whose leaves are thin and smooth are, in general, the sweetest; those whose leaves are rough, angular, and prickly have a pungent taste. There is also a kind of wild turnip [or wild radish] whose leaves resemble those of rocket [arugula]. At Rome, the highest esteem is given to the turnips of Amiternum and those of Nursia; after these, those grown in the vicinity of the City are held in the next degree of regard. The other particulars concerning the sowing of the turnip have already been mentioned when we spoke of the rape.

CHAPTER 26. RADISHES.

Radishes are composed of an outer skin and a cartilaginous inner substance, and in many cases the rind is found to be thicker than the bark of certain trees. This plant is remarkable for its pungency, which increases in proportion to the thickness of its rind; in some cases, furthermore, the surface takes on a woody [ligneous] character. Radishes are remarkably flatulent and cause eructations [belching]; hence they are regarded as a food fit only for common or low-bred people [cibus illiberalis], and this is especially so when cole worts [colewort/cabbage] are eaten directly after them. If, on the other hand, they are eaten together with green olives, the eructations produced are less frequent and less offensive. In Egypt the radish is held in very high esteem on account of the abundance of oil extracted from its seed. Indeed, the Egyptians sow this plant in preference to almost any other whenever opportunity permits, the profits derived from it being considerable.

entities_mentioned.name: Boeotian turnip
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as round, short, and notably sweet; a regional variety from Boeotia in central Greece.

entities_mentioned.name: Cleonaean turnip
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica rapa (turnip, Cleonaean variety) or possibly Raphanus sativus
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as elongated in form. Cleona was a town in the northeastern Peloponnese. The footnote in the source text suggests this may actually refer to a radish rather than a true turnip.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild turnip (leaves resembling rocket)
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote in source text identifies this as properly a radish and suggests possible identification with Raphanus raphanistrum of botanists.

entities_mentioned.name: Turnip of Amiternum
entities_mentioned.notes: Amiternum was an ancient Sabine town in central Italy (near modern L'Aquila); its turnips were the most prized at Rome according to Pliny.

entities_mentioned.name: Turnip of Nursia
entities_mentioned.notes: Nursia is modern Norcia in Umbria, Italy; its turnips ranked second in esteem at Rome.

entities_mentioned.name: Rocket
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the leaf shape of the wild turnip/radish. Discussed more fully in Pliny Book XX, c. 49.

entities_mentioned.name: Radish (raphanus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as composed of outer skin and cartilaginous interior, noted for pungency, flatulence, and eructations. The Egyptian oil-producing variety is identified in the source footnote as Raphanus sativus var. Oleifera, still cultivated in Egypt and Nubia for oil extraction.

entities_mentioned.name: Cole worts
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a food that, when eaten directly after radishes, worsens flatulence and eructations.

entities_mentioned.name: Green olives
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended to be eaten with radishes to reduce the frequency and offensiveness of eructations.

entities_mentioned.name: Egyptian radish (oil variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivated extensively in Egypt for its seed oil. Source footnote notes possible confusion by Pliny with Brassica napus var. Oleifera, also grown in Egypt. Comparable to Colza oil (from Brassica oleracea) used for lamp oil in France and Belgium.

entities_mentioned.name: Rape
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced by Pliny as treated earlier (Book XVIII, c. 34–35) in the context of turnip cultivation.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 25–26, dealing with turnips (rapa/napus) and radishes (raphanus). The source text includes extensive editorial footnotes from the translator (likely John Bostock and H.T. Riley, mid-19th century edition), which have been silently separated from Pliny's own text in the translation above. Several editorial notes flag inaccuracies in Pliny: (1) the claim that repeated sowing of round turnip seed produces long roots is unfounded and apparently a misreading of Theophrastus; (2) Pliny's discussion of 'wild turnip' likely refers to radishes (Raphanus) rather than true turnips (Brassica rapa); (3) the distinction between turnip varieties borrowed from Theophrastus is described as 'absurd and unfounded.' The medical/dietary content describes radishes as causing flatulence and eructations, making them food fit only for common people [cibus illiberalis], and notes that eating them with green olives mitigates these effects — a dietary/gastrointestinal observation rather than a therapeutic claim. The Egyptian use of radish seed oil is a significant economic-botanical note. The term 'ligneous' [lignosus] preserves a technical descriptor for the woody texture sometimes found on radish surfaces.

Our own people, again, have found other varieties of the radish: there is the Algidan radish, long and transparent, so called from the place of its growth: another, similar to the rape in form, is known as the Syrian radish; it is pretty nearly the mildest and the most tender of them all, and is well able to bear the winter. results.translation: The leaf of this [last] variety is smooth, but shorter than that of the others; it is also round, grows in great abundance, and spreads like a shrub. The taste of this last variety is acrid [pungent], and it acts medicinally as a strong purgative [laxative]. In the first kind, again, there are certain differences determined by the seed: in some varieties the seed is of inferior quality, and in others remarkably small; these defects, however, are found only in the kind that has the crisped [curly] leaf. Our own people have moreover found other varieties of the radish: there is the Algidan radish—long and transparent, so named from the place of its growth [Mount Algidus, near Tusculum, some fifteen miles from Rome]; another, similar in form to the rape [turnip], is known as the Syrian radish; it is very nearly the mildest and most tender of them all, and bears the winter well. The very best of all, however, is the one recently imported from Syria—so recently, it seems, that we find it mentioned by none of our earlier writers—and it keeps through the whole of the winter. Beyond these varieties there is yet another, a wild kind, called by the Greeks "agrion" [i.e., wild], by the people of Pontus "armon," by others "leuce" [i.e., white, from the extreme whiteness of its roots], and by our own people "armoracia" [horseradish; probably meaning 'radish of Armorica']; this plant has more leaf, however, than root. In judging the quality of the radish, it is above all the stem [stalk] that is examined: in those varieties that are acrid to the taste, the stem is rounder and thicker than in the others, and furrowed with long channels, while the leaves are more unsightly to the eye, being angular and covered with prickles. The radish requires a loose, humid soil; it has a great aversion to dung-manure and is content with a dressing of chaff alone. So fond is it of cold that in Germany it is known to grow as large as an infant. For the spring crop it is sown immediately after the Ides of February, and again about the time of the Vulcanalia [festival of Vulcan, 23 August], this latter crop being regarded as the best; many persons, however, sow radishes in March, April, and September. As the plant grows to any size, it is considered good practice to draw the leaves successively aside and to earth up the root as well, since the portion left above ground is apt to become hard and pithy. Aristomachus recommends removing the leaves in winter and heaping the soil well up around the roots to prevent water from collecting about them; by these precautions, he maintains, the roots will be all the finer in summer. Some writers describe a method of boring a hole with a dibble and lining the bottom with a layer of chaff six fingers deep; upon this the seed is placed and then covered with manure and earth; the result of which is [text continues in next segment].

entities_mentioned.name: Radish (general)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's primary subject throughout this passage. He notes that the translator's footnote flags a textual confusion: the Greek word ῥάφανος properly means 'cabbage' in classical usage but was taken by Pliny to mean 'radish,' which it does in later Greek writers.

entities_mentioned.name: Algidan radish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raphanus sativus var. (local cultivar from Mount Algidus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Long, transparent variety named after Mount Algidus near Tusculum; the cold climate of that locality was credited with its quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Syrian radish
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as rape-shaped [Brassica rapa-like in form], mild, tender, and winter-hardy. A second, superior Syrian introduction is also mentioned as keeping through the entire winter.

entities_mentioned.name: Armoracia / agrion / armon / leuce (wild radish / horseradish)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Armoracia rusticana (Lam.) Gaertn., Mey. & Scherb. [horseradish]; alternatively Raphanus raphanistrum L. [wild radish]
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'agrion' (Greek: wild), 'armon' (Pontic), 'leuce' (white, from root color), and 'armoracia' (Latin, possibly from Armorica). Footnote in source suggests identification with Raphanus rusticanus of Lobellius = Cochlearia Armoracia of Linnaeus (now Armoracia rusticana), i.e., horseradish. Has more leaf than root.

entities_mentioned.name: Acrid/purgative radish variety
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raphanus sativus var. or possibly Raphanus raphanistrum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as shrub-like, with smooth but short leaves; taste acrid; used medicinally as a strong purgative [cathartic]. Preparation method not specified beyond consumption.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a direct translation of Pliny, Historia Naturalis, Book XIX, on radishes. The source text already appears in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock & Riley); the task here is to produce a clean, scholarly English rendering preserving technical and period vocabulary. Key editorial issues: (1) Pliny's notorious conflation of ῥάφανος ('cabbage' in classical Greek) with 'radish' is flagged in the original footnotes and preserved here. (2) The segment ends mid-sentence ('the result of which is'), indicating continuation in the next chunk. (3) 'Vulcanalia' is the Roman festival of Vulcan, 23 August, used as a calendrical marker for the second radish sowing. (4) 'Armoracia' is glossed in the original footnote as possibly derived from Armorica (Brittany/NW Gaul), though this etymology is uncertain. (5) The medicinal use extracted: acrid variety of radish used as a strong purgative; no specific preparation method is described beyond implied oral ingestion.

results.title_inferred: Radishes, Parsnips, and Their Medical and Culinary Uses (Pliny, Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 26–27)
results.original_text: over them, the roots being remarkable for their mildness. The salt, too, has the similar effect of removing all their pungency, and when thus treated, they become very similar in their qualities to radishes that have been boiled: for when boiled they become sweet and mild, and eat, in fact, just like turnips. Medical men recommend raw radishes to be eaten fasting, with salt, for the purpose of collecting the crude humours of the viscera; and in this way they prepare them for the action of emetics. It is said, too, that the juices of this plant are absolutely necessary for the cure of certain diseases of the diaphragm; for it has been found by experiment, in Egypt, that the phthiriasis which attaches itself to the internal parts of the heart, cannot possibly be eradicated by any other remedy, the kings of that country having ordered the bodies of the dead to be opened and examined, for the purpose of enquiring into certain diseases. Such, too, is the frivolity of the Greeks, that, in the temple of Apollo at Delphi, it is said, the radish is so greatly preferred to all other articles of diet, as to be represented there in gold, the beet in silver, and the rape in lead. You might be very sure that Manius Curius was not a native of that country, the general whom, as we find stated in our Annals, the ambassadors of the Samnites found busy roasting rape at the fire, when they came to offer him the gold which he so indignantly refused. Moschion, too, a Greek author, has written a volume on the subject of the radish. These vegetables are considered a very useful article of food during the winter, but they are at all times very injurious to the teeth, as they are apt to wear them away; at all events, they give a polish to ivory. There is a great antipathy between the radish and the vine; which last will shrink from the radish, if sown in its vicinity. CHAP. 27. — PARSNIPS. Among these, there is one kind of wild parsnip which grows spontaneously; by the Greeks it is known as "staphylinos." Another kind of parsnip is grown either from the root transplanted, or else from seed, at the beginning of spring or in the autumn; Hyginus says that this may be done in February, August, September, and October, the ground being dug to a [depth].

over them, the roots being remarkable for their mildness. Salt likewise has the similar effect of removing all their pungency, and when thus treated they become very similar in their qualities to radishes that have been boiled; for when boiled, radishes become sweet and mild, and taste, in fact, just like turnips.

Medical men recommend that raw radishes be eaten fasting, with salt, for the purpose of collecting the crude humours [undigested or phlegmatic matter] of the viscera; and in this way they prepare the stomach for the action of emetics. It is said, moreover, that the juices of this plant are absolutely necessary for the cure of certain diseases of the diaphragm; for it has been found by experiment in Egypt that phthiriasis [morbus pediculosus, i.e., louse-disease] which attaches itself to the internal parts of the heart cannot possibly be eradicated by any other remedy, the kings of that country having ordered the bodies of the dead to be opened and examined for the purpose of investigating certain diseases. Such, too, is the frivolity of the Greeks, that in the temple of Apollo at Delphi it is said the radish is so greatly preferred above all other articles of diet as to be represented there in gold, the beet in silver, and the rape in lead. You might be quite sure that Manius Curius was not a native of that country — the general whom, as we find recorded in our Annals, the ambassadors of the Samnites found busy roasting rape at the fire when they came to offer him the gold which he so indignantly refused. Moschion, too, a Greek author, has written an entire volume on the subject of the radish. These vegetables are considered a very useful article of food during winter, but they are at all times very injurious to the teeth, being apt to wear them away; at all events, they give a polish to ivory. There is a great antipathy between the radish and the vine; the vine will shrink away from the radish if it is sown in its vicinity.

CHAPTER 27. — PARSNIPS.

The other kinds which we have classified among the cartilaginous plants are of a more ligneous [woody] nature; and it is a singular thing that they all have a strong flavour. Among these, there is one kind of wild parsnip that grows spontaneously; by the Greeks it is known as "staphylinos" [Daucus carota, wild carrot or wild parsnip]. Another kind of parsnip is cultivated either from the root transplanted, or else from seed, at the beginning of spring or in the autumn; Hyginus states that this may be done in February, August, September, and October, the ground being dug to a [considerable depth].

entities_mentioned.name: Radish
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally: eaten raw and fasting with salt to collect crude humours [phlegm/undigested matter] of the viscera and prepare for emetics; juice used for diseases of the diaphragm and for phthiriasis of the heart.

entities_mentioned.name: Beet
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the context of the temple of Apollo at Delphi, where it was represented in silver. Footnote 98 suggests Pliny may be speaking of beet-root as a native of northern Europe.

entities_mentioned.name: Rape / Turnip
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as represented in lead at Delphi; also the vegetable being roasted by Manius Curius when visited by Samnite ambassadors.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild parsnip / Staphylinos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daucus carota (wild carrot) or Pastinaca sativa (wild parsnip)
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek name 'staphylinos' is used in Dioscorides for wild carrot (Daucus carota); the cultivated form may refer to Pastinaca sativa.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron (salt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a natural production (carbonate of sodium), used to remove pungency from root vegetables and for watering leguminous plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Phthiriasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient belief that this condition could affect internal organs, specifically the heart; radish juice was believed to penetrate the organ and eradicate it.

results.translator_notes: This segment is taken from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, transitioning from Chapter 26 (radishes) to Chapter 27 (parsnips). The text as received appears to be a 19th-century English translation (likely Bostock & Riley) with editorial footnotes embedded in the body; these have been retained in the cleaned original and translated text for archival completeness. The claim about phthiriasis of the heart reflects ancient Graeco-Egyptian medical belief rather than sound anatomy. The antipathy between the radish and vine attributed elsewhere to the cabbage-vine relationship (see Book XXIV) is noted by the original translator. 'Staphylinos' is the Greek term used by Dioscorides for wild carrot/parsnip and its identification as Daucus carota vs. Pastinaca sativa remains debated by classical botanists.

results.title_inferred: Hibiscum, Skirret, and Elecampane: Roots and Their Medicinal and Culinary Uses
results.original_text:
is found quite impossible to get rid of. The hibiscum differs from the parsnip in being more slender: it is rejected as a food, but is found useful for its medicinal properties. There is a fourth kind, also, which bears a similar degree of resemblance to the parsnip; by our people it is called the "gallica," while the Greeks, who have distinguished four varieties of it, give it the name of "daucus." We shall have further occasion to mention it among the medicinal plants.

CHAP. 28. THE SKIRRET.
The skirret, too, has had its reputation established by the Emperor Tiberius, who demanded a supply of it every year from Germany. It is at Gelduba, a fortress situate on the banks of the Rhenus, that the finest are grown; from which it would appear that they thrive best in a cold climate. There is a string running through the whole length of the skirret, and which is drawn out after it is boiled; but still, for all this, a considerable proportion of its natural pungency is retained; indeed, when modified by the addition of honied wine, this is even thought to impart to dishes an additional relish. The larger parsnip has also a similar sting inside, but only when it is a year old. The proper time for sowing the skirret is in the months of February, March, April, August, September, and October.

CHAP. 29. ELECAMPANE.
Elecampane is not so elongated as the preceding roots, but more substantial and more pungent; eaten by itself it is very injurious to the stomach, but when mixed with other condiments of a sweet nature, it is extremely wholesome. There are several methods employed for modifying its natural acridity and rendering it agreeable to the palate: thus, for instance, when dried it is reduced to a fine flour, and then mixed with some sweet liquid or other, or else it is boiled in vinegar and water, or kept in soak in it; it is also steeped in various other ways, and then mixed with boiled grape-juice, or else incorporated with honey or raisins, or dates with plenty of meat on them. Other persons, again, have a method of preparing it with quinces, or else sorbs or plums, while sometimes the flavour is varied by the addition of pepper or thyme. This plant is particularly good for weakness of the stomach, and it has acquired a high reputation from the circumstance that Julia Augusta used to eat it daily. The seed of it is quite useless, as the plant is reproduced, like the reed, from eyes extracted from the root. This vegetable, as well as the skirret and the parsnip, is sown both in spring and autumn, a considerable distance being left between the plants; indeed, for elecampane, a space of no less than three feet is required, as [text ends mid-sentence; cf. Inula Helenium].
results.translation:
…[the pungency] is found quite impossible to get rid of. The hibiscum [marsh-mallow, probably Althaea officinalis] differs from the parsnip in being more slender: it is rejected as a food, but is found useful for its medicinal properties. There is a fourth kind as well, which bears a similar degree of resemblance to the parsnip; our people call it the 'gallica,' while the Greeks, who have distinguished four varieties of it, give it the name 'daucus' [carrot]. We shall have further occasion to mention it among the medicinal plants.

CHAPTER 28. THE SKIRRET.
The skirret [Sium sisarum], too, has had its reputation established by the Emperor Tiberius, who demanded a supply of it every year from Germany. It is at Gelduba, a fortress situated on the banks of the Rhine, that the finest specimens are grown; from which it would appear that they thrive best in a cold climate. There is a fibrous cord running through the whole length of the skirret, which is drawn out after the root is boiled; but even so, a considerable proportion of its natural pungency is retained. Indeed, when this pungency is tempered by the addition of honeyed wine [mulsum], it is thought to impart an additional relish to dishes. The larger parsnip has a similar sharpness inside as well, but only when it is a year old. The proper times for sowing the skirret are the months of February, March, April, August, September, and October.

CHAPTER 29. ELECAMPANE.
Elecampane [Inula helenium] is not as elongated as the roots described above, but is more substantial and more pungent. Eaten on its own, it is very injurious to the stomach, but when mixed with other condiments of a sweet nature it is extremely wholesome. Several methods are employed to moderate its natural acridity and render it agreeable to the palate: for instance, when dried it is ground to a fine flour and then mixed with some sweet liquid; or else it is boiled in vinegar and water, or kept steeped therein; it is also prepared by steeping in various other ways and then mixed with boiled grape-juice [defrutum], or incorporated with honey, raisins, or well-fleshed dates. Others prepare it with quinces, or with sorb-apples [service berries] or plums, while sometimes the flavour is varied by the addition of pepper or thyme. This plant is particularly beneficial for weakness of the stomach, and it has acquired great renown from the fact that Julia Augusta [Livia] was accustomed to eat it daily. Its seed is quite useless for propagation, as the plant is reproduced—like the reed—from buds [eyes] extracted from the root. This vegetable, as well as the skirret and the parsnip, is sown both in spring and in autumn, with considerable spacing between plants; for elecampane, an interval of no less than three feet is required, as… [passage breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: hibiscum
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny distinguishes it from the parsnip by its more slender form; rejected as food but valued medicinally. Footnote in source suggests probable identification as marsh-mallow.

entities_mentioned.name: daucus / gallica
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'gallica' by Romans, 'daucus' by Greeks. Pliny notes four Greek varieties. Source footnotes identify it as Daucus carota of Linnaeus.

entities_mentioned.name: skirret (siser)
entities_mentioned.notes: Valued by Emperor Tiberius; finest grown at Gelduba on the Rhine. Has a central fibrous string removed after boiling. Said to be originally native to China. Sown February–April and August–October.

entities_mentioned.name: elecampane
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated condition: weakness of the stomach. Preparation methods: dried and ground to flour mixed with sweet liquid; boiled or steeped in vinegar and water; mixed with defrutum (boiled grape-juice), honey, raisins, dates, quinces, sorbs, or plums; seasoned with pepper or thyme. Notably consumed daily by Julia Augusta (Livia). Propagated from root eyes, not seed. Requires 3-foot spacing when cultivated.

entities_mentioned.name: parsnip (pastinaca)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced for comparison with hibiscum and daucus; noted to develop internal sharpness when one year old.

entities_mentioned.name: Julia Augusta
entities_mentioned.notes: Livia Drusilla, wife of Augustus, cited as daily consumer of elecampane, lending the plant its medicinal reputation.

results.translator_notes: The source text is an English translation (likely Holland's or Bostock & Riley's) of Pliny's Naturalis Historia, not a Latin original; the task here is therefore cleaning and annotating rather than translating from Latin. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence with 'Inula Hel-' (evidently 'Inula Helenium'), which is a chunk boundary artifact. The word 'Ehenus' in the OCR source is corrected to 'Rhenus' (Rhine). 'Honied wine' is mulsum. 'Boiled grape-juice' is defrutum. 'Sorbs' are the fruit of Sorbus domestica (service tree). 'Eyes' refers to vegetative buds on the root used for propagation. Gelduba is identified in the footnotes as likely modern Gelb near Neuss, Germany (cf. Tacitus, Historiae IV.26, 32).

results.title_inferred: On Bulbs, Squills, and Arum (Pliny Natural History, Book XIX, Ch. 30)
results.original_text:
being pickled in brine, or else in the juice of rue, which, as Fee remarks, would produce neither more nor less than a veritable poison. The modern Pharmacopoeias give the receipt of a conserve of elecampane, which, however, is no longer used. [Note 15: "Defrutum." Must, boiled down to one half.] [Note 17: The daughter of Augustus Caesar.]

It throws out its shoots to a very considerable distance. Skirrets, however, are best transplanted.

CHAP. 30. — BULBS, SQUILLS, AND ARUM.

Next in affinity to these plants are the bulbs, which Cato, speaking in high terms of those of Megara, recommends most particularly for cultivation. Among these bulbs, the squill, we find, occupies the very highest rank, although by nature it is medicinal, and is employed for imparting an additional sharpness to vinegar; indeed, there is no bulb known that grows to a larger size than this, or is possessed of a greater degree of pungency. There are two varieties of it employed in medicine, the male squill, which has white leaves, and the female squill, with black ones. There is a third kind also, which is good to eat, and is known as the Epimenidian squill; the leaf is narrower than in the other kinds, and not so rough. All the squills have numerous seeds, but they come up much more quickly if propagated from the offsets that grow on the sides. To make them attain a still greater size, the large leaves that grow around them are turned down and covered over with earth; by which method all the juices are carried to the heads. Squills grow spontaneously and in vast numbers in the Baleares and the island of Ebusus, and in the Spanish provinces. The philosopher Pythagoras has written a whole volume on the merits of this plant, setting forth its various medicinal properties; of which we shall have occasion to speak more at length in the succeeding Book.

The other species of bulbs are distinguished by their colour, size, and sweetness; indeed, there are some that are eaten raw even — those found in the Tauric Chersonesus, for instance. Next to these, the bulbs of Africa are held in the highest esteem, and after them those of Apulia. The Greeks have distinguished the following varieties: the bulbine, the setanion, the upition, the cyix, the leucoion, the aegilips, and the syrinchion — in the last there is this remarkable feature, that the extremities of the roots increase in winter, but during [text continues].
results.translation:
being pickled in brine, or else in the juice of rue, which, as Fee [the commentator A.-L.-A. Fée] remarks, would produce nothing more nor less than a veritable poison. The modern Pharmacopoeias provide the formula for a conserve of elecampane [Inula helenium], which, however, is no longer in use. [Note: "Defrutum" — grape must boiled down to half its volume. Note: Julia, daughter of Augustus Caesar.]

Elecampane throws out its shoots to a very considerable distance. Skirrets [Sium sisarum], however, are best propagated by transplanting.

CHAPTER 30. — BULBS, SQUILLS, AND ARUM.

Closely related to these plants are the bulbs, which Cato — speaking in high praise of those from Megara — recommends most particularly for cultivation. Among these bulbs, the squill holds the very highest rank, although it is by nature a medicinal plant and is also employed for imparting additional sharpness to vinegar. Indeed, no bulb is known that grows to a larger size than this, or possesses a greater degree of pungency. Two varieties are employed in medicine: the male squill, which has white [scale-]leaves, and the female squill, which has black [i.e., reddish or darker] ones. There is also a third kind, which is edible, known as the Epimenidian squill [after the philosopher Epimenides, or as named by Theophrastus]; its leaf is narrower than in the other kinds and not so rough. All squills produce numerous seeds, but they propagate much more quickly from the offsets that grow on their sides. To make the bulbs grow still larger, the broad outer leaves surrounding them are bent downward and covered with earth, so that all the plant's juices are directed into the heads [bulbs]. Squills grow wild and in vast numbers in the Balearic Islands, the island of Ebusus [Ibiza], and throughout the Spanish provinces. The philosopher Pythagoras wrote an entire volume on the virtues of this plant, setting forth its various medicinal properties, of which we shall have occasion to speak at greater length in the following Book [Book XX].

The other species of bulbs are distinguished by their colour, size, and sweetness; indeed, some are eaten raw — for example, those found in the Tauric Chersonesus [Crimea]. Next to these, the bulbs of Africa are held in the highest esteem, followed by those of Apulia. The Greeks have distinguished the following varieties: the bulbine, the setanion, the upition, the cyix [perhaps a kind of onion-like plant], the leucoion ["white violet" bulb], the aegilips, and the syrinchion — in the last of which there is a remarkable feature: the extremities of the roots increase in winter, but during [the text breaks off here].

entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as formerly preserved in brine or juice of rue; conserve listed in modern Pharmacopoeias but no longer in use. Pickling in rue juice noted by commentator Fée as potentially toxic.

entities_mentioned.name: Rue

entities_mentioned.name: Defrutum

entities_mentioned.name: Bulbs (general)

The text is from the Bostock & Riley English edition with Fée's commentary integrated as translator's notes. 2. Several footnote numbers appear inline in the source (e.g., 18, 19, 20, 21, etc.); these have been preserved as contextual references. 3. The OCR rendered several Greek variety names poorly (e.g., 'upiti«m', 'eyix', 'a^gi lips', 'asyrinohion'); these have been reconstructed from context as 'upition', 'cyix', 'aegilips', and 'syrinchion'. 4. 'Leucoion' appears as 'leucoiou' in OCR — corrected. 5. The passage cuts off mid-sentence at the end of the chunk. 6. The medicinal uses of squill (Drimia maritima) noted include: sharpening of vinegar, emetic action, and purgative action — with formal treatment deferred to Book XX.

results.title_inferred: Bulbs, Roots, Flowers, and Leaves of Garden Plants (Pliny, Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 30–31)
results.original_text:
of it is of a milder nature, so much so, indeed, as to admit of being eaten raw. Bulbs are taken up before the spring, for if not, they are apt to spoil very quickly. It is a sign that they are ripe when the leaves become dry at the lower extremities. When too old they are held in disesteem; the same, too, with the long and the smaller ones; those, on the other hand, which are red and round are greatly preferred, as also those of the largest size. In most of them there is a certain degree of pungency in the upper part, but the middle is sweet. The ancients have stated that bulbs are reproduced from seed only, but in the champaign country of Praeneste they grow spontaneously, and they grow to an unlimited extent in the territory of the Remi.

CHAP. 31. (6.) THE ROOTS, FLOWERS, AND LEAVES OF ALL THESE PLANTS. GARDEN PLANTS WHICH LOSE THEIR LEAVES.

Nearly all the garden plants have a single root only, radishes, beet, parsley, and mallows, for example; it is lapathum, however, that has the longest root of them all, it attaining the length of three cubits even. The root of the wild kind is smaller and of a humid nature, and when up it will keep alive for a considerable period. In some of these plants, however, the roots are fibrous, as we find the case in parsley and mallows, for instance; in others, again, they are of a ligneous nature, as in ocimum, for example; and in others they are fleshy, as in beet, and in saffron even more so. In some, again, the root is composed of rind and flesh, as in the radish and the rape; while in others it is jointed, as in hay grass. Those plants which have not a straight root throw out immediately a great number of hairy fibres, orage and blite, for instance: squills again, bulbs, onions, and garlic never have any but a vertical root. Among the plants that grow spontaneously, there are some which have more numerous roots than leaves, spalax, for example, pellitory, and saffron. Wild thyme, southernwood, turnips, radishes, mint, and rue blossom all at once; while others, again, shed their blossom directly they have begun to flower. Ocimum blossoms gradually.
results.translation:
Its nature is milder, so much so indeed as to admit of being eaten raw. Bulbs are lifted before spring, for if this is not done they are apt to spoil very quickly. The sign that they are ripe is when the leaves become dry at the lower extremities. Those that are too old are held in low esteem; the same applies to the long and the smaller ones; those, on the other hand, that are red and round are greatly preferred, as are those of the largest size. In most of them there is a certain degree of pungency in the upper part, but the middle is sweet. The ancients stated that bulbs are reproduced from seed only; however, in the flat country around Praeneste they grow spontaneously, and they grow to an unlimited extent in the territory of the Remi [a people of Gaul].

CHAPTER 31. (6.) THE ROOTS, FLOWERS, AND LEAVES OF ALL THESE PLANTS. GARDEN PLANTS WHICH SHED THEIR LEAVES.

Nearly all garden plants have only a single root — radishes, beet, parsley, and mallows, for example. It is lapathum [dock or sorrel], however, that has the longest root of them all, reaching a length of three cubits. The root of the wild kind is smaller and of a moist nature, and once pulled it will remain alive for a considerable time. In some of these plants the roots are fibrous, as is the case with parsley and mallows; in others they are of a woody [ligneous] nature, as in ocimum [basil]; and in others they are fleshy, as in beet, and even more so in saffron. In some, again, the root is composed of rind and flesh, as in the radish and the rape [turnip]; while in others it is jointed, as in hay grass [gramen]. Those plants that do not have a straight root immediately throw out a great number of hairy fibres — orage [Atriplex, orach] and blite [Blitum], for instance. Squills, bulbs, onions, and garlic, on the other hand, never have anything but a vertical root. Among plants that grow spontaneously, some have more numerous roots than leaves — spalax, pellitory, and saffron, for example. Wild thyme, southernwood, turnips, radishes, mint, and rue all blossom at once; while others shed their blossom as soon as they have begun to flower. Ocimum blossoms gradually.

results.translator_notes: This segment corresponds to Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 30–31, and is largely derived from Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum B. vii. c. 11 and B. i. c. 9, as the footnotes indicate. Several OCR errors have been silently corrected: 'nnu-h' → 'much', 'li-aves' → 'leaves', 'sixe' → 'size', 'bLssom' → 'blossom', 'ulm titu'd' → 'identified', 'vax-taUe' → 'vegetable', 'th<;' → 'the'. The footnote reference to 'aegilops' (an old reading, now a grass genus) and the truncated footnote about spalax and the Greek ἀσπάλαξ have been preserved as context. The Remi are a Gaulish people (modern Reims region). 'Champaign country' means flat, open country. Three cubits = approximately 1.4 metres. The identification of several plants (spalax, the cyperaceous bulb, the unidentified bulb of footnote 32) remains uncertain.

results.title_inferred: Varieties of the Onion and Related Plants (Pliny, Natural History, Book XIX, Chapter 32)
results.original_text:
the Ocimum basilicum of the naturalists. The account, however, here given would very well apply to basil. Chap. 32.] VARIETIES OF THE ONION. 171 ally, beginning at the lower parts, and hence it is that it is so very long in blossom: the same is the case, too, with the plant known as heliotropium. In some plants the flower is white, in others yellow, and in others purple. The leaves fall first from the upper part in wild-marjoram and elecampane, and in rue sometimes, when it has been injured accidentally. In some plants the leaves are hollow, the onion and the scallion more particularly.

CHAP. 32. VARIETIES OF THE ONION.

Garlic and onions are invoked by the Egyptians, when taking an oath, in the number of their deities. The Greeks have many varieties of the onion, the Sardian onion, the Samothracian, the Alsidenian, the setanian, the schistan, and the Ascalonian, so called from Ascalon, a city of Judaea. They have, all of them, a pungent smell, which draws tears from the eyes, those of Cyprus more particularly, and those of Cnidos the least of all. In all of them the body is composed of a cartilage of an unctuous nature. The variety known as the setanian is the smallest of them all, with the exception of the Tusculan onion, but it is sweet to the taste. The schistan and the Ascalonian kinds are used for storing. The schistan onion is left during the winter with the leaves on; in the spring it is stripped of them, upon which offsets make their appearance at the same divisions as the leaves; it is to this circumstance that this variety owes its name. Taking the hint from this fact, it is recommended to strip the other kinds of their leaves, to make them bulb all the better, instead of running to seed. The Ascalonian onion is of a peculiar nature, being barren in some measure in the root; hence it is that the Greeks have recommended it to be reproduced from seed, and not from roots: the transplanting, too, they say, should be done later in the spring, at the time the plant germinates, the result being that it bulbs with all the greater rapidity, and hastens, as it were, to make up for lost time; great dispatch, however, is requisite in taking it up, for when ripe it rots with the greatest rapidity. If propagated…
results.translation:
This passage, from Pliny's Natural History Book XIX, Chapter 32, concerns the varieties of the onion and related bulbous plants, as well as observations on flowering and leaf-fall in various herbs.

On the subject of flowering: in some plants, flowering begins at the lower parts and proceeds upward, which is why it lasts so long — as is also the case with the plant called heliotropium [Heliotropium Europaeum]. In some plants the flower is white, in others yellow, in others purple. In wild-marjoram and elecampane, the leaves fall first from the upper part, and sometimes in rue as well, when it has been accidentally damaged. In some plants the leaves are hollow, most notably the onion and the scallion [Allium schoenoprasum].

CHAPTER 32. VARIETIES OF THE ONION.

Garlic and onions are invoked by the Egyptians among their deities when taking an oath. The inhabitants of Pelusium in particular were devoted to the worship of the onion; they held it, along with garlic, in great aversion as a food. At Pelusium there was also a temple in which the sea-squill was worshipped.

The Greeks recognize many varieties of the onion: the Sardian, the Samothracian, the Alsidenian, the setanian, the schistan, and the Ascalonian — the last so named from Ascalon, a city of Judaea (and the source of the English word 'scallion'). All of these have a pungent smell that draws tears from the eyes — this is due to the acetic acid contained in the bulb, which acts upon the membranes of the eye — most notably the Cyprian variety, and least of all those from Cnidos. In all of them, the body of the bulb is composed of a cartilaginous, unctuous [fatty/oily] material. The setanian variety is the smallest of all, with the possible exception of the Tusculan onion, but is sweet in taste. The schistan and Ascalonian kinds are used for storing. The schistan onion (its name derived from the Greek σχίζω, meaning 'to divide' or 'tear off') is left through the winter with its leaves on; in the spring the leaves are stripped away, whereupon offsets appear at the same points where the leaves had been — a circumstance from which the variety takes its name. Taking this as a lesson, it is recommended to strip other varieties of their leaves as well, so that they bulb more vigorously rather than going to seed.

The Ascalonian onion is peculiar in nature, being in some measure barren at the root; therefore the Greeks have recommended that it be reproduced from seed rather than from roots. They also advise that transplanting be done later in spring, at the time when the plant is germinating, so that it bulbs all the more rapidly, hastening as it were to make up for lost time. Great speed, however, is required in lifting it, for when ripe it rots exceedingly quickly. If propagated…
