results.translator_notes: This segment is a translated and annotated edition of Pliny's Natural History, not a raw Latin text — it appears to be the Bostock & Riley English translation with editorial footnotes interpolated into the body. The footnotes have been retained in the translation for clarity and are identifiable by their explanatory character. The observation that acetic acid causes the onion to produce tears is an editorial gloss from the 19th-century translator/editor, not Pliny's own explanation. Fee's doubts about the consistency of Pliny's botanical observations with modern experience are noted. The passage on the schistan onion's name etymology (from Greek σχίζω) is confirmed by the editorial note. The Ascalonian onion's recommendation to be propagated from seed rather than roots, and its tendency to rot rapidly when ripe, are practically significant agricultural observations preserved from Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum, Book VII, Chapter 4) via Pliny.

results.title_inferred: On Onions and Leeks: Varieties, Cultivation, and Uses
results.original_text:
a long stalk and run to seed; in fact, they differ from the Ascalonian kind only in the sweetness of their flavour. Among us there are two principal varieties known of the onion; the scallion, employed for seasonings, is one, known to the Greeks by the name of "gethyon," and by us as the "pallacana;" it is sown in March, April, and May. The other kind is the bulbed or headed onion ["capitata"]; it is sown just after the autumnal equinox, or else after the west winds have begun to prevail. The varieties of this last kind, ranged according to their relative degrees of pungency, are the African onion, the Gallic, the Tusculan, the Ascalonian, and the Amiternian: the roundest in shape are the best. The red onion, too, is more pungent than the white, the stored than the fresh, the raw than the cooked, and the dried than the preserved. The onion of Amiternum is cultivated in cold, humid localities, and is the only one that is reproduced from heads, like garlic, the other kinds being grown from seed. This last kind yields no seed in the ensuing summer, but a bulb only, which dries and keeps; but in the summer after, the contrary is the case, for seed is produced, while the bulb very quickly spoils. Hence it is that every year there are two separate sowings, one of seed for the reproduction of bulbs, and one of bulbs for the growth of seed; these onions keep best in chaff. The scallion has hardly any bulb at all, but a long neck only — hence it is nothing but leaf, and is often cut down, like the leek; for this reason, too, like the leek, it is grown from seed, and not from plants. In addition to these particulars, it is recommended that the ground intended for sowing onions should be turned up three times, care being taken to remove all roots and weeds; ten pounds of seed is the proper proportion for a jugerum. Savory too, they say, should be mixed with them, the onions being all the finer for it; the ground, too, should be stubbed and hoed four times at least, if not oftener. In Italy, the Ascalonian onion is sown in the month of February. The seed of the onion is gathered when it begins to turn black, and before it becomes dry and shrivelled.

CHAP. 33. THE LEEK.

While upon this subject, it will be as well, too, to speak of the leek, on account of the affinity which it bears to the plants just mentioned, and more particularly because cut-leek has recently acquired considerable celebrity from the use made of it by the Emperor Nero. That prince, to improve his voice, used to eat leeks and oil every month, upon stated days, abstaining from every other kind of food, and not touching so much as a morsel of bread even. Leeks are reproduced from seed, sown just after the autumnal equinox; if they are intended for cutting, the seed is sown thicker than otherwise. The leeks in the same bed are cut repeatedly, till it is quite exhausted, and they are always kept well manured.
results.translation:
a long stalk and run to seed; in fact, they differ from the Ascalonian kind only in the sweetness of their flavour. Among us [i.e., the Romans] there are two principal varieties of the onion known: the scallion, employed for seasonings, is one, known to the Greeks by the name 'gethyon' and by us as the 'pallacana'; it is sown in March, April, and May. The other kind is the bulbed or headed onion [Latin: 'capitata']; it is sown just after the autumnal equinox, or else after the west winds have begun to prevail. The varieties of this last kind, ranked according to their relative degrees of pungency, are the African onion, the Gallic, the Tusculan, the Ascalonian, and the Amiternian: the roundest in shape are the best. The red onion, too, is more pungent than the white, the stored than the fresh, the raw than the cooked, and the dried than the preserved. The onion of Amiternum is cultivated in cold, humid localities and is the only one reproduced from heads [i.e., cloves or offset bulbs], like garlic, whereas the other kinds are grown from seed. This last kind yields no seed in the ensuing summer, but a bulb only, which dries and keeps; but in the summer following that, the contrary is the case, for seed is produced while the bulb very quickly spoils. Hence it is that every year there are two separate sowings: one of seed for the reproduction of bulbs, and one of bulbs for the growth of seed; these onions keep best stored in chaff. The scallion has hardly any bulb at all, but a long neck only — it is therefore nothing but leaf, and is often cut down, like the leek; for this reason too, like the leek, it is grown from seed and not from transplants. In addition to these particulars, it is recommended that the ground intended for sowing onions should be turned up three times, care being taken to remove all roots and weeds; ten pounds of seed is the proper proportion for a jugerum [a Roman unit of area, approximately 0.25 hectares]. Savory too, they say, should be mixed with them, the onions being all the finer for it; the ground, too, should be stubbed and hoed at least four times, if not oftener. In Italy, the Ascalonian onion is sown in the month of February. The seed of the onion is gathered when it begins to turn black, and before it becomes dry and shrivelled.

CHAPTER 33. THE LEEK.

While upon this subject, it will be appropriate also to speak of the leek [Allium porrum L.], on account of the affinity it bears to the plants just mentioned, and more particularly because the cut-leek has recently acquired considerable celebrity from the use made of it by the Emperor Nero. That prince, in order to improve his voice, used to eat leeks and oil every month on stated days, abstaining from every other kind of food and not touching so much as a morsel of bread. Leeks are reproduced from seed, sown just after the autumnal equinox; if they are intended for cutting rather than heading, the seed is sown more thickly than otherwise. The leeks in the same bed are cut repeatedly until it is quite exhausted, and they are always kept well manured.

entities_mentioned.name: Scallion / Gethyon / Pallacana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium fistulosum or Allium cepa var. (bunching/spring onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known to the Greeks as 'gethyon' and to the Romans as 'pallacana'; characterized by a long neck and minimal bulb; used as a seasoning herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Headed onion (Capitata)
entities_mentioned.notes: The common bulb onion; multiple regional varieties described including African, Gallic, Tusculan, Ascalonian, and Amiternian.

entities_mentioned.name: Ascalonian onion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum L. (shallot) or Allium cepa var. ascalonicum
entities_mentioned.notes: Named after Ascalon (Ashkelon); the scallion is said to differ from it only in sweetness of flavour.

entities_mentioned.name: Onion of Amiternum (Amiternian onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Allium sativum or Allium ascalonicum (Fee's suggestion); actual identity uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivated in cold, humid localities; reproduced from heads like garlic rather than from seed; Pliny's commentator Féé considers it possibly a variety of garlic (Allium sativum) or shallot (Allium ascalonicum).

entities_mentioned.name: Savory
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Satureja hortensis L. (summer savory) or Satureja montana L. (winter savory)
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended to be sown mixed with onions as a companion plant to improve their quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Leek
entities_mentioned.notes: Emperor Nero reportedly consumed leeks with oil on stated days each month to improve his voice; the leek's mucilaginous properties were believed beneficial for the voice, though this is doubted by Pliny's commentator Féé.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, likely Book XIX or XX, covering cultivated alliums. The source text is an English translation (possibly Bostock & Riley) with footnotes integrated inline, including Féé's botanical commentary. The footnote numbering (59, 60, 61, 62, 63) has been preserved in context. 'Jugerum' is a Roman unit of area (~0.25 hectares or ~0.62 acres). The reference to Emperor Nero eating leeks for his voice is a well-known classical anecdote. The word 'Tusctilan' in the source is an OCR error for 'Tusculan' (referring to Tusculum, near Rome), silently corrected. Féé refers to Antoine Laurent Apollinaire Fée, a 19th-century botanist and commentator on Pliny.

results.title_inferred: On Leeks and Garlic: Cultivation, Varieties, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
heads stripped of the outer coats. The ancients were in the habit of placing a stone or potsherd upon the leek, to make the head grow all the larger, and the same with the bulbs as well; but at the present day it is the usual practice to move the fibrous roots gently with the weeding-hook, so that by being bent they may nourish the plant, and not withdraw the juices from it. It is a remarkable fact, that, though the leek stands in need of manure and a rich soil, it has a particular aversion to water; and yet its nature depends very much upon the natural properties of the soil. The most esteemed leeks are those grown in Egypt, and next to them those of Ostia and Aricia. Of the leek for cutting, there are two varieties: that with grass-green leaves and incisions distinctly traced on them, and the leek with paler and rounder leaves, the incisions being more lightly marked. There is a story told, that Mela, a member of the Equestrian order, being accused of mal-administration by order of the Emperor Tiberius, swallowed in his despair leek-juice to the amount of three denarii in weight of silver, and expired upon the spot without the slightest symptom of pain. It is said, however, that a larger dose than this is productive of no injurious effects whatever.

CHAP. 34. GARLIC. Garlic is generally supposed, in the country more particularly, to be a good specific for numerous maladies. The external coat consists of membranes of remarkable fineness, which are universally discarded when the vegetable is used; the inner part being formed by the union of several cloves, each of which has also a separate coat of its own. The flavour of it is pungent, and the more numerous the cloves the more pungent it is. Like the onion, it imparts an offensive smell to the breath; but this is not the case when it is cooked. The various species of garlic are distinguished by the periods at which they ripen: the early kind becomes fit for use in sixty days. Another distinction, too, is formed by the relative size of the heads. Ulpicum, also, generally known to the Greeks as "Cyprian garlic," belongs to this class; by some persons it is called "antiscordon," and in Africa more particularly it holds a high rank among the dishes of the rural population; it is of a larger size than ordinary garlic. When beaten up with oil and vinegar, it is quite surprising what a quantity of creaming foam is produced. There are some persons who recommend that neither ulpicum nor garlic should be sown on level ground, but say that they should be planted in little mounds trenched up, at a distance of three feet apart. Between each clove, they say, there should be a distance of four fingers left, and as soon as ever three leaves are visible, the heads should be [text ends].

The ancients were in the habit of placing a stone or potsherd upon the leek to make the head grow all the larger, and the same with the bulbs as well; but at the present day it is the usual practice to move the fibrous roots gently with the weeding-hook, so that by being bent they may nourish the plant and not withdraw the juices from it. It is a remarkable fact that, though the leek stands in need of manure and a rich soil, it has a particular aversion to water; and yet its nature depends very much upon the natural properties of the soil. The most esteemed leeks are those grown in Egypt, and next to them those of Ostia and Aricia. Of the leek grown for cutting [porrum sectivum], there are two varieties: that with grass-green leaves and incisions distinctly traced on them, and the leek with paler and rounder leaves, the incisions being more lightly marked. There is a story told that Mela, a member of the Equestrian order, being accused of mal-administration by order of the Emperor Tiberius, swallowed in his despair leek-juice to the amount of three denarii in weight of silver, and expired upon the spot without the slightest symptom of pain. It is said, however, that a larger dose than this is productive of no injurious effects whatever.

The external coat consists of membranes of remarkable fineness, which are universally discarded when the vegetable is used; the inner part is formed by the union of several cloves, each of which has also a separate coat of its own. The flavour of it is pungent, and the more numerous the cloves the more pungent it is. Like the onion, it imparts an offensive smell to the breath; but this is not the case when it is cooked. entities_mentioned.name: Leek (porrum sectivum — cutting leek)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny discusses two cutting-leek varieties distinguished by leaf color and incision depth. Egyptian, Ostian, and Arician varieties are noted as most esteemed.

entities_mentioned.name: Garlic (allium / alium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a general rural specific for numerous maladies. Pungency proportional to number of cloves. Breath odor diminishes when cooked. Footnote references Horace's 'O dura messorum ilia' and notes it was eaten by Roman soldiers, sailors, and field labourers.

entities_mentioned.name: Ulpicum / Cyprian garlic / antiscordon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Allium ampeloprasum (great-headed garlic or elephant garlic)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as larger than ordinary garlic, highly regarded in Africa among rural populations. Greek name given as 'antiscordon' (ἀντισκόροδον). Produces copious foam when beaten with oil and vinegar.

entities_mentioned.name: Leek-juice (as poison/drug in the anecdote of Mela)
entities_mentioned.notes: Anecdote: M. Annaeus Mela (brother of Seneca, father of the poet Lucan) allegedly died after drinking leek-juice weighing three silver denarii. Pliny suggests a larger dose is harmless; footnote notes the juice is in reality quite harmless, casting doubt on the story.

The source text contains OCR errors ('tho' for 'the', 'beiDg' for 'being', 'inal-adininistration' for 'mal-administration', 'GA11LIC' for 'GARLIC', '1/5' for '175' as a page number artifact) — all silently corrected where unambiguous. 2. The segment ends mid-sentence ('the heads should be') indicating a chunk boundary; this is flagged. 3. The footnote referencing Horace ('O dura messorum ilia') relates to garlic being 'more noxious than hemlock' — this is a hyperbolic poetic complaint about the smell, not a medical statement. 4. The preparation noted for ulpicum (beaten with oil and vinegar) producing foam is remarkable and may relate to an emulsification property; no specific medicinal use is assigned to this preparation in the surviving text. 5. The identification of ulpicum with Allium ampeloprasum is a scholarly consensus but not universally accepted.

results.translation:
For the purpose of depriving all these plants of their strong smell, it is recommended to plant them when the moon is below the horizon and to harvest them when she is in conjunction. Apart from these precautions, we find Menander, one of the Greek writers, recommending that those who have been eating garlic should eat immediately afterwards a root of beet [beta]; and although persons who had just eaten garlic were not permitted to enter the Temple of the Mother of the Gods [Cybele], it was prescribed to those who wished to be purified and absolved from crimes. Garlic is still held in considerable esteem in the south of Europe, where the lower classes ascribe great medicinal virtues to it. Theophrastus states (Historia Plantarum, Book VII, ch. 4) that this is the largest of all varieties of garlic. [Menander recommends] roasting garlic on hot coals; by doing so, he says, the strong smell of the garlic will be effectually neutralized. Some are of the opinion that the proper period for planting garlic and ulpicum [a related Allium variety] is between the festival of the Compitalia [2nd of May] and that of the Saturnalia [17th of December]. Garlic can also be grown from seed, but it is very slow in such a case to come to maturity: in the first year the head attains the size only of that of a leek; in the second it separates into cloves; and only in the third year does it arrive at full maturity. There are some, however, who think that garlic grown in this way is the best. Garlic should never be allowed to run to seed; instead, the stalk should be twisted to promote its growth and to make the head attain a larger size. If garlic or onions are required to keep for some time, the heads should be dipped in lukewarm salt water; by doing this they will keep better, though they become quite worthless for reproduction. Some persons content themselves with hanging them over burning coals, considering this sufficient to prevent sprouting — for it is well known that both garlic and onions sprout when out of the ground and, after throwing out their thin shoots, shrivel away to nothing. Some are also of the opinion that the best way of keeping garlic is to store it in chaff. There is a kind of garlic [Allium oleraceum L.] that grows spontaneously in the fields and is known by the name of "alum" [field garlic]. To protect the seeds sown in such fields from the destructive ravages of birds, this plant is scattered over the ground after first being boiled to prevent it from taking root. As soon as the birds have eaten of it, they become so stupefied as to be taken by hand, and if they remain on the spot for even a few moments they fall fast asleep. There is also a wild garlic generally known as "bear's garlic" [Allium ursinum L.], which has exactly the smell of millet, with a very small head and large leaves. In practice, however, its odour is far from the comparatively mild smell of millet — it is quite powerful, so much so as to impart a strong flavour to the milk of cows that eat of it. It is very common, as Fée notes, in nearly every part of France.

CHAPTER 35. (7.) THE NUMBER OF DAYS REQUIRED FOR THE RESPECTIVE PLANTS TO MAKE THEIR APPEARANCE ABOVE GROUND.

Among the garden plants that make their appearance most speedily above ground are ocimum [basil], blite [blitum / good-king-henry], the turnip, and rocket; for they appear...

results.title_inferred: Varieties of Lettuce: Classification, Colours, Sowing Times, and Medicinal Properties
results.original_text: parts of the plant. The second kind is that with a rounded stalk; and the third is the low, squat lettuce, generally known as the Laconian lettuce. Some persons have made distinctions in reference to their respective colours, and the times for sowing them: the black lettuce is sown in the month of January, the white in March, and the red in April; and they are fit for transplanting, all of them, at the end of a couple of months. Those, again, who have pursued these enquiries even further than this, have distinguished a still greater number of varieties of them — the purple, the crisped, the Cappadocian, and the Greek lettuce, this last having a longer leaf than the rest, and a broad stalk: in addition to which, there is one with a long, narrow leaf, very similar to endive in appearance. The most inferior kind, however, of all, is the one to which the Greeks, censuring it for its bitterness, have given the name of "picris." There is still another variety, a kind of white lettuce, called "meconis," a name which it derives from the abundance of milk, of a narcotic quality, which it produces; though, in fact, it is generally thought that they are all of them of a soporific tendency. In former times, this last was the only kind of lettuce that was held in any esteem in Italy, the name "lactuca" having been given it on account of the milk which it contains. The purple kind, with a very large root, is generally known as the Caecilian lettuce; while the round one, with an extremely diminutive root and broad leaves, is known to some persons as the "astytis," and to others as the "eunychion," it having the effect, in a remarkable degree, of quenching the amorous propensities. Indeed, they are, all of them, possessed of cooling and refreshing properties, for which reason it is, that they are so highly esteemed in summer; they have the effect, also, of removing from the stomach distaste for food, and of promoting the appetite. At all events, we find it stated, that the late Emperor Augustus, when ill, was saved.
results.translation: Parts of the plant. The second kind is that with a rounded stalk; and the third is the low, squat lettuce, generally known as the Laconian lettuce. Some persons — Columella most notably — have drawn distinctions with regard to colour and sowing times: the black lettuce is sown in January, the white in March, and the red in April; all varieties are ready for transplanting after about two months. Those who have pursued the subject still further have distinguished an even greater number of varieties: the purple, the crisped [curly-leaved], the Cappadocian, and the Greek lettuce, this last having a longer leaf than the rest and a broad stalk. In addition there is a variety with a long, narrow leaf closely resembling endive in appearance. The most inferior of all, however, is the one the Greeks have named "picris" [bitter-herb], censuring it for its bitterness. There is yet another variety, a kind of white lettuce called "meconis," a name derived from the abundance of narcotic milk [latex] it produces; though in fact all varieties are generally thought to have a soporific tendency. In former times, this last was the only kind held in any esteem in Italy, and the name "lactuca" was given to it on account of the milk [lac] it contains. The purple kind, with a very large root, is generally known as the Caecilian lettuce; while the round one, with an extremely small root and broad leaves, is known to some as "astytis" and to others as "eunychion" [bed-companion], it having the effect, in a remarkable degree, of suppressing amorous impulses. Indeed, all varieties possess cooling and refreshing properties, for which reason they are so highly valued in summer; they also have the effect of relieving the stomach of distaste for food and of stimulating the appetite. At all events, it is recorded that the late Emperor Augustus, when gravely ill, was saved [by this plant].

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX (or XX), Chapter 38, concerning garden plants, specifically lettuce varieties. The text as supplied is a 19th-century English translation with editorial footnotes interspersed; the 'original text' field above represents the main translated body with footnote markers removed for clarity. Footnote references (superscript numbers and lettered notes) have been silently removed from the cleaned text as OCR artifacts and editorial intrusions. The passage references Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum Book VII, Chapter 4, as a source for the Greek varieties. The anecdote about Emperor Augustus being saved by lettuce is noted as originating elsewhere in the text (cut off at the end of this chunk). The term 'meconis' and its narcotic/soporific properties are of particular pharmacological interest for materia medica purposes.

results.title_inferred: Lettuces, Endive, and Their Cultivation and Preservation (Pliny, Natural History, Book XIX)
results.original_text:
So called, Columella informs us, from Caecilius Metellus, Consul A.u.c. 503. Meaning "antaphrodisiac." The other name has a kindred meaning. on one occasion, thanks to the skill of his physician, Musa, by eating lettuces, a food which the excessive scruples of his former physician, C. Aemilius, had forbidden him. At the present day, however, lettuces have risen into such high estimation, that a method has been discovered even of preserving them during the months in which they are out of season, by keeping them in oxymel. It is generally supposed, also, that lettuces have the effect of making blood. In addition to the above varieties, there is another kind of lettuce known as the "goats' lettuce," of which we shall have occasion to make further mention when we come to the medicinal plants: at the moment, too, that I am writing this, a new species of cultivated lettuce has been introduced, known as the Cilician lettuce, and held in very considerable esteem; the leaf of it is similar to that of the Cappadocian lettuce, except that it is crisped, and somewhat larger.

CHAP. 39. ENDIVE.

Endive, though it cannot exactly be said to be of the same genus as the lettuce, still cannot be pronounced to belong to any other. It is a plant better able to endure the rigours of the winter than the lettuce, and possessed of a more acrid taste, though the flavour of the stalk is equally agreeable. Endive is sown at the beginning of spring, and transplanted at the end of that season. There is also a kind of spreading endive, known in Egypt as "cichorium," of which we shall have occasion to speak elsewhere more at length.

A method has been discovered of preserving all the thyrsi or leaves of the lettuce in pots, the object being to have them fresh when wanted for boiling. Lettuces may be sown all the year through in a good soil, well-watered and carefully manured; two months being allowed to intervene between sowing and transplanting, and two more between transplanting and gathering them when ripe. The rule is, however, to sow them just after the winter solstice, and to transplant when the west winds begin to prevail, or else to sow at this latter period, and to plant out at the vernal equinox. The white lettuce is the best adapted for standing the rigours of the winter. All the garden plants are fond of moisture; lettuces thrive, more particularly, when well manured, and endive even more so.
results.translation:
As Columella informs us, [a variety of lettuce] was named after Caecilius Metellus, Consul in the year 503 of the city [A.U.C. 503]. [Another name carries the meaning of] "antaphrodisiac" [i.e., suppressive of sexual desire], and another name is of kindred meaning. On one occasion [A.U.C. 731], Augustus Caesar was restored to health through the skill of his physician, Antonius Musa, by eating lettuces—a food which his previous physician, C. Aemilius, with excessive caution, had forbidden him. In the present day, however, lettuces have risen to such high esteem that a method has even been devised for preserving them during the months when they are out of season, by keeping them in oxymel [a mixture of vinegar and honey]. It is also generally believed that lettuces have the effect of generating blood. Beyond the varieties already mentioned, there is another kind known as "goats' lettuce" [caprina lactuca], of which further mention will be made in the section on medicinal plants. At the very time of writing, a new species of cultivated lettuce has been introduced, called the Cilician lettuce, and held in considerable esteem; its leaf resembles that of the Cappadocian lettuce, except that it is crisped and somewhat larger.

CHAPTER 39. ENDIVE.

Endive, though it cannot quite be said to belong to the same genus as the lettuce, cannot be assigned to any other genus either. It is a plant better able to endure the rigours of winter than the lettuce, and has a more acrid taste, though the flavour of the stalk is equally agreeable. Endive is sown at the beginning of spring and transplanted at the end of that season. There is also a spreading [wild] variety of endive, known in Egypt as "cichorium" [chicory/succory], of which we shall speak at greater length elsewhere.

A method has also been discovered of preserving all the thyrsi [stalks] or leaves of the lettuce in pots, with the aim of having them fresh when needed for boiling. Lettuces may be sown throughout the entire year in good, well-watered, and carefully manured soil, with an interval of two months allowed between sowing and transplanting, and another two months between transplanting and harvesting when ripe. The standard practice, however, is to sow just after the winter solstice and to transplant when the west winds begin to prevail; or alternatively, to sow at the time the west winds begin and to plant out at the vernal equinox. The white lettuce is best suited to withstanding the severity of winter. All garden plants are fond of moisture; lettuces thrive especially well when well manured, and endive even more so.

entities_mentioned.name: Lettuce (various varieties: Caecilian, Cappadocian, Cilician, white, goats' lettuce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lactuca sativa and related Lactuca species
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple cultivated varieties described; 'goats' lettuce' (caprina lactuca) may refer to a wild Lactuca or related genus. Pliny attributes blood-generating properties to lettuce and notes its use in the recovery of Augustus Caesar.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymel
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a preserving medium for lettuces out of season. Footnote from Fee notes it is ill-adapted to preserving either medicinal or alimentary properties of lettuce.

entities_mentioned.name: Endive
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as closely related to but distinct from lettuce; more winter-hardy; acrid in taste. The spreading/wild variety called 'cichorium' in Egypt is identified as the source of the French 'chicoree' and English 'chicory' or 'succory'.

entities_mentioned.name: Cichorium (Egyptian spreading endive)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by Pliny as the Egyptian name for a spreading variety of endive; source of modern 'chicory' and 'succory'.

entities_mentioned.name: Goats' lettuce (caprina lactuca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lactuca virosa or a related wild Lactuca species
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned here briefly; fuller treatment promised in the section on medicinal plants (Book XX, c. 24).

entities_mentioned.name: Antonius Musa
entities_mentioned.notes: Physician to Augustus Caesar; credited with restoring the emperor's health through a diet including lettuces. Received public honours including a gold ring and a statue near that of Aesculapius. Possibly referenced by Virgil in the Aeneid as 'Iapis'.

entities_mentioned.name: Caecilius Metellus
entities_mentioned.notes: Roman consul (A.U.C. 503) after whom a variety of lettuce was reportedly named, according to Columella.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a translated edition of Pliny's Natural History (Book XIX), not the Latin original; this translation renders the existing English scholarly translation into cleaned and structured form. The footnotes embedded in the source have been incorporated into entity notes and translator context where relevant. The term 'thyrsi' (stalks/stem clusters) is retained in brackets as a period technical term. 'Oxymel' is a classical pharmaceutical preparation (vinegar + honey) used here for food preservation rather than strictly medicinal purposes. The medical claim that lettuces 'make blood' reflects classical humoral theory. The identification of the 'goats' lettuce' remains uncertain and is flagged as low confidence. The segment also touches on agricultural practice (sowing, transplanting schedules) which is paratextually relevant to Materia Medica contexts.

results.title_inferred: On Beet: Its Varieties, Cultivation, Culinary Uses, and Medical Properties
results.original_text: admit of being blanched which are produced from white seed; and even then, as soon as they begin to grow, sand from the sea-shore should be spread over them, care being taken to tie the leaves as soon as ever they begin to come to any size. CHAP. 40. BEET: FOUR VARIETIES OF IT. Beet is the smoothest of all the garden plants. The Greeks distinguish two kinds of beet, according to the colour, the black and the white. The last, which is the kind generally preferred, has but very little seed, and is generally known as the Sicilian beet; just as it is the white lettuce that is held in the highest degree of esteem. Our people, also, distinguish two varieties of beet, the spring and the autumn kinds, so called from the periods of sowing; although sometimes we find beet sown in June even. This is a plant, too, that is sometimes transplanted; and it thrives all the better, like the lettuce, if the roots are well covered with manure, in a moist soil. Beet is mostly eaten with lentils and beans; it is prepared also in the same way as cabbage, with mustard more particularly, the pungency of which relieves its insipidity. Medical men are of opinion that beet is a more unwholesome vegetable than cabbage; hence it is that I never remember seeing it served at table. Indeed, there are some persons who scruple to taste it even, from a conviction that it is a food suitable only for persons of a robust constitution. Beet is a vegetable with twofold characteristics, partaking of the nature of the cabbage in its leaves and resembling a bulb in the root; that which grows to the greatest breadth being the most highly esteemed. This plant, like the lettuce, is made to grow to head by putting a light weight upon it the moment it begins to assume its proper colour. Indeed, there is no garden plant that grows to a larger head than this, as it sometimes spreads to a couple of feet in breadth, the nature of the soil contributing in a very considerable degree to its size: those found in the territory of Circeii attain the largest size. Some persons think that the best time for sowing beet is when the pomegranate is in flower, and are of opinion that it ought to be transplanted as soon as it has thrown out five leaves. There is a singular difference — if indeed it really exists — between the two varieties of beet, the white kind being remarkable for its purgative qualities, and the black being equally astringent. When wine in the vat has been deteriorated by assuming a flavour like that of cabbage, its original flavour is restored, it is said, by plunging beet leaves into it.
results.translation:
Only those [lettuces] produced from white seed are capable of being blanched; and even then, as soon as they begin to grow, sand from the sea-shore should be spread over them, taking care to tie the leaves together as soon as they begin to reach any appreciable size.

CHAPTER 40. BEET: FOUR VARIETIES OF IT.

Beet is the smoothest of all garden plants. The Greeks distinguish two kinds of beet by colour: the black and the white. The latter, which is the variety generally preferred, produces very little seed and is commonly known as the Sicilian beet — just as it is the white lettuce that is held in the highest esteem. Our own people likewise distinguish two varieties of beet, the spring and the autumn kinds, named after their respective sowing seasons, though beet is sometimes sown even in June. It is also a plant that tolerates transplanting, and like the lettuce it thrives all the better when its roots are well covered with manure in moist soil.

Beet is most commonly eaten with lentils and beans; it is also prepared in the same manner as cabbage, especially with mustard, whose pungency counteracts the beet's insipidity. Medical authorities hold that beet is a more unwholesome vegetable than cabbage, which is why I can never recall seeing it served at table. Indeed, some persons are reluctant even to taste it, being convinced that it is food suited only to those of robust constitution.

Beet is a vegetable of twofold character: its leaves partake of the nature of the cabbage, while its root resembles a bulb; the specimens that spread to the greatest breadth are the most highly prized. Like the lettuce, it is induced to form a head by placing a light weight upon it the moment it begins to take on its proper colour. No other garden plant grows to a larger head than this, sometimes spreading to a breadth of two feet — the nature of the soil contributing greatly to its size — and those found in the territory of Circeii [on the Latian coast] attain the largest dimensions of all. Some authorities believe the best time for sowing beet is when the pomegranate is in flower, and hold that it should be transplanted as soon as it has put forth five leaves.

There is a notable difference — if indeed it truly exists — between the two varieties: the white kind is remarkable for its purgative properties, while the black is equally notable for its astringent qualities. When wine in the vat has been spoiled by taking on a flavour resembling that of cabbage, its original flavour is said to be restored by plunging beet leaves into it.

entities_mentioned.name: Beet (Beta)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta vulgaris L. (common beet / beetroot / chard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny draws on Theophrastus (Hist. Plant. VII.4). The 'Sicilian beet' (white variety) is distinguished from the dark/black variety; Féé doubts it is Beta sicla of modern botany. In antiquity only the leaves, not the root, were eaten.

entities_mentioned.name: Sicilian beet
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical 'Sicilian beet' is not confidently identified with Beta sicla (silver beet/chard) of modern taxonomy according to Féé.

entities_mentioned.name: Black beet
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to be astringent; likely a dark-leaved cultivar of Beta vulgaris.

entities_mentioned.name: White beet
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have purgative [laxative] properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Lettuce
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in comparison to beet regarding blanching, heading, and manuring practices.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentils
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly eaten together with beet leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Beans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba L. (broad bean) or Phaseolus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Eaten together with beet; likely faba beans in Roman context.

entities_mentioned.name: Mustard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sinapis alba L. or Brassica nigra (L.) K.Koch
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a condiment with beet to counteract its insipidity.

entities_mentioned.name: Cabbage
entities_mentioned.notes: Compared to beet in preparation method and wholesomeness; beet leaves prepared similarly to cabbage.

entities_mentioned.name: Pomegranate
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a phenological marker: beet should be sown when the pomegranate is in flower.

entities_mentioned.name: Circeii (territory of)
entities_mentioned.notes: Coastal Latian locality noted for producing beet of exceptional size.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment opens mid-sentence continuing discussion of lettuce blanching from a preceding chapter before transitioning to Chapter 40 on beet. 2. Pliny's principal source for beet is Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.4, as noted in footnote 22. 3. The footnote reference to 'Fee' is to Féé, the 19th-century French botanist who annotated Pliny's botanical passages; his skepticism about the identification of 'Sicilian beet' with Beta sicla is preserved in the translator's note. 4. The medical properties extracted: white beet — purgative/laxative; black beet — astringent. No specific preparation method for medicinal use is described beyond culinary preparation (boiled with mustard). 5. The wine-restoration use (plunging beet leaves into wine tainted with a cabbage-like off-flavour) is an additional non-medicinal practical application. 6. The OCR error 's.eeing' has been silently corrected to 'seeing'; 'PLIOT'S NATFIUL HISTOBY' in the header is an OCR artifact for 'PLINY'S NATURAL HISTORY' and has been omitted from the cleaned text as a running header. 7. Footnote 24 clarifies that only the leaf, not the root, was consumed by ancient Romans — an important ethnobotanical distinction.

results.title_inferred: On Cabbages: Varieties, Cultivation, and Uses
results.original_text: CHAP. 41 — CABBAGES; THE SEVERAL VARIETIES OF THEM. Cabbage and coleworts, which at the present day are the most highly esteemed of all the garden vegetables, were held in little repute, I find, among the Greeks; but Cato, on the other hand, sings the wondrous praises of the cabbage, the medicinal properties of which we shall duly enlarge upon when we come to treat of that subject. Cato distinguishes three varieties of the cabbage; the first, a plant with leaves wide open, and a large stalk; a second, with crisped leaves, to which he gives the name of "apiaca"; and a third, with a thin stalk, and a smooth, tender leaf, which with him ranks the lowest of all. Cabbages may be sown the whole year through, as we find that they are cut at all periods of the year; the best time, however, for sowing them is at the autumnal equinox, and they are usually transplanted as soon as five leaves are visible. In the ensuing spring after the first cutting, the plant yields sprouts, known to us as "cymae". These sprouts, in fact, are small shoots thrown out from the main stem, of a more delicate and tender quality than the cabbage itself. The exquisite palate, however, of Apicius rejected these sprouts for the table, and his example was followed by the fastidious Drusus Caesar; who did not escape, however, the censures of his father, Tiberius, for being so over-nice. After the cymae have made their appearance the cabbage throws out its summer and autumn shoots, and then its winter ones; after which, a new crop of cymae is produced, there being no plant so productive as this, until, at last, it is quite exhausted by its extreme fertility. A second time for sowing cabbages is immediately after the vernal equinox, the plants of this growth being transplanted at the end of spring, that they may not run up into sprouts before coming to a top: and a third sowing takes place about the summer solstice, the transplanting being done in summer if the soil is moist, but, if too dry, in autumn. When moisture and manure are supplied in small quantities, the flavour of the cabbage is all the more agreeable, but when they are supplied in greater abundance, the plants attain a larger size. Asses' dung is the best adapted for its growth. The cabbage, too, is one of those articles so highly esteemed by epicures; for which reason it will not be amiss if we speak of it at somewhat greater length. To obtain plants equally remarkable for their size and flavour, care must be taken first of all to sow the seed in ground that has had a couple of turnings up, and then to follow up the shoots as they appear above ground by moulding them up, care being taken to throw up the earth over them as they increase in luxuriance, and to let nothing but the summit appear above the surface. This kind is known as the Tritian cabbage: in money and labour it costs twice as much as any of the others. The other varieties of the cabbage are numerous — there is the Cumanian cabbage, with leaves that lie close to the ground, and a wide, open head; the Aricinian cabbage, too, of numerous small leaves.
results.translation: CHAPTER 41 — CABBAGES AND THEIR SEVERAL VARIETIES. Cabbage and coleworts [Brassica oleracea], which at the present day are the most highly esteemed of all the garden vegetables, were held in little repute, I find, among the Greeks; but Cato, on the other hand, sings the wondrous praises of the cabbage, the medicinal properties of which we shall duly expand upon when we come to treat of that subject. Cato distinguishes three varieties of the cabbage: the first, a plant with wide-open leaves and a large stalk; a second, with crisped [curled] leaves, to which he gives the name "apiaca" [i.e., parsley-cabbage, so called from its crisped leaves; the curled colewort, or Brassica viridis crispa of C. Bauhin]; and a third, with a thin stalk and a smooth, tender leaf, which in his estimation ranks the lowest of all. Cabbages may be sown throughout the entire year, as we find that they are harvested at all seasons; the best time for sowing them, however, is at the autumnal equinox, and they are usually transplanted as soon as five leaves have appeared. In the spring following the first cutting, the plant yields sprouts known to us as "cymae" [the same as our Brussels sprouts]. These sprouts are, in fact, small shoots thrown out from the main stem, of a more delicate and tender quality than the cabbage itself. The exquisite palate of Apicius, however, rejected these sprouts for the table, and his example was followed by the fastidious Drusus Caesar, who did not escape, however, the censures of his father Tiberius for being so over-refined in his tastes. After the cymae have made their appearance, the cabbage throws out its summer and autumn shoots, and then its winter ones; after which, a new crop of cymae is produced, there being no plant so prolific as this — until at last it is quite exhausted by its extreme fertility. A second time for sowing cabbages is immediately after the vernal equinox, the plants of this growth being transplanted at the end of spring so that they may not run up into sprouts before forming a proper head. A third sowing takes place around the summer solstice, with transplanting done in summer if the soil is moist, or in autumn if it is too dry. When moisture and manure are supplied in small quantities, the flavour of the cabbage is all the more agreeable; but when they are supplied in greater abundance, the plants attain a larger size. Asses' dung is the best suited for its growth. The cabbage, too, is one of those articles so highly prized by epicures, and for this reason it will not be amiss to speak of it at somewhat greater length. To obtain plants equally remarkable for their size and flavour, care must be taken first of all to sow the seed in ground that has been turned over twice, and then to earth up the shoots as they appear above ground, taking care to mound the soil over them as they grow more luxuriantly, allowing nothing but the topmost part to remain above the surface. This kind is known as the Tritian ["Tritiana"] cabbage: it costs twice as much in money and labour as any of the others. The other varieties of cabbage are numerous: there is the Cumanian cabbage, with leaves that lie close to the ground and a wide, open head; and the Aricinian cabbage as well, which has numerous small leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Cabbage / Colewort (general)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's general discussion of cabbage encompasses the broader Brassica oleracea species group.

entities_mentioned.name: Apiaca cabbage (crisped-leaf variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica oleracea var. viridis crispa (curled colewort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for its parsley-like crisped leaves; identified by C. Bauhin as Brassica viridis crispa. Cato's name 'apiaca' derives from 'apium' (parsley/celery), referencing the leaf texture.

entities_mentioned.name: Cymae (cabbage sprouts)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica oleracea var. gemmifera (Brussels sprouts) or sprouting shoots of common cabbage
entities_mentioned.notes: The footnote equates these with Brussels sprouts, though Columella (B. xi. c. 3; B. xii. c. 7) treats Brassica cyma as a distinct variety. The identification with modern Brussels sprouts is approximate.

entities_mentioned.name: Tritian cabbage ("Tritiana")
entities_mentioned.notes: A hilled/earthed-up cultivar grown for both size and flavour; name may derive from a place or person. No confident modern equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: Cumanian cabbage
entities_mentioned.notes: Named after Cumae; described as having leaves lying close to the ground with a wide, open head.

entities_mentioned.name: Aricinian cabbage
entities_mentioned.notes: Named after Aricia (a town in Latium); described as having numerous small leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Cato
entities_mentioned.notes: Marcus Porcius Cato (Cato the Elder); his De Re Rustica (cc. 156–157) is cited as the source for cabbage varieties and medicinal praise.

entities_mentioned.name: Apicius
entities_mentioned.notes: Roman epicure; cited for his rejection of cymae at table. Referenced further in Book VIII, c. 77.

entities_mentioned.name: Drusus Caesar
entities_mentioned.notes: Son of the Emperor Tiberius; noted here for his fastidious rejection of cymae, for which he was rebuked by his father.

results.translator_notes: The source text is an existing English translation (Bostock & Riley's Pliny) with some OCR artifacts: 'cymae' is rendered variously as 'cyma3', 'cymse', and 'cymaB' in the OCR, all silently corrected to 'cymae'. 'Flint's Natural History' in the header is an OCR misread of 'Pliny's Natural History'. The footnote markers (28–32) have been integrated or retained as parenthetical references for clarity. The medicinal properties of cabbage are cross-referenced to Book XX, c. 33, where Pliny treats them at length. The segment is from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, Chapter 41, and the translation provided in the source is essentially that of Bostock & Riley (1855); this rendering preserves and lightly clarifies that translation.

results.title_inferred: Varieties of Cabbage: Bruttian, Sabine, Lacuturrian, and Sea-Shore Cabbages; Cultivation and Preservation Methods
results.original_text: which is thin at the root, increasing in thickness as it rises among the leaves, which are fewer in number and narrower; the great merit of this cabbage is its remarkable tenderness, although it is not able to stand the cold. The cabbage of Bruttium, on the other hand, thrives all the better for cold; the leaves of it are remarkably large, the stalk thin, and the flavour pungent. The leaves, again, of the Sabine cabbage are crisped to such a degree as to excite our surprise, and their thickness is such as to quite exhaust the stem; in sweetness, however, it is said to surpass all the others. There have lately come into fashion the cabbages known as the "Lacuturres;" they are grown in the valley of Aricia, where there was formerly a lake, now no longer in existence, and a tower which is still standing. The head of this cabbage is very large, and the leaves are almost without number, some of them being round and smooth, and others long and sinewy; indeed, there is no cabbage that runs to a larger head than this, with the sole exception of the Tritian variety, which has a head sometimes as much as a foot in thickness, and throws out its cymae the latest of all. In all kinds of cabbages, hoar-frost contributes very materially to their sweetness; but it is apt to be productive of considerable injury, if care is not taken to protect the pith by cutting them aslant. Those plants which are intended for seed are never cut. There is another kind, again, that is held in peculiar esteem, and which never exceeds the height of an herbaceous plant; it is known by the name of "halmyridia," from the circumstance of its growing on the sea-shore only. It will keep green and fresh during a long voyage even, if care is taken not to let it touch the ground from the moment that it is cut, but to put it into oil-vessels lately dried, and then to bung them so as to effectually exclude all air. There are some who are of opinion, that the plant will come to maturity all the sooner if some sea-weed is laid at the root when it is transplanted, or else as much pounded nitre as can be taken up with three fingers; and others, again, sprinkle the leaves with trefoil seed and nitre pounded together. Nitre, too, preserves the greenness of cabbage when cooked, a result which is equally ensured by the Apician mode of boiling, or in other words, by steeping the plants in oil and salt before they are cooked. There is a method of grafting vegetables by cutting the shoots and the stalk, and then inserting in the pith the seed.
results.translation: This variety [of cabbage] is thin at the root, increasing in thickness as it rises among the leaves, which are fewer in number and narrower; the great merit of this cabbage is its remarkable tenderness, although it is unable to withstand cold. The cabbage of Bruttium [modern Calabria], on the other hand, thrives all the better for cold; its leaves are remarkably large, the stalk thin, and the flavour pungent. The leaves of the Sabine cabbage, again, are so crisped as to excite wonder, and their thickness is such as to quite exhaust the stem; in sweetness, however, it is said to surpass all the others. There have lately come into fashion the cabbages known as the "Lacuturres" ["Lake-towers"]; they are grown in the valley of Aricia, where there was formerly a lake, now no longer in existence, and a tower which is still standing. The head of this cabbage is very large, and the leaves are almost without number, some of them being round and smooth, and others long and sinewy; indeed, there is no cabbage that runs to a larger head than this, with the sole exception of the Tritian variety, which has a head sometimes as much as a foot in thickness, and sends out its cymae [secondary shoots] the latest of all. In all kinds of cabbages, hoar-frost contributes very materially to their sweetness; but it is apt to cause considerable injury if care is not taken to protect the pith by cutting the plants at a slant. Those plants intended for seed are never cut. There is yet another kind, held in peculiar esteem, which never exceeds the height of an herbaceous plant; it is known by the name "halmyridia" [from the Greek hals, "sea"], owing to its growing only on the sea-shore. It will remain green and fresh even throughout a long voyage, provided that care is taken not to let it touch the ground from the moment it is cut, but instead to place it in oil-vessels that have recently been dried, and then to bung them so as to exclude all air effectively. Some authorities are of the opinion that the plant will come to maturity all the sooner if some sea-weed is laid at the root when it is transplanted, or else as much pounded nitre [potassium or sodium nitrate; here likely saltpetre or natron] as can be taken up with three fingers; and others, again, sprinkle the leaves with trefoil seed and nitre pounded together. Nitre, too, preserves the greenness of cabbage when cooked, a result equally ensured by the Apician method of boiling — that is to say, by steeping the plants in oil and salt before they are cooked. There is a method of grafting vegetables by cutting the shoots and the stalk, and then inserting seed into the pith.

entities_mentioned.name: Cabbage of Bruttium (Calabrian cabbage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as thriving in cold, with large leaves, thin stalk, and pungent flavour; not identified by modern botanists.

entities_mentioned.name: Sabine cabbage (Brassica oleracea Sabellica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica oleracea var. sabellica L. (fringed or curly cabbage / kale)
entities_mentioned.notes: Noted for its extremely crisped leaves and exceptional sweetness.

entities_mentioned.name: Lacuturres (Lake-towers cabbage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica oleracea var. gongylodes L. (kohlrabi / turnip-cabbage / rape-colewort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Grown in the valley of Aricia; named for a former lake and standing tower in the area. Very large head with numerous leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Tritian cabbage
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a variety of Brassica oleracea; possibly related to Lacuturrian cabbage per Fée
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having the largest head of all cabbages, up to a foot in thickness, and the last to produce cymae.

entities_mentioned.name: Halmyridia (sea-shore cabbage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crambe maritima L. (sea-kale); alternatively Calystegia soldanella (syn. Convolvulus soldanella) has been suggested
entities_mentioned.notes: Name derived from Greek hals (sea); grows only on the sea-shore; preserved fresh by storing in sealed, dried oil-vessels without touching the ground.

entities_mentioned.name: Nitre
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a cultivation amendment (pounded and applied to roots) and as a cooking additive to preserve the green colour of cabbage.

entities_mentioned.name: Trefoil seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Pounded with nitre and sprinkled on cabbage leaves as a cultivation or preservative treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea-weed
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended as a root amendment at transplanting to accelerate maturity of halmyridia cabbage.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XIX (or closely related book on garden plants), Chapter 41, concerning varieties of cabbage. The source text is already in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock & Riley), so this task involves cleaning, annotating, and structuring rather than translating from Latin. Footnote numbers (33–43) have been integrated into the cleaned text as contextual notes where relevant. The term 'cymae' (Latin plural of cyma) refers to the young shoots or sprouts of the cabbage plant. The 'Apician mode of boiling' refers to culinary practices attributed to the Roman gourmet Apicius. Fée's remark in the final footnote that this may represent the first germ of the practice leading to sauerkraut is noted but cut off in the source; it has been preserved in the translation to the extent the text allows. OCR error 'cyma3' corrected to 'cymae'; 'eifectually' corrected to 'effectively'; 'ofsour-krout (s' is a truncated footnote and has been left as-is in the cleaned original.

results.title_inferred: Wild and Cultivated Asparagus, and the Plant Lapsana
results.original_text: the triumphs of the late Emperor Julius, in consequence of the songs and jokes of his soldiers more particularly; for in the alternate lines sung by them, they used to reproach him for having made them live on lapsana at the siege of Dyrrhachium, and to rally him upon the parsimonious scale on which he was in the habit of recompensing their services. The lapsana is nothing more than a wild cyma. CHAP. 42. WILD AND CULTIVATED ASPARAGUS. Of all the garden plants, asparagus is the one that requires the most delicate attention in its cultivation. We have already spoken at considerable length of its origin, when treating of the wild plants, and have mentioned that Cato recommends it to be grown in reed-beds. There is another kind, again, of a more uncultivated nature than the garden asparagus, but less pungent than corruda; it grows upon the mountains in different countries, and the plains of Upper Germany are quite full of it, so much so, indeed, that it was a not unhappy remark of Tiberius Caesar, that a weed grows there which bears a remarkably strong resemblance to asparagus. That which grows spontaneously upon the island of Nesis, off the coast of Campania, is looked upon as being by far the best of all. Garden asparagus is reproduced from roots, the fibres of which are exceedingly numerous, and penetrate to a considerable depth. When it first puts forth its shoots, it is green; these in time lengthen out into stalks, which afterwards throw out streaked branches from the head: asparagus admits, also, of being grown from seed. Cato has treated of no subject with greater care than this, the last chapter of his work being devoted to it, from which we may conclude that it was quite new to him, and a subject which had only very recently occupied his attention. He recommends that the ground prepared for it should be a moist or dense soil, the seed being set at intervals of half a foot every way, to avoid treading upon the heads; the seed, he says, should be put two or three into each hole, these being made with the dibble as the line runs — for in his day, it should be remembered, asparagus was only grown from seed — this being done about the vernal equinox. It requires, he adds, to be abundantly manured, and to be kept well hoed, due care being taken not to pull up the young plants along with the weeds. The first year, he says, the plants must be protected from the severity of the winter with a covering of straw, care being taken to uncover them in the spring, and to hoe and stub up the ground about them. In the spring of the third year, the plants must be set fire to, and the earlier the period at which the fi[re is applied, the better].
results.translation:
The triumphs of the late Emperor Julius [Caesar] were celebrated in song by his soldiers, who, in their customary alternating verses, reproached him for having forced them to subsist on lapsana [a wild pot-herb] during the siege of Dyrrhachium, and mocked him for the meagre scale on which he habitually rewarded their services. Lapsana is nothing other than a wild cyma [wild cabbage-sprout].

CHAPTER 42. WILD AND CULTIVATED ASPARAGUS.

Of all garden plants, asparagus is the one requiring the most delicate care in cultivation. We have already spoken at some length of its origin when treating of wild plants, and have noted that Cato recommends growing it in reed-beds. There is another variety of a wilder character than the garden asparagus, yet less pungent than corruda [wild sperage]; it grows on mountains in various regions, and the plains of Upper Germany are so full of it that Tiberius Caesar aptly remarked that a weed grows there bearing a very strong resemblance to asparagus. That which grows spontaneously on the island of Nesis, off the coast of Campania, is considered by far the finest of all.

Garden asparagus is propagated from roots [sponges/rhizomes], whose fibres are exceedingly numerous and penetrate to a considerable depth. When it first sends forth its shoots they are green; these in time lengthen into stalks, which subsequently throw out streaked branches from the crown. Asparagus may also be grown from seed. Cato has treated no subject with greater care than this — the final chapter of his work being devoted to it — from which we may infer that it was quite new to him and had only recently come to his attention. He recommends that the ground prepared for it be moist or dense soil, with seeds set at half-foot intervals in every direction so as to avoid treading on the crowns. Two or three seeds, he says, should be placed in each hole made with the dibble along the row — for in his day, it should be remembered, asparagus was grown only from seed — and this should be done around the vernal equinox. The crop requires, he adds, abundant manuring and frequent hoeing, with care not to pull up young plants together with the weeds. In the first year, the plants must be protected from the harshness of winter with a covering of straw, which should be removed in spring, after which the ground around them should be hoed and broken up. In the spring of the third year the plants must be burned off, and the earlier this burning is carried out, the better [the results].

entities_mentioned.name: Lapsana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Crambe tataria (Tatar bread-plant) or Brassica napobrassica (rape-colewort), or common nipplewort (Lapsana communis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a wild cyma (wild cabbage-sprout); eaten by Caesar's soldiers during the siege of Dyrrhachium. Hardouin identifies it with common parsnip; Clusius and Cuvier with Crambe tataria; Fée with Brassica napobrassica. Could also be Lapsana communis (nipplewort), which bears the same name in modern botany.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wild cabbage-sprout, likely Brassica oleracea (wild form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a synonym or near-equivalent of lapsana; denotes a wild pot-herb of the cabbage family.

entities_mentioned.name: Asparagus (garden)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivated asparagus; Pliny follows Cato's instructions on cultivation from seed or root. Identified as Asparagus officinalis by Linnaeus and confirmed by footnote 46.

entities_mentioned.name: Corruda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus acutifolius L. or wild sperage (Asparagus officinalis var. sylvestris)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a wilder, more pungent form than garden asparagus; referred to in B. xvi. c. 67 and B. xx. c. 43.

entities_mentioned.name: Asparagus of the island of Nesis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus officinalis or a related wild Asparagus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Wild asparagus growing on the island of Nesis (Nisida) off Campania, regarded by Pliny as the finest variety.

results.translator_notes: This passage is a translated and lightly edited excerpt from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (Natural History), Book XIX, Chapters 41–42, as rendered in the Bostock & Riley Victorian English translation. The text is already in English; the task here is to produce a clean, scholarly rendering, which largely preserves the existing translation while correcting OCR artifacts (e.g., 'Dyrrhachium' restored, 'ASPAEAGUS' corrected to 'ASPARAGUS', 'AUD' corrected to 'AND', 'Ee Eust' in footnote restored to 'Re Rust.' for Cato's De Re Rustica). The segment ends mid-sentence ('the fi[re is applied]') due to a chunk boundary; the bracketed completion is supplied by editorial inference. Footnote references (44–49) have been incorporated as inline notes within the entities section or translator notes rather than reproduced as footnotes. The preparation method for asparagus per Cato: sow 2–3 seeds per dibble-hole at half-foot spacing in moist/dense soil around the vernal equinox; manure and hoe carefully; protect with straw in winter of year one; burn off in spring of year three.

results.title_inferred: Cultivation and Properties of Rocket, Nasturtium, Rue, Parsley, and Mint
results.original_text: say that it may be done in autumn as well, and recommend, when it is sown in winter, to drench the seed thoroughly with vinegar. Rocket, too, and nasturtium may be grown with the greatest facility either in summer or winter. Rocket, more particularly, is able to stand the cold, and its properties are quite different from those of the lettuce, as it is a great provocative of lust. Hence it is that we are in the habit of mixing these two plants in our dishes, the excess of cold in the one being compensated by the equal degree of heat in the other. Nasturtium has received that name from the smarting sensation which its pungency causes to the nostrils, and hence it is that a certain notion of smartness has attached itself to the word, it having become quite a proverbial saying, that a sluggish man should eat nasturtium, to arouse him from his torpidity. In Arabia, it is said, this plant attains a size that is quite marvellous. CHAP. 45. — RUE. Rue, too, is generally sown while the west winds prevail, as well as just after the autumnal equinox. This plant has an extreme aversion to cold, moisture, and dung; it loves dry, sunny localities, and a soil more particularly that is rich in brick clay; it requires to be nourished, too, with ashes, which should be mixed with the seed as well, as a preservative against the attacks of caterpillars. The ancients held rue in peculiar esteem; for I find that honied wine flavoured with rue was distributed to the people, in his consulship, by Cornelius Cethegus, the colleague of Quintus Flamininus, after the closing of the Comitia. This plant has a great liking for the fig-tree, and for that tree only; indeed, it never thrives better than when grown beneath that tree. It is generally grown from slips, the lower end of which is inserted in a perforated bean, which holds it fast, and so nurtures the young plant with its juices. It also reproduces itself; for the ends of the branches bending downwards, the moment they reach the ground, they take root again. Ocimum is of a very similar nature to rue, except that it dries with greater difficulty. When rue has once gained strength, there is considerable difficulty in stubbing it, as it causes itching ulcerations on the hands, if they are not covered or previously protected by being rubbed with oil. Its leaves, too, are preserved, being packed in bundles for keeping. CHAP. 46. PARSLEY. Parsley is sown immediately after the vernal equinox, the seed being lightly beaten first in a mortar. It is thought that, by doing this, the parsley will be all the more crisped, or else by taking care to beat it down when sown with a roller or the feet. It is a peculiarity of this plant, that it changes colour: it has the honour, in Achaia, of forming the wreath of the victors in the sacred contests of the Nemean Games. CHAP. 47. — MINT. It is at the same season, too, that mint is transplanted.
results.translation:
Some authorities say that sowing may be done in autumn as well, and recommend that when seed is sown in winter it should be thoroughly drenched with vinegar. Rocket [Brassica eruca L.] and nasturtium [Lepidium sativum L., garden cress or nosesmart] may likewise be cultivated with the greatest ease in either summer or winter. Rocket in particular is able to withstand cold, and its properties are quite unlike those of lettuce, being a powerful stimulant of lust [aphrodisiac]. For this reason we habitually mix these two plants in our dishes, the excessive cooling quality of the one being counterbalanced by the equal degree of heat in the other. Nasturtium has received its name from the smarting sensation ['quod nares torqueat'] which its pungency inflicts upon the nostrils; hence a certain notion of sharpness or smartness has come to attach itself to the word, and it has become a proverbial saying that a sluggish man ought to eat nasturtium in order to rouse himself from his torpidity. In Arabia, it is reported, this plant attains a truly marvellous size.

CHAPTER 45. — RUE.
Rue [Ruta graveolens L.] is generally sown while the west winds prevail, as well as immediately after the autumnal equinox. This plant has an extreme aversion to cold, moisture, and dung; it prefers dry, sunny localities and a soil particularly rich in brick clay. It also requires to be nourished with ashes, which should be mixed in with the seed as a preservative against the attacks of caterpillars. The ancients held rue in peculiar esteem; I find, for instance, that honied wine [mulsum] flavoured with rue was distributed to the people, during his consulship, by Cornelius Cethegus, the colleague of Quintus Flamininus, after the closing of the Comitia. This plant has a special affinity for the fig-tree, and for that tree alone; indeed, it never thrives better than when grown beneath one. Rue is generally propagated from slips, the lower end of each slip being inserted into a perforated bean, which holds it fast and nourishes the young plant with its juices. It also reproduces itself naturally, for the tips of the branches bend downward and, the moment they touch the ground, take root again. Ocimum [likely basil or a related herb; identity uncertain in this context] is of a very similar nature to rue, except that it dries with greater difficulty. Once rue has become well established, it is considerably troublesome to eradicate, as it causes itching ulcerations on the hands unless they are covered or previously protected by rubbing with oil. Its leaves are also preserved by being packed in bundles for storage.

CHAPTER 46. — PARSLEY.
Parsley is sown immediately after the vernal equinox, the seed having first been lightly beaten in a mortar. It is believed that doing this will make the parsley all the more curled [crisped], or alternatively the same effect is achieved by carefully beating the seed down after sowing with a roller or with the feet. A peculiarity of this plant is that it changes colour. In Achaia it has the distinction of forming the wreath placed upon the victors in the sacred contests of the Nemean Games.

CHAPTER 47. — MINT.
It is at the same season, too, that mint is transplanted.
