entities_mentioned.notes: Described as heat-generating and aphrodisiac; mixed with lettuce to balance humoral properties. Used as a food/condiment; also implicitly a stimulant remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Nasturtium (Nasturtium / garden cress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name derived from 'nares torqueat' (torments the nostrils). Used proverbially as a stimulant against torpidity/sluggishness. Grows remarkably large in Arabia according to Pliny.

entities_mentioned.name: Rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivation: sown when west winds prevail or after autumnal equinox; prefers dry, sunny, brick-clay soil; ashes mixed with seed to repel caterpillars. Propagated from slips inserted into perforated beans; also self-layers. Causes itching ulcerations on hands when uprooted; protection by oil recommended. Leaves preserved in bundles. Mixed into mulsum (honied wine) for public distribution by Cornelius Cethegus.

entities_mentioned.name: Ocimum
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as similar in nature to rue but drying with greater difficulty. In classical Latin 'ocimum' often refers to basil (Ocimum basilicum) or occasionally other aromatic herbs; context here is ambiguous.

entities_mentioned.name: Parsley
entities_mentioned.notes: Sown after vernal equinox; seed beaten in mortar to promote curled/crisped growth; used as victor's wreath at the Nemean Games in Achaia.

entities_mentioned.name: Mint
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as transplanted at the same spring season; discussion is cut off at the end of the segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Mulsum (honied wine flavoured with rue)
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation of wine and honey (mulsum) infused with rue, distributed publicly by Cornelius Cethegus during his consulship after the Comitia.

results.translator_notes: 1. The footnote reference to 'Quintus Elamininus' in the OCR is corrected to 'Quintus Flamininus' (the Roman consul T. Quinctius Flamininus, whose colleague was C. Cornelius Cethegus, consul 197 BCE). 2. 'Demean Games' in the OCR is an error for 'Nemean Games,' corrected silently. 3. The identity of 'Ocimum' in chapter 45 is ambiguous; in Pliny it may refer to basil or to a different aromatic herb — cross-reference with Book XX, chapter 48 is indicated in the footnotes. 4. The segment is drawn from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, chapters 44–47. 5. Translator's footnote 68 notes that rue (Ruta graveolens), though treated as a vegetable by the Romans, was by the time of this edition (19th c.) regarded as an active medicament of near-poisonous qualities.

results.title_inferred: On Mint, Cummin, Olusatrum (Alexanders), and Caper: Cultivation and Uses
results.original_text: For the purpose of separating the seeds, which are slightly joined together; and of disengaging a portion of the perisperm. At the present day this is not done, for fear of bursting the kernel of the seed. See B. xx. c. 53. [ap. 48] OLUSATRUM. if it has not yet germinated, the matted tufts of the old roots are used for the purpose. This plant, too, is no less fond of a humid soil than parsley; it is green in summer and turns yellow in winter. There is a wild kind of mint, known to us as "mentastrum:" it is reproduced by layers, like the vine, or else by planting the branches upside down. It was the sweetness of its smell that caused this plant to change its name among the Greeks, its former name with them being "mintha," from which the ancient Romans derived their name for it; whereas now, of late, it has been called by them ἡδύοσμον. The mint that is used in the dishes at rustic entertainments pervades the tables far and wide with its agreeable odour. When once planted, it lasts a considerable length of time; it bears, too, a strong resemblance to pennyroyal, a property of which is, as mentioned by us more than once, to flower when kept in our larders. These other herbs, mint, I mean, and catmint, as well as pennyroyal, are all kept for use in a similar manner; but it is cummin that is the best suited of all the seasoning herbs to squeamish and delicate stomachs. This plant grows on the surface of the soil, seeming hardly to adhere to it, and raising itself aloft from the ground: it ought to be sown in the middle of the summer, in a crumbly, warm soil, more particularly. There is another wild kind of cummin, known by some persons as "rustic," by others as "Thebaic" cummin: bruised and drunk in water, it is good for pains in the stomach. The cummin most esteemed in our part of the world is that of Carpetania, though elsewhere that of Africa and Ethiopia is more highly esteemed; with some, indeed, this last is preferred to that of Egypt. CHAP. 48. OLUSATRUM. But it is olusatrum, more particularly, that is of so singular a nature, a plant which by the Greeks is called "hipposelinum," and by others "smyrnium." This plant is reproduced from a tear-like gum which exudes from the stem; it is also grown from the roots as well. Those whose business it is to collect the juice of it, say that it has just the flavour of myrrh; and, according to Theophrastus, it is obtained by planting myrrh. The ancients recommended that hipposelinum should be grown in uncultivated spots covered with stones, and in the vicinity of garden walls; but at the present day it is sown in ground that has been twice turned up, between the prevalence of the west winds and the autumnal equinox. The caper, too, should be sown in dry localities more particularly, the plot being hollowed out and surrounded with an embankment of stones erected around it: if this precaution is not taken, it will spread all over the adjoining land, and entail sterility upon the soil. The caper blossoms in summer, and retains its [text continues].
results.translation:
For the purpose of separating the seeds, which are slightly joined together, and of disengaging a portion of the seed-coat [perisperm]. At the present day this is not done, for fear of bursting the kernel of the seed. [Cross-reference: Book XX, ch. 53.] If the plant has not yet germinated, the matted tufts of the old roots are used for the purpose. This plant is no less fond of a humid soil than parsley; it is green in summer and turns yellow in winter.

There is a wild kind of mint known to us as "mentastrum" [field mint / wild mint]: it is propagated by layers, like the vine, or else by planting the branches upside down. It was the sweetness of its smell that caused this plant to change its name among the Greeks—its former name with them being "mintha," from which the ancient Romans derived their own name for it—whereas now, of late, it has been called by them ἡδύοσμον ["sweet-smelling"]. The mint used in the dishes served at rustic entertainments pervades the tables far and wide with its agreeable odour. Once planted, it lasts for a considerable length of time; it also bears a strong resemblance to pennyroyal, a property of which—as we have mentioned more than once—is to flower even when kept in our larders. These herbs—mint, catmint, and pennyroyal alike—are all stored for use in a similar manner.

Of all the seasoning herbs, it is cummin [Cuminum cyminum] that is best suited to squeamish and delicate stomachs. This plant grows on the surface of the soil, seeming hardly to adhere to it, and raising itself aloft from the ground; it ought to be sown in the middle of summer, preferably in a crumbly, warm soil. There is also another wild kind of cummin, known by some persons as "rustic" and by others as "Thebaic" cummin: bruised and drunk in water, it is good for pains in the stomach. The cummin most esteemed in our part of the world is that of Carpetania [in Hispania Tarraconensis], though elsewhere the cummin of Africa and Ethiopia is more highly prized; with some, indeed, this last is preferred even to that of Egypt.

CHAPTER 48. OLUSATRUM.

But it is olusatrum [Alexanders, Smyrnium olusatrum]—"the black herb"—more particularly that is of so singular a nature. The Greeks call it "hipposelinum" and others call it "smyrnium." This plant is propagated from a tear-like gum that exudes from the stem, and it is also grown from the roots. Those whose business it is to collect its juice say that it has exactly the flavour of myrrh; and according to Theophrastus it is obtained by planting myrrh. The ancients recommended that hipposelinum be grown in uncultivated spots covered with stones and in the vicinity of garden walls; but at the present day it is sown in ground that has been twice turned over, between the time when the west winds prevail and the autumnal equinox.

The caper [Capparis spinosa], too, should be sown preferably in dry localities, the plot being hollowed out and surrounded by an embankment of stones erected around it; if this precaution is not taken, it will spread all over the adjoining land and render the soil sterile. The caper blossoms in summer, and retains its [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: catmint

entities_mentioned.name: pennyroyal

entities_mentioned.name: cummin (cuminum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Recommended for delicate stomachs; sown in mid-summer in warm, crumbly soil. Varieties include wild/rustic, Thebaic, Carpetanian, African, Ethiopian, and Egyptian.

entities_mentioned.name: wild / Thebaic cummin

entities_mentioned.name: olusatrum / hipposelinum / smyrnium
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 'black herb.' Propagated from stem exudate (tear-like gum) or from roots. Juice said to taste like myrrh. Theophrastus associated its propagation with myrrh planting. Sown between prevalence of west winds and autumnal equinox in twice-tilled ground.

entities_mentioned.name: caper
entities_mentioned.notes: Sown in dry ground; requires a stone embankment to prevent invasive spreading and soil exhaustion. Flowers in summer.

results.translator_notes: The segment is drawn from Book XIX of Pliny's Natural History and continues into Chapter 48 (on olusatrum). The Greek term rendered in the OCR source as 'uooy.cof' has been reconstructed as ἡδύοσμον ('sweet-smelling'), which is the standard Greek name for mint referenced here. The footnote numbering (76–87) has been retained and integrated. The word 'perisperm' is preserved as a technical botanical term meaning the nutritive tissue surrounding the embryo in a seed. The phrase 'Smyrnhium olusatrum of Linnaeus' in the footnote corrects the OCR rendering 'Smyrnhium' to 'Smyrnium.' OCR corruption 'rnintha' corrected to 'mintha'; 'smyrnium' retained from footnote context. The segment ends mid-sentence at 'retains its,' indicating the original chunk boundary.

results.title_inferred: Caraway, Lovage, Dittander, Gith, and Poppy: Culinary and Medicinal Plants
results.original_text:
Caraway is an exotic plant also, which derives its name, 'careum,' from the country in which it was first grown; it is principally employed for culinary purposes. This plant will grow in any kind of soil, and requires to be cultivated just the same way as olusatrum; the most esteemed, however, is that which comes from Caria, and the next best is that of Phrygia.

CHAP. 50. — LOVAGE.
Lovage grows wild in the mountains of Liguria, its native country, but at the present day it is grown everywhere. The cultivated kind is the sweetest of the two, but is far from powerful; by some persons it is known as 'panax.' Crateuas, a Greek writer, gives this name, however, to the plant known to us as 'cunila bubula'; and others, again, call the conyza or cunilago, cunila, while they call cunila, properly so called, by the name of 'thymbra.' With us cunila has another appellation, being generally known as 'satureia,' and reckoned among the seasoning plants. It is usually sown in the month of February, and for utility rivals wild marjoram. These two plants are never used together, their properties being so extremely similar; but it is only the wild marjoram of Egypt that is considered superior to cunila.

CHAP. 51. — DITTANDER.
Dittander, too, was originally an exotic plant: it is usually sown after the west winds have begun to prevail. As soon as it begins to shoot, it is cut down close to the ground, after which it is hoed and manured, a process which is repeated the succeeding year. After this, the shoots are fit for use, if the rigour of the winter has not injured them; for it is a plant quite unable to withstand any inclemency of the weather. It grows to the height of a cubit, and has a leaf like that of the laurel, but softer; it is never used except in combination with milk.

CHAP. 52. — GITH.
Gith is employed by bakers, dill and anise by cooks and medical men. Sacopenium, so extensively used for adulterating laser, is also a garden plant, but is only employed for medicinal purposes.

CHAP. 53. — THE POPPY.
There are certain plants which are grown in company with others, the poppy, for instance, sown with cabbages and purslain and rocket with lettuce. Of the cultivated poppy there are three kinds, the first being the white poppy, the seed of which, parched, and mixed with honey, used to be served up in [the second course at table].
results.translation:
Caraway is likewise an exotic plant, deriving its name 'careum' from the country in which it was first cultivated; it is employed principally for culinary purposes. This plant will grow in any kind of soil and requires cultivation in the same manner as olusatrum [horse-parsley, Smyrnium olusatrum]; the most esteemed variety, however, is that which comes from Caria [in Asia Minor], and the next best is that of Phrygia.

CHAPTER 50. — LOVAGE.
Lovage grows wild in the mountains of Liguria, its native country, but today it is cultivated everywhere. The cultivated kind is the sweeter of the two, but is far less potent; by some persons it is called 'panax.' The Greek writer Crateuas, however, gives this name to the plant we know as 'cunila bubula' [ox cunila, an uncertain labiate]; and others again apply the name 'cunila' to conyza or cunilago, while they call cunila properly so called by the name 'thymbra.' Among us, cunila goes by yet another name, being generally known as 'satureia' [savory], and is counted among the seasoning plants. It is usually sown in the month of February, and in usefulness rivals wild marjoram [oregano]. These two plants are never employed together, their properties being so extremely similar; yet it is only the wild marjoram of Egypt that is held superior to cunila.

CHAPTER 51. — DITTANDER.
Dittander likewise was originally an exotic plant; it is usually sown after the west winds have begun to prevail. As soon as it begins to shoot, it is cut down close to the ground, after which it is hoed and manured — a process repeated the following year. After this the shoots are fit for use, provided the severity of winter has not damaged them, for it is a plant quite unable to endure any inclemency of weather. It grows to the height of a cubit and has a leaf resembling that of the laurel but softer; it is never used except in combination with milk.

CHAPTER 52. — GITH.
Gith [black cumin] is employed by bakers; dill and anise are used by cooks and medical men alike. Sagapenum [sacopenium], so widely used for adulterating laser [silphium juice], is also a garden plant, though employed solely for medicinal purposes.

CHAPTER 53. — THE POPPY.
Certain plants are habitually grown in company with others — the poppy, for instance, is sown together with cabbages, and purslain and rocket with lettuce. Of the cultivated poppy there are three kinds, the first being the white poppy, the seeds of which, when parched and mixed with honey, used to be served up [as a second course at table].

results.translator_notes: This segment is a lightly edited OCR transcription of the Bostock and Riley translation of Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX. The text contains several interlocking naming disputes regarding cunila, thymbra, satureia, panax, and related plants, which Pliny himself acknowledges as confusing. The footnotes embedded in the OCR source (numbered 84–99 and 1–3) have been used to inform identifications but are not reproduced in the translation proper. Pliny's note that dittander's leaf resembles laurel is flagged by a translator footnote as incorrect. The passage on the poppy is incomplete (cut off mid-sentence in the source). 'Sacopenium' is corrected to 'sagapenum' following standard nomenclature.

results.title_inferred: Poppies, Autumnal-Equinox Plants, Mustard, Wild Thyme, and Sisymbrium
results.original_text:
parsley and gith to heighten the flavour of the flour. The second kind is black poppy, from which, upon an incision being made in the stalk a milky juice distils; and the third is that known to the Greeks by the name of "rhoeas," and by us as the wild poppy. This last grows spontaneously, but in fields particularly which have been sown with barley: it bears a strong resemblance to rocket, grows to the height of a cubit, and bears a red flower, which quickly fades; it is to this flower that it is indebted for its Greek name. As to the other kinds of poppies which spring up spontaneously, we shall have occasion to speak of them when treating of the medicinal plants. That the poppy has always been held in esteem among the Romans, we have a proof in the story related of Tarquinius Superbus, who, by striking down the tallest poppies in his garden, surreptitiously conveyed, unknown to them, his sanguinary message through the envoys who had been sent by his son.

CHAP. 54. OTHER PLANTS WHICH REQUIRE TO BE SOWN AT THE AUTUMNAL EQUINOX.

There are some other plants, again, which require to be sown together at the time of the autumnal equinox; coriander, for instance, anise, orage, mallows, lapathum, chervil, known to the Greeks as "paederos," and mustard, which has so pungent a flavour, that it burns like fire, though at the same time it is remarkably wholesome for the body. This last, though it will grow without cultivation, is considerably improved by being transplanted; though, on the other hand, it is extremely difficult to rid the soil of it when once sown there, the seed when it falls germinating immediately. This seed, when cooked in the saucepan, is employed even for making ragouts, its pungency being rendered imperceptible by boiling; the leaves, too, are boiled just the same way as those of other vegetables. There are three different kinds of mustard, the first of a thin, slender form, the second, with a leaf like that of the rape, and the third, with that of rocket: the best seed comes from Egypt. The Athenians have given mustard the name of "napy," others, "thapsi," and others, again, "saurion."

CHAP. 55. WILD THYME; SISYMBRIUM.

Most mountains abound with wild thyme and sisymbrium, those of Thrace, for example, where branches of these wild plants are torn up and brought away for planting. So, too, the people of Sicyon seek for wild thyme on their mountains.

That the poppy has always been held in esteem among the Romans is proved by the story of Tarquinius Superbus, who, by striking down the tallest poppies in his garden, surreptitiously conveyed, without their knowledge, his murderous instructions through the envoys sent to him by his son.

CHAPTER 54. OTHER PLANTS WHICH REQUIRE TO BE SOWN AT THE AUTUMNAL EQUINOX.

There are other plants, again, which require to be sown together at the time of the autumnal equinox: coriander, for instance, anise, orage [orache], mallows, lapathum [dock], chervil—known to the Greeks as "paederos" ["lad's love"]—and mustard, which has so pungent a flavour that it burns like fire, though at the same time it is remarkably wholesome for the body. This last, though it will grow without cultivation, is considerably improved by being transplanted; yet, on the other hand, it is extremely difficult to rid the soil of it once sown, the seed germinating immediately upon falling. The seed, when cooked in a saucepan, is employed even for making ragouts [stewed dishes], its pungency being rendered imperceptible by boiling; the leaves, too, are boiled in the same way as those of other vegetables. There are three different kinds of mustard: the first of a thin, slender form; the second, with a leaf like that of the rape; and the third, with a leaf like that of rocket. The best seed comes from Egypt. The Athenians have given mustard the name of "napy," others call it "thapsi," and others again "saurion."

CHAPTER 55. WILD THYME; SISYMBRIUM.

Most mountains abound with wild thyme and sisymbrium [water mint or a related aromatic herb], those of Thrace for example, where branches of these wild plants are torn up and carried away for planting. In the same way, the people of Sicyon seek wild thyme on their mountains.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny's Natural History, Books XIX–XX, covering cultivated and wild plants. The source text contains OCR artefacts (e.g., 'bv' for 'by', 'iis' for 'us', 'u' for 'in', 'neids' for 'fields', 'm t' for 'in the', 'paederos' footnoted as 'lad's lore' should read 'lad's love') which have been silently corrected where unambiguous. The footnotes embedded in the OCR source have been noted where relevant but not reproduced as part of the main text. The story of Tarquinius Superbus refers to the legend in Livy and Ovid wherein the king, forbidden from speaking openly, communicated his instructions by lopping off the heads of the tallest poppies. Preparation methods extracted: (1) poppy seed sprinkled over pastry; (2) milky latex from black poppy stalk incision (proto-opium extraction); (3) mustard seed boiled in a saucepan for ragouts; (4) mustard leaves boiled as a vegetable; (5) wild thyme and sisymbrium transplanted from mountain cuttings.

results.title_inferred: Ferulaceous Garden Plants: Hemp, Fennel-Giant, and Maladies of Garden Plants
results.original_text: perfection upon the walls of wells, and around fish preserves and ponds. CHAP. 56. (9.) FOUR KINDS OF FERULACEOUS PLANTS. HEMP. The other garden plants are of the ferulaceous kind, such as fennel, for instance, very grateful to serpents, as already stated, and used for numerous seasonings when dried; thapsia, too, which bears a close resemblance to fennel, and already mentioned by us when speaking of the exotic shrubs. Then, too, there is hemp, a plant remarkably useful for making ropes, and usually sown after the west winds have begun to prevail: the more thickly it is sown, the thinner are the stalks. The seed is gathered when ripe, just after the autumnal equinox, and is dried by the agency of the sun, the wind, or smoke. The hemp itself is plucked just after vintage-time, and is peeled and cleaned by the labourers at night. The best hemp is that of Alabanda, which is used more particularly for making hunting-nets, and of which there are three varieties. The hemp which lies nearest the bark or the pith, is the least valuable, while that which lies in the middle, and hence has the name of "mesa," is the most esteemed. The hemp of Mylasa occupies the second rank. With reference to the size to which it grows, that of Rosea, in the Sabine territory, equals the trees in height. We have already mentioned two kinds of fennel-giant when speaking of the exotic shrubs: the seed of it is used in Italy for food; the plant, too, admits of being preserved, and, if stored in earthen pots, will keep for a whole year. There are two parts of it that are used for this purpose, the upper stalks and the umbels of the plant. This kind of fennel is sometimes known by the name of "corymbia," and the parts preserved are called "corymbi." CHAP. 57. (10.) THE MALADIES OF GARDEN PLANTS. The garden plants, too, like the rest of the vegetable productions, are subject to certain maladies. Thus, for instance, ocimum, when old, degenerates into wild thyme, and sisymbrium into mint, while the seed of an old cabbage produces rape, and vice versa. Cummin, too, if not kept well hoed, is killed by haemodorum, a plant with a single stalk, a root similar to a bulb in appearance, and never found except in a thin, meagre soil. Besides this, cummin is liable to a peculiar disease of its own, the scab: ocimum, too, turns pale at the rising of the Dog-star. All plants, indeed, will turn of a yellow complexion on the approach of a woman who has the menstrual discharge upon her. There are various kinds of insects, too, that breed upon the garden plants — fleas, for instance, upon turnips, and caterpillars and maggots upon radishes, as well as lettuce.
results.translation:
These plants reach their finest development on the walls of wells, and around fish preserves and ponds.

CHAPTER 56. (9.) FOUR KINDS OF FERULACEOUS [fennel-stalked] PLANTS. HEMP.

The remaining garden plants belong to the ferulaceous [hollow-stalked] kind — fennel, for instance, which is, as already noted, very agreeable to serpents, and which is widely used as a dried seasoning; thapsia likewise, which closely resembles fennel and was already discussed in our treatment of exotic shrubs. There is also hemp [Cannabis sativa], a plant of remarkable utility for the making of ropes, and ordinarily sown after the west winds have begun to blow. The more thickly it is sown, the more slender the stalks become. The seed is gathered when ripe, just after the autumnal equinox, and is dried by the action of the sun, the wind, or smoke. The hemp plant itself is pulled just after the vintage, and is peeled and cleaned by the workers at night. The finest hemp is that from Alabanda, which is used chiefly for the making of hunting-nets; it comes in three varieties. The hemp nearest the bark or the pith is of least value, while that in the middle layer — from which it takes its name "mesa" [middle] — is the most highly prized. The hemp of Mylasa ranks second. As regards height of growth, the hemp of Rosea in the Sabine territory rivals trees in stature. We have already noted two kinds of fennel-giant [Ferula] in our account of the exotic shrubs: its seed is used as food in Italy, and the plant is also suitable for preservation; if stored in earthen pots it will keep for a whole year. Two parts of the plant are used for this purpose: the upper stalks and the umbels. This species of fennel is sometimes called "corymbia," and the preserved portions are called "corymbi."

CHAPTER 57. (10.) THE MALADIES OF GARDEN PLANTS.

Garden plants, like all other vegetable productions, are subject to certain diseases. For instance, ocimum [basil], when old, degenerates into wild thyme, and sisymbrium [water-cress or hedge-mustard] into mint, while seed from an old cabbage produces rape, and vice versa. Cummin, moreover, if not kept thoroughly hoed, is killed by haemodorum, a plant with a single stalk and a root resembling a bulb in appearance, found only in thin, poor soil. In addition, cummin is liable to a disease peculiar to itself — the scab. Ocimum also turns pale at the rising of the Dog-star. Indeed, all plants will take on a yellowish colour when approached by a woman who is in the course of her menstrual discharge. Various insects also breed upon garden plants: fleas upon turnips, and caterpillars and maggots upon radishes, as well as upon lettuce.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment is from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX, covering ferulaceous garden plants and plant diseases. The text is largely legible with minor OCR artifacts ('coryrabi' corrected to 'corymbi', 'Ferflaceotjs' corrected to 'ferulaceous' in the title). 2. The footnote references (numbers 17–31) have been preserved in the cleaned original for scholarly context. 3. Pliny's claims about plant 'degeneration' (ocimum→wild thyme, sisymbrium→mint, cabbage↔rape) reflect ancient misunderstanding of plant genetics and should be understood as folk-botanical belief. 4. The claim that hemp at Rosea grew to tree-height is flagged by Fée as erroneous, as hemp rarely exceeds modest dimensions. 5. The assertion that menstruating women cause plants to turn yellow is a widespread ancient superstition, not a medical or botanical fact, and is reproduced here faithfully as part of the primary source.

results.title_inferred: Remedies Against Garden Pests: Ants, Caterpillars, and Flies; Seed Preservation
results.original_text:
dedicated to Maecenas, that it is not advisable to touch rue, cunila, mint, or ocimum with any implement of iron. These absurd notions are borrowed from Theophrastus, De Causis, c. 8. Or, according to some readings, "limodorum," a parasitical plant, probably the Lathraea phelypea of Sprengel. Fee suggests that this plant may be the Polygonum convolvulus of Linnaeus, or else one of the Cuscutae, or a variety of Orobanche. "Scabies." A fungous excrescence, Fee thinks, now known as "puccinia," or "uredo."

CHAP. 58. THE PROPER REMEDIES FOR THESE MALADIES. HOW ANTS ARE BEST DESTROYED. THE BEST REMEDIES AGAINST CATERPILLARS AND FLIES.

The same author recommends as a remedy against ants, which are by no means the slightest plague in a garden that is not kept well watered, to stop up the mouths of their holes with sea-slime or ashes. But the most efficient way of destroying them is with the aid of the plant heliotropium; some persons, too, are of opinion that water in which an unburnt brick has been soaked is injurious to them. The best protection for turnips is to sow a few fitches with them, and for cabbages chick-peas, these having the effect of keeping away caterpillars. If, however, this precaution should have been omitted, and the caterpillars have already made their appearance, the best remedy is to throw upon the vegetables a decoction of wormwood, or else of house-leek, known to some as "aizoum," a kind of herb already mentioned by us. If cabbage-seed, before it is sown, is steeped in the juice of house-leek, the cabbages, it is said, are sure not to be attacked by any insect. It is said, too, that all caterpillars may be effectually exterminated, if the skull of a beast of burden is set up upon a stake in the garden, care being taken to employ that of a female only. There is a story related, too, that a river crab, hung up in the middle of the garden, is a preservative against the attacks of caterpillars. Again, there are some persons who are in the habit of touching with slips of blood-red cornel such plants as they wish to preserve from caterpillars. Flies, too, infest well-watered gardens, and more particularly so, if there happen to be any shrubs there; they may be got rid of, however, by burning galbanum. With reference to the deterioration to which seed is subject, there are some seeds which keep better than others, such, for instance, as that of coriander, beet, leeks, cresses, mustard, rocket, cunila, nearly all the pungent plants in fact.
results.translation:
…dedicated to Maecenas, that it is not advisable to touch rue, cunila [savory], mint, or ocimum [basil] with any implement of iron. These absurd notions are borrowed from Theophrastus, De Causis, chapter 8. Or, according to some manuscript readings, "limodorum," a parasitical plant, probably the Lathraea phelypea of Sprengel. Fee suggests that this plant may be the Polygonum convolvulus of Linnaeus, or else one of the Cuscutae [dodders], or a variety of Orobanche [broomrape]. "Scabies" [a fungous excrescence]: Fee thinks this is now known as "puccinia" or "uredo" [rust fungi].

CHAPTER 58. THE PROPER REMEDIES FOR THESE MALADIES. HOW ANTS ARE BEST DESTROYED. THE BEST REMEDIES AGAINST CATERPILLARS AND FLIES.

The same author [Columella] recommends, as a remedy against ants—which are by no means the least of the plagues in a garden that is not kept well watered—that the mouths of their holes be stopped up with sea-slime or ashes. But the most efficient way of destroying them is with the aid of the plant heliotropium [Heliotropium europaeum]; some persons, moreover, are of the opinion that water in which an unburnt brick has been soaked is harmful to them. The best protection for turnips is to sow a few fitches [bitter vetches, Vicia ervilia] with them, and for cabbages to sow chick-peas alongside them, as these have the effect of keeping caterpillars away. If, however, this precaution has been omitted and the caterpillars have already appeared, the best remedy is to pour over the vegetables a decoction of wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], or else of house-leek [Sempervivum tectorum], known to some as "aizoum," a herb already mentioned by us. If cabbage-seed, before it is sown, is steeped in the juice of house-leek, the cabbages, it is said, will certainly not be attacked by any insect. It is also said that all caterpillars may be effectually exterminated if the skull of a beast of burden is set up upon a stake in the garden, care being taken to use only that of a female animal. There is also a story that a river crab, hung up in the middle of the garden, is a preservative against caterpillar attacks. Furthermore, there are those who are in the habit of touching with slips of blood-red cornel [Cornus sanguinea] such plants as they wish to keep free from caterpillars. Flies [culices, including both flies and gnats] also infest well-watered gardens, and especially so if there happen to be any shrubs growing there; they may be driven away, however, by burning galbanum [resin of Ferula galbaniflua]. Regarding the deterioration to which seed is subject, there are some seeds that keep better than others—for instance, those of coriander, beet, leeks, cresses, mustard, rocket, cunila [savory], and nearly all of the pungent-flavored plants.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a continuation of Book XIX and comprises scholarly footnotes interspersed with the main Pliny text; the footnotes (attributed to Fee and others) have been retained as part of the cleaned original and translated accordingly. The chapter heading (Chap. 58) and its treatment of garden pest remedies form the primary content. The passage on seed deterioration at the end transitions into what would be Chapter 59 or 60. The remedy involving a female beast-of-burden skull is acknowledged by the annotator as superstition, though he notes that insects may collect in such objects. The river-crab remedy is similarly folkloric. The close parallel with Theophrastus, Hist. Plant. B. vii. c. 6, is noted for the seed-keeping passage.

results.title_inferred: On Plants Benefitted by Salt or Fresh Water, Proper Watering Methods, and the Properties of Cultivated vs. Wild Plants
results.original_text: CHAP. 59. WHAT PLANTS ARE BENEFITTED BY SALT WATER. A peculiar remedy for the maladies to which radishes, beet, rue, and cunila are subject, is salt water, which has also the additional merit of conducing very materially to their sweetness and fertility. Other plants, again, are equally benefitted by being watered with fresh water, the most desirable for the purpose being that which is the coldest and the sweetest to drink: pond and drain-water, on the other hand, are not so good, as they are apt to carry the seeds of weeds along with them. It is rain, however, that forms the principal aliment of plants; in addition to which, it kills the insects as they develope themselves upon them. CHAP. 60. (12.) THE PROPER METHOD OF WATERING GARDENS. The proper times for watering are the morning and the evening, to prevent the water from being heated by the sun; with the sole exception, however, of ocimum, which requires to be watered at midday; indeed, this plant, it is generally thought, will grow with additional rapidity, if it is watered with boiling water when sown. All plants, when transplanted, grow all the better and larger for it, leeks and turnips more particularly. Transplanting, too, is attended with certain remedial effects, and acts as a preservative to certain plants, such as scallions, for instance, leeks, radishes, parsley, lettuces, rape, and cucumbers. All the wild plants are generally smaller in the leaf and stalk than the cultivated ones, and have more acrid juices, cunila, wild marjoram, and rue, for example. Indeed, it is only the lapathum that is better in a wild state than cultivated: in its cultivated state it is the same plant that is known to us as the "rumix," being the most vigorous by far of all the plants that are grown; so much so, indeed, that it is said that when it has once taken root, it will last for ever, and can never be extirpated from the soil, more particularly if water happens to be near at hand. Its juices, which are employed only in ptisans, as an article of food, have the effect of imparting to them a softer and more exquisite flavour. The wild variety is employed for many medicinal purposes. So true it is, that the careful research of man has omitted nothing, that I have even met with a poem, in which I find it stated, that if pellets of goats' dung, the size of a bean, are hollowed out, and the seed of leeks, rocket, lettuces, parsley, endive, and cresses is inserted in them, and then sown, the plants will thrive in a marvellous degree. Plants in a wild state, it is generally thought, are more dry and acrid than when cultivated. CHAP. 61. THE JUICES AND FLAVOURS OF GARDEN [PLANTS].
results.translation:
CHAPTER 59. WHAT PLANTS ARE BENEFITTED BY SALT WATER. A particular remedy for the diseases to which radishes, beet, rue, and cunila [savory] are prone is salt water, which also has the additional benefit of greatly contributing to their sweetness and fertility. Other plants are equally benefitted by being watered with fresh water, the most desirable of which is the coldest and sweetest to drink; pond water and drain water, on the other hand, are less suitable, as they tend to carry the seeds of weeds with them. It is rainwater, however, that constitutes the principal nourishment of plants; it also has the effect of killing insects as they develop upon the plants.

CHAPTER 60. THE PROPER METHOD OF WATERING GARDENS. The proper times for watering are morning and evening, to prevent the water from being heated by the sun; the sole exception is ocimum [basil], which requires watering at midday; indeed, this plant is generally thought to grow with greater speed if watered with boiling water at the time of sowing. All plants, when transplanted, grow better and larger for the process, leeks and turnips most especially. Transplanting also carries certain restorative effects and acts as a preservative for certain plants, such as scallions, leeks, radishes, parsley, lettuces, rape, and cucumbers. All wild plants are generally smaller in leaf and stalk than cultivated ones, and possess more acrid juices—cunila [savory], wild marjoram, and rue being examples. Indeed, it is only lapathum [dock] that is superior in its wild state to its cultivated form: in cultivation, it is the same plant known to us as 'rumix' [sorrel/dock], being by far the most vigorous of all cultivated plants; so much so that it is said once it has taken root it will persist forever and can never be eradicated from the soil, especially when water is close at hand. Its juices, used solely in ptisans [barley-gruel decoctions] as a foodstuff, impart to them a softer and more refined flavour. The wild variety is employed for many medicinal purposes. So thoroughly has human inquiry left nothing unexamined, that I have even encountered a poem stating that if pellets of goats' dung the size of a bean are hollowed out, and seeds of leeks, rocket, lettuces, parsley, endive, and cresses are placed inside them and then sown, the plants will thrive to a marvellous degree. Plants in a wild state are generally considered to be more dry and acrid than those that are cultivated.

CHAPTER 61. THE JUICES AND FLAVOURS OF GARDEN [PLANTS].

results.translator_notes: The source text is a 19th-century English translation of Pliny's Naturalis Historia (Book XIX), not a Latin original, so this is a translation of a translation. OCR errors silently corrected include 'BENEF1TTTED' → 'BENEFITTED' and 'PKOPKtt' → 'PROPER'. The footnotes (numbered 42–52 in the source) have been retained in the cleaned original text as contextual annotations but are not incorporated into the translation body. 'Ptisans' [ptisanae] refers to medicinal or nourishing gruel/decoctions, typically barley-based. The claim about mummy-wheat germinating after 2,000 years (footnote 42) is noted by the original translator as a known popular belief but is not scientifically valid. The goat-dung pellet sowing method is a folk-agronomic technique cited from an unnamed poem known to Pliny.

results.translation:
Chapter 62. Piperitis, Libanotis, and Smyrnium.

[Continuation of the discussion of flavours in plants:] centaury [taury] is bitter; cucumbers, gourds, and lettuces are watery; parsley, anise, and fennel are pungent and odoriferous. The salt flavour is the only one not found in plants, with the sole exception of the chicheling vetch [Lathyrus sativus], and even there it is found only on the exterior surface of the plant, in the form of a kind of dust that settles upon it.

In order to arrive at a full understanding here, as elsewhere, of how unfounded the notions commonly held tend to be, I shall take this opportunity to remark that panax has the flavour of pepper, and siliquastrum [pepper-wort] even more so — a circumstance to which it owes its name piperitis ["pepper-plant"]. Libanotis, for its part, has precisely the odour of frankincense, and smyrnium that of myrrh. Regarding panax, we have already spoken of it at sufficient length. Libanotis grows in thin, crumbly soil, and is generally sown in spots exposed to the falling dews; its root, which closely resembles that of olusatrum [alexanders], has a smell in no way different from that of frankincense; when one year old, it is extremely beneficial to the stomach. Some give it the name rosmarinum [rosemary, or "sea-dew"]. Smyrnium is a garden herb that grows in similar soils and has a root smelling of myrrh; siliquastrum likewise is cultivated in a similar manner. Still other plants differ from the foregoing both in smell and taste — anise, for example — and so great is the difference in this regard, and in their relative virtues, that not only are the properties of each modified by the other, but they are even entirely neutralized. It is in this way that cooks correct the sharpness of vinegar in their dishes by means of parsley, and that household stewards [butlers] use the same plant, enclosed in small bags [sachets], to remove an unpleasant odour from wine.

Thus far we have treated of garden plants considered solely as articles of food. It now remains for us — since up to this point we have spoken only of the various methods of their cultivation and some brief details connected therewith — to enlarge upon the more elaborate workings of Nature in this domain; for it is quite impossible to arrive at a full understanding of the true characteristics of each individual plant without a knowledge of its medicinal effects, which represent a sublime and truly mysterious manifestation of divine wisdom, beyond which nothing of a more exalted nature can be found. It is on principle that we have judged it proper not to treat of the medicinal properties of each plant when discussing [its cultivation and use as food] ...

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XIX, Chapter 62. The text as given is already an English translation (by John Bostock and H.T. Riley, Bohn's Classical Library edition) with editorial footnotes interleaved; it is not a fresh Latin source. The segment opens mid-sentence, suggesting it is a continuation from the preceding chunk. Footnote numbers in the original are inconsistent (e.g., 52 is skipped, 53 used in its place), likely reflecting OCR artefacts from the printed edition. The term 'rosmarinum' applied to libanotis is a point of botanical ambiguity: Pliny notes this as an alternative name used by some, but modern editors generally distinguish libanotis (a umbelliferous plant) from rosemary. The medicinal extractions below apply primarily to libanotis.

results.title_inferred: The Wild Cucumber and the Preparation of Elaterium: Twenty-Six Remedies
results.original_text:
descending from the contemplation of a loftier sphere, that the loadstone possesses the property of attracting iron, and another stone, again, that of repelling it; and that the diamond, that pride of luxury and opulence, though infrangible by every other object, and presenting a resistance that cannot be overcome, is broken asunder by a he-goat's blood — in addition to numerous other marvels of which we shall have to speak on more appropriate occasions, equal to this or still more wonderful even. My only request is that pardon may be accorded me for beginning with objects of a more humble nature, though still so greatly conducive to our health — I mean the garden plants, of which I shall now proceed to speak.

CHAP. 2. (1.) THE WILD CUCUMBER; TWENTY-SIX REMEDIES.

We have already stated that there is a wild cucumber, considerably smaller than the cultivated one. From this cucumber the medicament known as 'elaterium' is prepared, being the juice extracted from the seed. To obtain this juice the fruit is cut before it is ripe — indeed, if this precaution is not taken at an early period, the seed is apt to spirt out and be productive of danger to the eyes. After it is gathered, the fruit is kept whole for a night, and on the following day an incision is made in it with a reed. The seed, too, is generally sprinkled with ashes, with the view of retaining in it as large a quantity of the juice as possible. When the juice is extracted, it is received in rain water, where it falls to the bottom; after which it is thickened in the sun, and then divided into lozenges.
results.translation:
Descending from the contemplation of a loftier sphere: the loadstone [magnet] possesses the property of attracting iron, while another stone [the theamedes] has the property of repelling it; and the diamond, that pride of luxury and opulence, though unbreakable by every other means and presenting a resistance that cannot be overcome, is nonetheless shattered by the blood of a he-goat — in addition to numerous other marvels of which we shall have occasion to speak more appropriately elsewhere, marvels equal to these or still more wonderful. My only request is that pardon may be granted me for beginning with objects of a more humble nature, though still greatly conducive to our health — I mean the garden plants, of which I shall now proceed to speak.

CHAPTER 2. (1.) THE WILD CUCUMBER; TWENTY-SIX REMEDIES.

We have already stated that there exists a wild cucumber, considerably smaller than the cultivated one. From this cucumber the medicament known as 'elaterium' [elaterium] is prepared, being the juice extracted from the seed. To obtain this juice, the fruit is cut before it is ripe — indeed, if this precaution is not observed early enough, the seed is apt to spurt out and cause danger to the eyes. After being gathered, the fruit is kept whole overnight, and on the following day an incision is made in it with a reed. The seed is also generally sprinkled with ashes, with the aim of retaining as large a quantity of juice within it as possible. When the juice is extracted, it is collected in rain water, where it settles to the bottom; after which it is thickened in the sun and then divided into lozenges.

entities_mentioned.name: Loadstone
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as attracting iron; see Pliny, Natural History, Book XXXIV, c. 42.

entities_mentioned.name: Theamedes (stone that repels iron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as repelling iron, in contrast to the loadstone. See Pliny, Book XXXVI, c. 25.

entities_mentioned.name: Diamond
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny repeats the claim that diamond can be shattered by he-goat's blood, described by the footnote commentator as a singularly absurd notion unique to Pliny.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild Cucumber
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ecballium elaterium (L.) A.Rich. (squirting cucumber); formerly Momordica elaterium L. or Cucumis silvestris asininus of C. Bauhin
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny's 'wild cucumber' is not a true cucumber but the squirting cucumber. Fee notes Pliny has conflated two sources regarding whether elaterium is from the seed or the fruit itself; Dioscorides (Book IV, c. 154) gives a more accurate account.

entities_mentioned.name: Elaterium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried juice/extract of Ecballium elaterium; used historically as a strong cathartic/purgative
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared by cutting unripe fruit, collecting exuded juice in rain water, allowing it to settle, thickening in the sun, and forming into lozenges. Fee notes the name likely derives from the plant's strong purgative action rather than from the spurting of the seed (ἐλαύνω, 'to drive'). Condition treated: implied strong purgative use, consistent with Galen's testimony.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (Natural History), Book XX, Chapter 2, concerning the wild cucumber and the preparation of elaterium. The footnotes are those of the 19th-century English editor/translator (likely John Bostock and H.T. Riley) and commentary by Fee. The claim that diamonds are broken by he-goat's blood is noted as unique to Pliny and regarded as fabulous. The footnote text concerning elaterium's etymology is cut off mid-sentence at 'wa' — this is an OCR truncation of what is likely the Greek word ἐλαύνω or a continuation of Galen's testimony. The OCR error 'brokeu' has been silently corrected to 'broken'; 'lt elaterium' corrected to 'elaterium' (OCR artifact for open-quote); 'tractiug' corrected to 'attracting'; 'lie-goat' corrected to 'he-goat'; 'CUCUMBEK' and 'KEMEDIES' corrected to 'CUCUMBER' and 'REMEDIES'.

results.title_inferred: Wild Cucumber, Elaterium, and Their Medicinal Uses
results.original_text: wild cucumber, boiled in vinegar, is employed in fomentations for the gout, and the juice of it is used as a remedy for tooth-ache. Dried and mixed with resin, the root is a cure for impetigo and the skin diseases known as "psora" and "lichen:" it is good, too, for imposthumes of the parotid glands and inflammatory tumours, and restores the natural colour to the skin when a cicatrix has formed. — The juice of the leaves, mixed with vinegar, is used as an injection for the ears, in cases of deafness. CHAP. 3. ELATERIUM; TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES. The proper season for making elaterium is the autumn; and there is no medicament known that will keep longer than this. It begins to be fit for use when three years old; but if it is found desirable to make use of it at an earlier period than this, the acridity of the lozenges may be modified by putting them with vinegar upon a slow fire, in a new earthen pot. The older it is the better, and before now, as we learn from Theophrastus, it has been known to keep so long as two hundred years. Even after it has been kept so long as fifty years, it retains its property of extinguishing a light; indeed, it is the proper way of testing the genuineness of the drug to hold it to the flame and make it scintillate above and below, before finally extinguishing it. The elaterium which is pale, smooth, and slightly bitter, is superior to that which has a grass-green appearance and is rough to the touch. It is generally thought that the seed of this plant will facilitate conception if a woman carries it attached to her person, before it has touched the ground; and that it has the effect of aiding parturition, if it is first wrapped in ram's wool, and then tied round the woman's loins, without her knowing it, care being taken to carry it out of the house the instant she is delivered. The seed, it is said, is very similar in appearance to the tail of a scorpion thrown back, but is of a whitish hue. Indeed, there are some persons who give to this cucumber the name of "scorpionium," and say that its seed, as well as the elaterium, is remarkably efficacious as a cure for the sting of the scorpion. results.translation:
Wild cucumber, boiled in vinegar, is employed in fomentations [warm topical applications] for gout, and its juice is used as a remedy for toothache. When dried and mixed with resin, the root is a cure for impetigo [a contagious skin infection] and the skin diseases known as 'psora' [itch-scab] and 'lichen' [a scabrous skin condition resembling moss or lichen in appearance]. It is also beneficial for abscesses [imposthumes] of the parotid glands and inflammatory tumours [pani], and it restores the natural colour of the skin after a scar [cicatrix] has formed. The juice of the leaves, mixed with vinegar, is used as an ear injection in cases of deafness.

CHAPTER 3. ELATERIUM; TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES.

The proper season for preparing elaterium is autumn, and no medicament is known to keep longer than this. It begins to be fit for use at three years of age; but if one wishes to use it sooner, the acridity of the lozenges may be reduced by heating them slowly with vinegar in a new earthen pot over a low fire. The older it is, the better — as we learn from Theophrastus, it has been known to keep for as long as two hundred years. Even after fifty years of storage it retains its property of extinguishing a flame; indeed, the proper method of testing the genuineness of the drug is to hold it to a flame and make it scintillate above and below before it finally extinguishes the light. Elaterium that is pale, smooth, and slightly bitter is superior to that which appears grass-green and is rough to the touch.

It is generally believed that the seed of this plant will facilitate conception if a woman carries it on her person before it has touched the ground, and that it aids parturition [childbirth] if first wrapped in ram's wool and tied around the woman's loins without her knowledge — care being taken to remove it from the house the instant she has been delivered.

Those who speak most highly of the wild cucumber say that the best variety comes from Arabia, followed by that of Arcadia, and then that of Cyrene. It is said to resemble the heliotropium, and the fruit — about the size of a walnut — grows between the leaves and branches. The seed is said to closely resemble the curled-back tail of a scorpion, but is of a whitish colour. Some persons call this cucumber 'scorpionium,' and maintain that both its seed and the elaterium prepared from it are remarkably effective as a cure for scorpion stings. As a pu[rgative — text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: wild cucumber
entities_mentioned.notes: The source of elaterium; called 'scorpionium' by some due to seed resemblance to a scorpion's tail. Described as native to Arabia, Arcadia, and Cyrene in varying quality.

entities_mentioned.name: elaterium
entities_mentioned.notes: A concentrated medicament prepared from the juice of the squirting cucumber in autumn; noted for exceptional longevity, flame-extinguishing property used as authenticity test, and use as a purgative and remedy for scorpion stings.

entities_mentioned.name: heliotropium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heliotropium europaeum or related Heliotropium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a visual comparator for the wild cucumber plant's appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: psora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scabies or a similar pruritic skin condition
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as 'itch-scab' in the footnote; a broad ancient term for scaly/itchy skin diseases.

entities_mentioned.name: lichen
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lichen planus or a similar chronic skin eruption
entities_mentioned.notes: Ancient dermatological term for a scabrous skin condition in which the lesion resembles moss or lichen; does not map cleanly onto any single modern diagnosis.

entities_mentioned.name: impetigo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Impetigo (superficial bacterial skin infection) or ringworm/tinea (as suggested by the footnote equating it with 'tetter')
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote identifies this as a 'sort of tetter or ring-worm'; Celsus enumerates four varieties. The ancient term may cover several distinct modern conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: panus (inflammatory tumours / imposthumes of the parotid glands)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Superficial spreading abscess or bubo; possibly parotitis
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote defines 'panus' as a wide-spreading but not deeply-seated tumour with a blistered surface.

results.translator_notes: This segment is already in English — it is a 19th-century English translation of Pliny's Naturalis Historia (Book XX). The task here is to clean, annotate, and structure it rather than translate from Latin. The text breaks off mid-word ('pu[rgative]') at the end of the chunk. Footnotes from the source edition have been preserved as inline notes or entity notes where relevant. Fee's skeptical commentary on the longevity of elaterium (footnote 15) is noteworthy from a pharmacological standpoint. The magical/ritual uses of the seed for conception and parturition are ethnobotanically significant. The flame-extinguishing authenticity test for elaterium is a distinctive quality-control procedure worth flagging for historians of pharmacy.

results.title_inferred: Anguine/Erratic Cucumber, Cultivated Cucumber, and Pepones: Medicinal Remedies (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 4–6)
results.original_text: the cure of quinsy and affections of the trachea. CHAP. 4. (2.) THE ANGUINE OR ERRATIC CUCUMBER: FIVE REMEDIES. Many authors are of opinion that the wild cucumber is identical with the plant known among us as the "anguine," and by some persons as the "erratic" cucumber. Objects sprinkled with a decoction of this plant will never be touched by mice. The same authors say, too, that a decoction of it in vinegar, externally applied, gives instantaneous relief in cases of gout and diseases of the joints. As a remedy, too, for lumbago, the seed of it is dried in the sun and pounded, being given in doses of twenty denarii to half a sextarius of water. Mixed with woman's milk and applied as a liniment, it is a cure for tumours which have suddenly formed. Elaterium promotes the menstrual discharge; but if taken by females when pregnant, it is productive of abortion. It is good, also, for asthma, and, injected into the nostrils, for the jaundice. Rubbed upon the face in the sun, it removes freckles [lentigo] and spots upon the skin. CHAP. 5. — THE CULTIVATED CUCUMBER: NINE REMEDIES. Many persons attribute all these properties to the cultivated cucumber as well, a plant which even without them would be of very considerable importance, in a medicinal point of view. A pinch of the seed, for instance, in three fingers, beaten up with cummin and taken in wine, is extremely beneficial for a cough: for phrenitis, also, doses of it are administered in woman's milk, and doses of one acetabulum for dysentery. As a remedy for purulent expectorations, it is taken with an equal quantity of cummin; and it is used with hydromel for diseases of the liver. Taken in sweet wine, it is a diuretic; and, in combination with cummin, it is used as an injection for affections of the kidneys. CHAP. 6. PEPONES: ELEVEN REMEDIES. The fruit known as pepones are a cool and refreshing diet, and are slightly relaxing to the stomach. Applications are used of the pulpy flesh in defluxions or pains of the eyes. The root, too, of this plant cures the hard ulcers known to
results.translation:
— for the cure of quinsy [angina] and affections of the trachea.

CHAPTER 4. (2.) THE ANGUINE OR ERRATIC CUCUMBER: FIVE REMEDIES.

Many authors are of the opinion that the wild cucumber is identical with the plant known among us as the 'anguine,' and by some persons as the 'erratic' cucumber. Objects sprinkled with a decoction of this plant will never be touched by mice. The same authors also state that a decoction of it in vinegar, applied externally, gives instantaneous relief in cases of gout and diseases of the joints. As a remedy for lumbago, the seed is dried in the sun and pounded, then given in doses of twenty denarii [by weight] to half a sextarius of water. Mixed with woman's milk and applied as a liniment, it cures tumours that have formed suddenly. Elaterium [the dried juice of the wild cucumber] promotes the menstrual discharge; but if taken by pregnant women, it causes abortion. It is also beneficial for asthma, and, when injected into the nostrils, for jaundice [morbus regius, literally 'the royal disease']. Rubbed upon the face in the sun, it removes freckles [lentigo] and blemishes of the skin.

CHAPTER 5. THE CULTIVATED CUCUMBER: NINE REMEDIES.

Many persons attribute all these same properties to the cultivated cucumber as well, a plant which, even setting these aside, would be of very considerable medicinal importance. A pinch of the seed taken in three fingers, beaten up with cummin and taken in wine, is extremely beneficial for a cough. For phrenitis [inflammation of the mind/brain fever], doses are administered in woman's milk; and doses of one acetabulum are given for dysentery. As a remedy for purulent expectorations [suppurative cough], it is taken with an equal quantity of cummin; and it is used with hydromel [honey-water] for diseases of the liver. Taken in sweet wine, it acts as a diuretic; and, in combination with cummin, it is used as an injection [enema or instillation] for affections of the kidneys.

CHAPTER 6. PEPONES: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The fruit known as pepones [a type of large melon or gourd] make a cool and refreshing food, and are mildly relaxing to the stomach. Applications of the pulpy flesh are used for defluxions [discharges/inflammations] or pains of the eyes. The root of this plant also cures the hard ulcers known as — [text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: Anguine or Erratic Cucumber (cucumis anguinus / erraticus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cucumis flexuosus L. (snake cucumber) or an unidentified indigenous species; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Fee disputes the identification with Cucumis flexuosus, noting it originates from India and was likely unknown to the ancients, and has no medicinal properties. Treats: gout, joint diseases, lumbago, sudden tumours, amenorrhoea, abortion (abortifacient), asthma, jaundice, freckles/skin spots, and repels mice.

entities_mentioned.name: Elaterium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The dried juice/extract of Ecballium elaterium (L.) A.Rich. (squirting cucumber)
entities_mentioned.notes: A powerful cathartic and emmenagogue derived from the wild cucumber. Used for promoting menstruation, abortion, asthma, and jaundice (instilled into nostrils). Preparation: dried expressed juice of the wild cucumber fruit.

entities_mentioned.name: Cultivated Cucumber (cucumis sativus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Treats: cough (seed beaten with cummin in wine), phrenitis (in woman's milk), dysentery (one acetabulum dose), purulent expectoration (with cummin), liver disease (with hydromel), as diuretic (in sweet wine), kidney affections (injection with cummin). Preparation methods: seed ground with cummin, administered in wine, woman's milk, or hydromel.

entities_mentioned.name: Cummin (cuminum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjunct with cucumber seed in multiple preparations. Fee notes the combination is pharmacologically contradictory, as cucumber seed is soothing and cummin is moderately stimulating.

entities_mentioned.name: Pepones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Cucumis melo L. (muskmelon) or a large gourd/melon variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as cool, refreshing, and mildly laxative. Pulpy flesh applied to eye inflammations; root treats hard ulcers (text breaks off). Preparation: topical application of pulp; root used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild Cucumber
entities_mentioned.notes: Discussed in prior chapter context; source of elaterium. Treats quinsy and tracheal affections (preceding context).

results.translator_notes: The source text is already in English (a 19th-century translation of Pliny's Latin Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 4–6), with editorial footnotes interspersed throughout. The 'translation' here represents a cleaned, clarified, and annotated rendering distinguishing main text from footnote content. Footnote material from 'Fee' (the French commentator Henri-Marie Ducrotay de Blainville / Antoine Étienne Renaud Augustin Serres — more likely referring to Antoine Laurent de Jussieu or the annotator J.-É. Féé, i.e., Félix-Archimède Pouchet, or most probably Antoine Augustin Féé, 1789–1874, French botanist) has been silently separated from the main text. The text breaks off mid-sentence in Chapter 6 at 'the hard ulcers known to,' indicating the segment is incomplete. Measures noted: 'denarii' here refers to a unit of weight (the denarius weight, approx. 3.4 g); 'sextarius' is approx. 546 mL; 'acetabulum' is approx. 68 mL. 'Morbus regius' (royal disease) = jaundice. 'Phrenitis' = ancient term for acute brain fever with delirium. 'Hydromel' = a mixture of honey and water.

results.title_inferred: Remedies from Cucumbers, Gourds, Somphos, and Colocynthis (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 6–8)
results.original_text:
a result which is equally produced by an external application of the leaves of all the cultivated cucumbers. These leaves, mixed with honey, are employed for the cure of the pustules known as "epinyctis;" steeped in wine, they are good, too, for the bites of dogs and of multipedes, insects known to the Greeks by the name of "seps," of an elongated form, with hairy legs, and noxious to cattle more particularly; the sting being followed by swelling, and the wound rapidly putrifying. The smell of the cucumber itself is a restorative in fainting fits. It is a well-known fact, that if cucumbers are peeled and then boiled in oil, vinegar, and honey, they are all the more pleasant eating for it.

CHAP. 7. (3.) — THE GOURD: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES. THE SOMPHOS: ONE REMEDY.
There is found also a wild gourd, called "somphos" by the Greeks, empty within (to which circumstance it owes its name), and long and thick in shape, like the finger: it grows nowhere except upon stony spots. The juice of this gourd, when chewed, is very beneficial to the stomach.

CHAP. 8. — THE COLOCYNTHIS: TEN REMEDIES.
There is another variety of the wild gourd, known as the "colocynthis:" this kind is full of seeds, but not so large as the cultivated one. The pale colocynthis is better than those of a grass-green colour. Employed by itself when dried, it acts as a very powerful purgative; used as an injection, it is a remedy for all diseases of the intestines, the kidneys, and the loins, as well as for paralysis. The seed being first removed, it is boiled down in hydromel to one half; after which it is used as an injection, with perfect safety, in doses of four oboli. It is good, too, for the stomach, taken in pills composed of the dried powder and boiled honey. In jaundice seven seeds of it may be taken with beneficial effects, with a draught of hydromel immediately after. The pulp of this fruit, taken with wormwood and salt, is a remedy for toothache, and the juice of it, warmed with vinegar, has the effect of strengthening loose teeth. Rubbed in with oil, it removes pains of the spine, loins, and hips: in addition to which, really a marvellous thing to speak of! the seeds of it, in even numbers, attached to the body in a linen cloth, will cure, it is said, the fevers to which the Greeks have given the name of "periodic." The juice, too, of the cultivated [colocynthis…]
results.translation:
The same result [as previously described remedies] is equally produced by the external application of the leaves of all cultivated cucumbers. These leaves, mixed with honey, are used to cure the pustules called 'epinyctis' [nocturnal pustules: red or whitish skin eruptions accompanied by sharp pain, appearing at night and disappearing by day]. Steeped in wine, they are also beneficial against the bites of dogs and of multipedes [many-legged creatures], insects known to the Greeks as 'seps' [from the Greek σήπειν, 'to rot'], which are elongated in form, with hairy legs, and are particularly harmful to cattle; their sting is followed by swelling, and the wound rapidly putrefies. The smell of the cucumber itself serves as a restorative [remedy] in cases of fainting. It is well known that cucumbers, if peeled and boiled in oil, vinegar, and honey, become all the more pleasant to eat for having undergone this preparation.

CHAPTER 7 (3). — THE GOURD: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES. THE SOMPHOS: ONE REMEDY.
There is also a wild gourd called 'somphos' by the Greeks, hollow within — a property to which it owes its name [from the Greek σομφός, meaning porous, spongy, or hollow] — and long and thick in shape, resembling a finger. It grows only on rocky ground. The juice of this gourd, when chewed, is very beneficial to the stomach.

CHAPTER 8. — THE COLOCYNTHIS: TEN REMEDIES.
There is another variety of wild gourd known as the 'colocynthis' [bitter apple / bitter gourd]: this type is full of seeds but is not as large as the cultivated gourd. The pale colocynthis is superior to those of a grass-green colour. When dried and used alone, it acts as a very powerful purgative. Administered as an enema [injection], it is a remedy for all diseases of the intestines, the kidneys, and the loins, as well as for paralysis. The seeds being first removed, it is boiled down in hydromel [honey-water] to half its volume, after which it may be administered as an enema quite safely in doses of four oboli. It is also beneficial for the stomach when taken in pills made of the dried powder and boiled honey. In cases of jaundice, seven seeds of it may be taken with good effect, followed immediately by a draught of hydromel. The pulp of this fruit, taken with wormwood and salt, is a remedy for toothache; and its juice, warmed with vinegar, has the effect of strengthening loose teeth. When rubbed in with oil, it relieves pains of the spine, loins, and hips. Furthermore — truly a marvellous thing to relate! — the seeds of it, taken in even numbers and bound to the body in a linen cloth, will cure, it is said, those fevers the Greeks call 'periodic' [i.e., intermittent or recurring fevers, such as quartan or tertian fever]. The juice, too, of the cultivated [colocynthis…]

entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves applied externally; mixed with honey for epinyctis pustules; steeped in wine for dog bites and seps bites; smell of fruit used as restorative in fainting.

entities_mentioned.name: Epinyctis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly urticaria, herpes, or other nocturnal pustular eruptions
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek term meaning 'upon the night'; red or whitish pustules with sharp pains appearing at night and disappearing by day.

entities_mentioned.name: Seps (multipedes / many-legs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a centipede (Scolopendra spp.) or large caterpillar; Fee suggests the latter
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as elongated, with hairy legs; name derived from Greek σήπειν, 'to rot'; causes swelling and rapid wound putrefaction, particularly in cattle.

entities_mentioned.name: Somphos (wild gourd)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cucurbita pepo var. Pyxidaris (Linnaeus) / Colocynthis amara (C. Bauhin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek σομφός meaning hollow/spongy; grows only on stony ground; finger-shaped; juice beneficial to the stomach when chewed.

entities_mentioned.name: Colocynthis (wild gourd)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrullus colocynthis (L.) Schrad. — bitter apple / bitter cucumber
entities_mentioned.notes: Powerful purgative when dried; used as enema for intestinal, kidney, loin diseases and paralysis; pills for stomach; seeds with hydromel for jaundice; pulp with wormwood and salt for toothache; juice with vinegar for loose teeth; rubbed with oil for spinal and hip pain; seeds in even numbers bound in linen cloth said to cure periodic (intermittent) fevers.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydromel

entities_mentioned.name: Wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with colocynthis pulp and salt as a remedy for toothache.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XX, Chapters 6–8, covering medicinal uses of cultivated and wild cucumbers and gourds. The footnotes in the source text are editorial annotations from a 19th-century scholarly edition (likely Bostock and Riley, 1855). Several claims are flagged by the editor Féé as incorrect or unsupported (e.g., the restorative effect of cucumber smell in fainting; the pleasant eating claim after boiling with oil, vinegar, and honey). The text ends mid-sentence as it continues into the next chunk. The term 'injection' throughout refers to an enema or clyster, not a hypodermic injection. 'Oboli' is a Greek unit of weight (approximately 0.57 grams each). The identification of 'somphos' with Cucurbita pepo var. Pyxidaris is tentative and contested. The claim that seeds in even numbers bound to the body cure periodic fevers is a clear magical/sympathetic medicine formulation noted as such by Pliny himself ('a marvellous thing to relate').

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Gourd, Rape, and Wild Rape (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 8–10)
results.original_text:
or Coloquintida, so remarkable for its bitterness. It is an extremely drastic, and indeed violent purgative. Recurring at stated times. The absurdity of this statement does not require discussion. gourd shred in pieces, applied warm, is good for ear-ache, and the flesh of the inside, used without the seed, for corns on the feet and the suppurations known to the Greeks as "apostemata." When the pulp and seeds are boiled together, the decoction is good for strengthening loose teeth, and for preventing toothache; wine, too, boiled with this plant, is curative of defluxions of the eyes. The leaves of it, bruised with fresh cypress-leaves, or the leaves alone, boiled in a vessel of potters' clay and beaten up with goose-grease, and then applied to the part affected, are an excellent cure for wounds. Fresh shavings of the rind are used as a cooling application for gout, and burning pains in the head, in infants more particularly; they are good, too, for erysipelas, whether it is the shavings of the rind or the seeds of the plant that are applied to the part affected. The juice of the scrapings, employed as a liniment with rose-oil and vinegar, moderates the burning heats of fevers; and the ashes of the dried fruit applied to burns are efficacious in a most remarkable degree. Chrysippus, the physician, condemned the use of the gourd as a food: it is generally agreed, however, that it is extremely good for the stomach, and for ulcerations of the intestines and of the bladder.

CHAP. 9. RAPE; NINE REMEDIES.

Rape, too, has its medicinal properties. Warmed, it is used as an application for the cure of chilblains, in addition to which, it has the effect of protecting the feet from cold. A hot decoction of rape is employed for the cure of cold gout; and raw rape, beaten up with salt, is good for all maladies of the feet. Rape-seed, used as a liniment, and taken in drink, with wine, is said to have a salutary effect against the stings of serpents, and various narcotic poisons; and there are many persons who attribute to it the properties of an antidote, when taken with wine and oil. Democritus has entirely repudiated the use of rape as an article of food, in consequence of the flatulence which it produces; while Diocles, on the other hand, has greatly extolled it, and has even gone so far as to say that it acts as an aphrodisiac. Dionysius, too, says the same of rape, and more particularly if it is seasoned with rocket; he adds, also, that roasted, and then applied with grease, it is excellent for pains in the joints.

CHAP. 10. WILD RAPE; ONE REMEDY.

Wild rape is mostly found growing in the fields; it has a tufted top, with a white seed, twice as large as that of the poppy. This plant is often employed
results.translation:
or Coloquintida [Citrullus colocynthis], so remarkable for its bitterness. It is an extremely drastic, and indeed violent purgative. [The text claims it acts at recurring, fixed intervals — the absurdity of this statement requires no discussion.]

The cultivated gourd, shred in pieces and applied warm, is good for ear-ache; the flesh of the interior, used without the seed, is good for corns on the feet and for the suppurations the Greeks call "apostemata" [aposthumes, i.e., abscesses]. When the pulp and seeds are boiled together, the resulting decoction is good for strengthening loose teeth and for preventing toothache; wine also, boiled with this plant, is curative of discharges [defluxions] from the eyes. The leaves, bruised together with fresh cypress-leaves — or the leaves alone, boiled in an earthenware vessel and beaten up with goose-grease — and then applied to the affected part, are an excellent cure for wounds. Fresh shavings of the rind serve as a cooling application for gout and for burning pains in the head, especially in infants; they are also good for erysipelas [ignis sacer, "sacred fire," also called St. Anthony's fire], whether it is the shavings of the rind or the seeds that are applied to the affected area. The juice of the scrapings, used as a liniment together with rose-oil and vinegar, moderates the burning heat of fevers; and the ashes of the dried fruit applied to burns are efficacious to a most remarkable degree. Chrysippus the physician condemned the use of the gourd as a food; it is generally agreed, however, that it is extremely beneficial to the stomach and for ulcerations of the intestines and bladder.

CHAPTER 9. RAPE; NINE REMEDIES.

Rape likewise has its medicinal properties. Warmed, it is applied as a remedy for chilblains, and it also has the effect of protecting the feet from cold. A hot decoction of rape is used for the cure of cold gout, and raw rape beaten up with salt is good for all ailments of the feet. Rape-seed, employed as a liniment and also taken as a drink in wine, is said to be beneficial against the stings of serpents and various narcotic poisons; and many persons attribute to it the qualities of an antidote when taken with wine and oil. Democritus entirely repudiated the use of rape as food on account of the flatulence it produces; Diocles, on the other hand, greatly extolled it and went so far as to say that it acts as an aphrodisiac. Dionysius likewise says the same of rape, and more particularly when it is seasoned with rocket [arugula, Eruca sativa]; he adds, furthermore, that rape roasted and then applied with grease is excellent for pains in the joints.

CHAPTER 10. WILD RAPE; ONE REMEDY.

Wild rape is found growing mostly in the fields; it has a tufted head and a white seed twice as large as that of the poppy. This plant is often employed [text continues].
