results.translator_notes: This segment is a continuation of Book XX of Pliny's Natural History, covering chapters 8–10. The source text is an existing English translation (likely Bostock & Riley) with footnotes integrated into the body; these footnotes (numbered 41–49) have been preserved contextually and attributed. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'hoiled' → 'boiled', 'erj-sipelas' → 'erysipelas', 'lias' → 'has', 'EAPE J NINE 11EMEBYES' → 'RAPE; NINE REMEDIES', 'BEMEDY' → 'REMEDY', 'tho' → 'the'. The segment ends mid-sentence at 'This plant is often employed,' indicating the chunk cuts off at this point. 'Apostemata' / 'aposthumes' is retained with bracketed clarification as a period technical term. 'Defluxions' (catarrhal discharges) is also retained as period vocabulary. 'Ignis sacer' is noted as the Latin for erysipelas as used in this tradition, though Celsus distinguishes the two conditions. Fée's editorial skepticism about the gourd's food value and about rape's universal antidotal attribution is preserved in the footnotes.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Properties of Turnip, Bunion, Bunias, Wild Radish (Armoracia), and Cultivated Radish

that of anise, and known by them as "bunion," is good for promoting the menstrual discharge in females and for affections of the bladder; it acts, also, as a diuretic. For these purposes, a decoction of it is taken with hydromel, or else one drachma of the juice of the plant. The seed, parched, and then beaten up, and taken in warm water, in doses of four cyathi, is a good remedy for dysentery; it will stop the passage of the urine, however, if linseed is not taken with it. The other kind of turnip is known by the name of "bunias," and bears a considerable resemblance to the radish and the rape united, the seed of it enjoying the reputation of being a remedy for poisons; hence it is that we find it employed in antidotes.

CHAP. 12. THE WILD RADISH, OR ARMORACIA: ONE REMEDY.
We have already said that there is also a wild radish. The most esteemed is that of Arcadia, though it is also found growing in other countries as well. It is only efficacious as a diuretic, being in other respects of a heating nature. In Italy, it is known also by the name of "armoracia."

CHAP. 13. THE CULTIVATED RADISH: FORTY-THREE REMEDIES.
The cultivated radish, too, in addition to what we have already said of it, purges the stomach, attenuates the phlegm, acts as a diuretic, and detaches the bilious secretions. A decoction of the rind of radishes in wine, taken in the morning in doses of three cyathi, has the effect of breaking and expelling calculi of the bladder. A decoction, too, of this rind in vinegar and water, is employed as a liniment for the stings of serpents. Taken fasting in the morning with honey, radishes are good for a cough. Parched radish-seed, as well as...
results.translation:
...and known by them as 'bunion' [Latin: bunion; possibly Bunium pumilum or related umbellifera]. It is good for promoting the menstrual discharge in women and for disorders of the bladder; it also acts as a diuretic. For these purposes, a decoction of it is taken with hydromel [honey-water], or alternatively one drachma of the plant's juice is administered. The seed, when parched and then ground up and taken in warm water in doses of four cyathi [approximately 180 ml], is a good remedy for dysentery; it will, however, suppress the passage of urine unless linseed is taken alongside it.

The other variety of turnip is known by the name 'bunias' [Brassica napo-brassica of Linnaeus], and closely resembles a cross between the radish and the rape. Its seed has the reputation of being an antidote to poisons, and for this reason it is incorporated into antidote preparations.

CHAPTER 12. THE WILD RADISH, OR ARMORACIA: ONE REMEDY.
As we have already noted, there is also a wild radish [Cochlearia Armoracia of Linnaeus; modern horseradish]. The most highly regarded variety comes from Arcadia, though it is found growing in other regions as well. It is effective only as a diuretic; in other respects it is of a heating nature. In Italy, it is also known by the name 'armoracia.'

CHAPTER 13. THE CULTIVATED RADISH: FORTY-THREE REMEDIES.
The cultivated radish, in addition to what has already been said of it, purges the stomach, thins the phlegm, acts as a diuretic, and promotes the discharge of bilious secretions. A decoction of the rind of radishes in wine, taken in the morning in doses of three cyathi [approximately 135 ml], has the effect of breaking up and expelling bladder stones [calculi]. A decoction of this same rind in vinegar and water is employed as a topical liniment for serpent stings. Taken on an empty stomach in the morning with honey, radishes are beneficial for a cough. Parched radish seed, as well as...

The segment covers chapters 11–13 of Book XX. 'Cyathus' is a Roman liquid measure of approximately 45 ml. 'Drachma' is a small weight measure (~3.4 g). Several footnote references to Dioscorides and Galen are embedded in the text and have been preserved in context. The footnote numbering in the source (54–60) is retained in annotations above. results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of the Radish (Raphanus sativus) from Pliny's Natural History
results.original_text: remedy for phthiriasis. Pounded radishes, too, are employed as a liniment for inflammations under the skin, and the rind, mixed with honey, for bruises of recent date. Lethargic persons are recommended to eat them as hot as possible, and the seed, parched and then pounded with honey, will give relief to asthmatic patients. Radishes, too, are useful as a remedy for poisons, and are employed to counteract the effects of the sting of the cerastes and the scorpion: indeed, after having rubbed the hands with radishes or radish-seed, we may handle those reptiles with impunity. If a radish is placed upon a scorpion, it will cause its death. Radishes are useful, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi or henbane; and according to Nicander, they are salutary against the effects of bullock's blood, when drunk. The two physicians of the name of Apollodorus prescribe radishes to be given in cases of poisoning by mistletoe; but whereas Apollodorus of Citium recommends radish-seed pounded in water, Apollodorus of Tarentum speaks of the juice. Radishes diminish the volume of the spleen, and are beneficial for maladies of the liver and pains in the loins: taken, too, with vinegar or mustard, they are good for dropsy and lethargy, as well as epilepsy and melancholy. Praxagoras recommends that radishes should be given for the iliac passion, and Plistonicus for the coeliac disease. Radishes are good, too, for curing ulcerations of the intestines and suppurations of the thoracic organs, if eaten with honey. Some persons say, however, that for this purpose they should be boiled in earth and water; a decoction which, according to them, promotes the menstrual discharge. Taken with vinegar or honey, radishes expel worms from the intestines; and a decoction of them boiled down to one-third, taken in wine, is good for intestinal hernia. Employed in this way, too, they have the effect of drawing off the superfluous blood. Medius recommends them to be given boiled to persons troubled with spitting of blood, and to women who are suckling, for the purpose of increasing the milk. Hippocrates recommends females whose hair falls off, to rub the head with radishes, and he says that for pains of the uterus, they should be applied to the navel.
results.translation: Radishes are cited as a remedy for phthiriasis [louse infestation]. Pounded radishes are employed as a liniment for phlegmonic inflammations [subcutaneous inflammatory swellings characterized by heat, redness, swelling, and pain], and the rind, mixed with honey, is used for fresh bruises. Lethargic [veternosus: a state of somnolence regarded as a prelude to apoplexy] persons are recommended to eat them as hot as possible, and the seed, parched and then pounded with honey, is said to give relief to asthmatic patients. Radishes are also useful as a remedy against poisons, and are employed to counteract the effects of the sting of the cerastes [Coluber cerastes, the horned viper] and the scorpion; indeed, after rubbing the hands with radishes or radish-seed, one may handle those reptiles with impunity. If a radish is placed upon a scorpion, it is said to cause its death. Radishes are useful, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi or henbane; and according to Nicander, they are beneficial against the effects of drinking bullock's blood. [Note: Nicander in his Alexipharmaca actually attributes these properties to cabbage, not radish; Pliny may have confused the Greek words for cabbage (raphanos) and radish (raphanis).] The two physicians named Apollodorus prescribe radishes in cases of poisoning by mistletoe: Apollodorus of Citium recommends radish-seed pounded in water, while Apollodorus of Tarentum recommends the juice. Radishes diminish the volume of the spleen, and are beneficial for liver ailments and pains in the loins; taken with vinegar or mustard, they are good for dropsy and lethargy, as well as epilepsy and melancholy. Praxagoras recommends them for the iliac passion [intestinal obstruction/colic], and Plistonicus for coeliac disease. Radishes are also good for curing ulcerations of the intestines and suppurations of the thoracic organs, if eaten with honey. Some hold that for this purpose they should be boiled in earth and water, a decoction which is also said to promote the menstrual discharge. Taken with vinegar or honey, radishes expel intestinal worms; and a decoction reduced to one-third, taken in wine, is good for intestinal hernia. Used in this way they also have the effect of drawing off superfluous blood. Medius recommends giving them boiled to persons with spitting of blood, and to nursing women to increase milk production. Hippocrates recommends that women suffering from hair loss rub the head with radishes, and states that for pains of the uterus, radishes should be applied to the navel.

entities_mentioned.name: Radish (Raphanus sativus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named explicitly in the text as Raphanus sativus. Multiple preparations described: pounded as liniment, rind with honey, seed parched and pounded with honey, juice, decoction boiled to one-third in wine, applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: Cerastes
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to by Pliny as Coluber cerastes of Linnaeus; the horned viper of North Africa and the Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Henbane
entities_mentioned.notes: A toxic plant; radishes claimed (dubiously) to counteract its poisoning effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Mistletoe
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed against by both Apollodori; one recommends radish-seed pounded in water, the other the juice of radish.

entities_mentioned.name: Bullock's blood
entities_mentioned.notes: Drunk as a poison; Themistocles reportedly died from it. Pliny cites Nicander's Alexipharmaca, but Nicander actually credits cabbage rather than radish as the antidote.

entities_mentioned.name: Fungi (poisonous mushrooms)
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic reference to toxic fungi; Dioscorides makes the same claim for radishes as an antidote, though this is regarded as unfounded.

entities_mentioned.name: Nicander
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicander of Colophon (c. 197–130 BCE), Greek poet and physician
entities_mentioned.notes: Author of Alexipharmaca; Pliny's citation of him here is likely erroneous — Nicander credits cabbage, not radish.

entities_mentioned.name: Apollodorus of Citium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apollodorus of Citium, Greek physician, c. 1st century BCE

entities_mentioned.name: Apollodorus of Tarentum

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Pliny the Elder's Natural History, Book XX, Chapter 13 on the cultivated radish. The text is already in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock and Riley), so this represents a cleaned and annotated version rather than a fresh translation from Latin. Key philological note: Pliny's attribution of certain antidotal properties to the radish based on Nicander is almost certainly a confusion between the Greek words ῥάφανος ('cabbage') and ῥαφανίς ('radish'), as noted by the original translator's footnote 68. The footnote numbering and marginal notes (62–75) embedded in the source text have been retained in context where they add scholarly value. Several claimed medicinal virtues (e.g., handling venomous reptiles safely after rubbing hands with radish) are noted as illusory or folkloric by Fee and other commentators.

'Praecordiorum.' 'Enterocele.' De Morb. Mulier. B. ii. c. 67. Eating or corroding ulcers. Hippocrates, De Diaeta, B. ii. cc. 25, 26, says that radishes are of a cold, and hyssop of a warm, nature. PLINY'S NATURAL HISTORY. [Book XX] dulness of hearing, too, radish-juice is injected into the ear. To promote vomiting, it is extremely beneficial to eat radishes fasting.

CHAP. 14. THE PARSNIP: FIVE REMEDIES. THE HIBISCUM, WILD MALLOW, OR PLISTOLOCHIA: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The hibiscum, by some persons known as the wild mallow, and by others as the 'plistolochia,' bears a strong resemblance to the parsnip; it is good for ulcerations of the cartilages, and is employed for the cure of fractured bones. The leaves of it, taken in water, relax the stomach; they have the effect, also, of keeping away serpents, and, employed as a liniment, are a cure for the stings of bees, wasps, and hornets. The root, pulled up before sunrise, and wrapped in wool of the colour known as 'native,' taken from a sheep which has just dropped a ewe lamb, is employed as a bandage for scrofulous swellings, even after they have suppurated. Some persons are of opinion, that for this purpose the root should be dug up with an implement of gold, and that care should be taken not to let it touch the ground. Celsus, too, recommends this root to be boiled in wine, and applied in cases of gout unattended with swelling.

CHAP. 15. (5.) — THE STAPHYLINOS, OR WILD PARSNIP: TWENTY-TWO REMEDIES.

The staphylinos, or, as some persons call it, 'erratic parsnip,' is another kind. The seed of this plant, pounded and taken in wine, reduces swelling of the abdomen, and alleviates hysterical suffocations and pains, to such a degree as to restore the uterus to its natural condition. Used as a liniment, also, with raisin wine, it is good for pains of the bowels in females; for men, too, beaten up with an equal proportion of bread, and taken in wine, it may be found beneficial for similar pains. It is a diuretic also, and it will arrest the progress of phagedaenic ulcers, if applied fresh with honey, or else dried and sprinkled on them with meal. Dietiches recommends the root of it to be given, with hydromel, for affections of the liver and spleen, as also the sides, loins, and kidneys.
results.translation:
There is an antipathy between these two plants [radish and hyssop]. 'Morbus comitialis' — literally the 'comitial disease' — is epilepsy; it was so called because, if anyone was seized with it at the Comitia [the public assemblies of the Roman people], it was customary to adjourn the meeting to another day. [Melancholia derives] from the Greek μέλας ('black') and χολή ('bile'): melancholy, or low spirits, was so named from the belief that it resulted from a predominance of an imaginary secretion the ancients called 'black bile.' The coeliac flux, according to Fee, is symptomatic of chronic enteritis, and is a species of diarrhoea in which the chyme [partially digested food] is voided without undergoing any change in passing through the intestines. [References to praecordiorum (the region about the heart/diaphragm), enterocele (intestinal hernia), De Morb. Mulier. Book II, ch. 67, and corroding/eating ulcers are noted.] Hippocrates, in De Diaeta, Book II, chapters 25–26, states that radishes are of a cold nature and hyssop of a warm nature. Furthermore, for dullness of hearing, radish-juice is injected into the ear. To promote vomiting, it is extremely beneficial to eat radishes on an empty stomach.

CHAPTER 14. THE PARSNIP: FIVE REMEDIES. THE HIBISCUM, WILD MALLOW, OR PLISTOLOCHIA: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The hibiscum — known to some as the wild mallow [moloche agria] and to others as the 'plistolochia' — closely resembles the parsnip. It is beneficial for ulcerations of the cartilages and is used in the treatment of fractured bones. Its leaves, taken in water, relax the stomach; they also have the property of repelling serpents, and when applied as a liniment, they cure the stings of bees, wasps, and hornets. The root, pulled up before sunrise and wrapped in undyed wool ('native' coloured) taken from a sheep that has just given birth to a ewe lamb, is applied as a bandage to scrofulous [tuberculosis of the lymph nodes] swellings, even after these have suppurated [discharged pus]. Some hold that for this purpose the root must be dug up with a golden implement and care must be taken not to let it touch the ground. Celsus likewise recommends that this root be boiled in wine and applied in cases of gout unaccompanied by swelling.

CHAPTER 15. (5.) THE STAPHYLINOS, OR WILD PARSNIP: TWENTY-TWO REMEDIES.

The staphylinos — which some call the 'erratic' (or wild) parsnip — is another variety. The seed of this plant, pounded and taken in wine, reduces abdominal swelling and relieves hysterical suffocations and pains to such a degree as to restore the uterus to its natural position. Applied as a liniment with raisin wine, it is also good for bowel pains in women; for men too, when beaten together with an equal portion of bread and taken in wine, it may be found helpful for similar pains. It is also diuretic, and it arrests the progress of phagedaenic [rapidly spreading, corroding] ulcers when applied fresh with honey, or dried and sprinkled on them with meal. Dietiches recommends that its root be given with hydromel [honey-water] for affections of the liver and spleen, and likewise for complaints of the sides, loins, and kidneys.

results.translator_notes: This chunk is largely a continuation of footnotes and commentary from a prior chapter, followed by two new chapters on hibiscum/wild mallow and wild parsnip. The footnote numbering (70–83) and editorial comments from 'Fee' (botanist F.-E. Féé, who annotated a 19th-century French Pliny edition) are preserved in the original and integrated here as parenthetical references. The ritual instructions for gathering the hibiscum root (before sunrise, with a gold implement, wrapped in undyed wool from a sheep that just lambed a female) are characteristic of ancient magical-medical practice and are translated faithfully. 'Plistolochia' remains unidentified in modern botany and is retained as a proper name. 'Dietiches' is an otherwise obscure authority cited by Pliny; identity unknown.

results.title_inferred: Properties and Remedies of Staphylinos, Gingidion, and Skirret
results.original_text:
eaten of it will receive no hurt from them. Mixed with axle-grease, it is applied to parts of the body stung by reptiles; and the leaves of it are eaten as a remedy for indigestion. Orpheus has stated that the staphylinos acts as a philtre, most probably because, a very well-established fact, when employed as a food, it is an aphrodisiac; a circumstance which has led some persons to state that it promotes conception. In other respects the cultivated parsnip has similar properties; though the wild kind is more powerful in its operation, and that which grows in stony soils more particularly. The seed, too, of the cultivated parsnip, taken in wine, or vinegar and water, is salutary for stings inflicted by scorpions. By rubbing the teeth with the root of this plant, tooth-ache is removed.

CHAP. 16. — GINGIDION: ONE REMEDY.
The Syrians devote themselves particularly to the cultivation of the garden, a circumstance to which we owe the Greek proverb, "There is plenty of vegetables in Syria." Among other vegetables, that country produces one very similar to the staphylinos, and known to some persons as "gingidion," only that it is smaller than the staphylinos and more bitter, though it has just the same properties. Eaten either raw or boiled, it is very beneficial to the stomach, as it entirely absorbs all humours with which it may happen to be surcharged.

CHAP. 17. THE SKIRRET: ELEVEN REMEDIES.
The wild skirret, too, is very similar to the cultivated kind, and is productive of similar effects. It sharpens the stomach, and, taken with vinegar flavoured with silphium, or with pepper and hydromel, or else with garum, it promotes the appetite. According to Opion, it is a diuretic, and acts as an aphrodisiac. Diocles is also of the same opinion; in addition to which, he says that it possesses cordial virtues for convalescents, and is extremely beneficial after frequent vomitings. Heraclides has prescribed it against the effects of mercury, and for occasional impotence, as also generally for patients when convalescent. Hicesius says that skirrets would appear to be prejudicial to the stomach, because no one is able to eat three of them following; still, however, he looks upon them as beneficial to patients who are just resuming the use of wine. The juice of the cultivated skirret, taken in goats' milk, arrests looseness of the stomach.
results.translation:
Whoever has eaten of it [staphylinos] will receive no harm from them [reptiles]. Mixed with axle-grease [axungia: swine's grease used to lubricate chariot axles], it is applied to parts of the body stung by reptiles; and its leaves are eaten as a remedy for indigestion. Orpheus stated that the staphylinos acts as a love-philtre [aphrodisiac agent], most probably because it is a well-established fact that when used as food it acts as an aphrodisiac — a circumstance which has led some persons to claim that it promotes conception. In other respects the cultivated parsnip has similar properties, though the wild kind is more powerful in its operation, and especially so when it grows in stony soils. The seed of the cultivated parsnip, taken in wine, or in vinegar and water [posca / oxycrate], is also beneficial against the stings of scorpions. Toothache is relieved by rubbing the teeth with the root of this plant.

CHAPTER 16. — GINGIDION: ONE REMEDY.
The Syrians are particularly devoted to garden cultivation, a circumstance which gave rise to the Greek proverb, "There is plenty of vegetables in Syria." Among other vegetables, that country produces one very similar to the staphylinos, known to some as "gingidion," though it is smaller than the staphylinos and more bitter, while possessing the same properties. Whether eaten raw or boiled, it is very beneficial to the stomach, as it thoroughly absorbs whatever excess of humours [superfluous bodily fluids] may be present.

CHAPTER 17. THE SKIRRET: ELEVEN REMEDIES.
The wild skirret is also very similar to the cultivated kind and produces similar effects. It stimulates [sharpens] the stomach, and taken with vinegar flavoured with silphium, or with pepper and hydromel [honey-water], or with garum [fish sauce], it promotes the appetite. According to Opion, it is diuretic and acts as an aphrodisiac. Diocles shares this opinion, and adds that it possesses restorative [cordial] virtues for convalescents and is extremely beneficial after repeated vomiting. Heraclides prescribed it against the effects of mercury [mercurial poisoning], for occasional impotence, and generally for patients who are convalescing. Hicesius holds that skirrets appear to be harmful to the stomach, since no one is able to eat three of them in succession; yet he nonetheless considers them beneficial to patients who are just resuming the use of wine. The juice of the cultivated skirret, taken in goats' milk, arrests looseness of the stomach [diarrhoea].

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, Book XX, chapters 15–17. The source text is already in English (a 19th-century translation, likely Bostock & Riley), so the task here is to produce a clean, scholarly English rendering with clarifications rather than a translation from Latin. Footnote numbers have been removed from inline text for readability but their content has been incorporated into entity notes where relevant. 'Posca' (vinegar and water) and 'oxycrate' are preserved as bracketed technical terms. 'Mercury' in the Heraclides passage likely refers to mercurial preparations used medicinally rather than elemental mercury poisoning, though both interpretations are possible. The Greek proverb fragment (Πολλὰ Συρῶν λάχανα) appears in the original footnote and is noted accordingly.

results.title_inferred: Sile or Hartwort, Elecampane, and Onions: Remedies from Pliny's Natural History (Book XX, Chapters 18–20)
results.original_text:
CHAP. 18. SILE, OR HARTWORT: TWELVE REMEDIES. As the similitude which exists between their Greek names has caused most persons to mistake the one for the other, we have thought it as well to give some account here of sile or hartwort, though it is a plant which is very generally known. The best hartwort is that of Massilia, the seed of it being broad and yellow; and the next best is that of Æthiopia, the seed of which is of a darker hue. The Cretan hartwort is the most odoriferous of the several kinds. The root of this plant has a pleasant smell; the seed of it is eaten by vultures, it is said. Hartwort is useful to man for inveterate coughs, ruptures, and convulsions, being usually taken in white wine; it is employed also in cases of opisthotony, and for diseases of the liver, as well as for griping pains in the bowels and for strangury, in doses of two or three spoonfuls at a time. The leaves of this plant are useful also, and have the effect of aiding parturition — in animals even: indeed, it is generally said that roes, when about to bring forth, are in the habit of eating these leaves in particular. They are topically applied, also, in erysipelas; and either the leaves or the seed, taken fasting in the morning, are very beneficial to the digestion. Hartwort has the effect, too, of arresting looseness in cattle, either bruised and put into their drink, or else eaten by them after it has been chewed with salt. When oxen are in a diseased state, it is beaten up and poured into their food.

CHAP. 19. ELECAMPANE: ELEVEN REMEDIES. Elecampane, too, chewed fasting, has the effect of strengthening the teeth, if, from the moment that it is plucked, it is not allowed to touch the ground: a confection of it is a cure for cough. The juice of the root boiled is an expellent of intestinal tapeworm; and dried in the shade and reduced to powder, the root is curative in cases of cough, convulsions, flatulency, and affections of the trachea. It is useful too, for the bites of venomous animals; and the leaves steeped in wine are applied topically for pains in the loins.

CHAP. 20. ONIONS: TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES. There are no such things in existence as wild onions. The cultivated onion is employed for the cure of dimness of sight, the patient being made to smell at it till tears come into the eyes: it is
results.translation:
CHAPTER 18. SILE, OR HARTWORT: TWELVE REMEDIES. Because the similarity between their Greek names [Σίσαρον, 'skirret,' and Σέσελι / Σελι / Σίλι, 'hartwort'] has caused most people to confuse one plant with the other, we have thought it appropriate to include here some account of sile or hartwort [Seseli tortuosum L.], even though it is a plant that is very widely known. The finest hartwort is that of Massilia [Marseilles], its seed being broad and yellow; the next best is that of Æthiopia, whose seed is of a darker colour. The Cretan hartwort is the most fragrant of the several varieties. The root of this plant has a pleasant odour; its seed is eaten by vultures, so it is said. Hartwort is beneficial to humans for chronic [inveterate] coughs, ruptures, and convulsions, and is ordinarily taken in white wine; it is also used in cases of opisthotonos [a form of severe muscular spasm], and for diseases of the liver, as well as for griping pains in the bowels and for strangury [painful, obstructed urination], in doses of two or three spoonfuls at a time. The leaves of this plant are likewise useful, and have the property of aiding parturition [childbirth] — even in animals: indeed, it is commonly reported that does [roes], when about to give birth, are accustomed to eat these leaves in particular. They are also applied topically in cases of erysipelas [a skin infection]; and either the leaves or the seed, taken fasting in the morning, are very beneficial to digestion. Hartwort also has the effect of checking looseness of the bowels in cattle, either bruised and added to their drink, or eaten by them after being chewed with salt. When oxen are in a diseased state, it is beaten up and poured into their feed.

CHAPTER 19. ELECAMPANE: ELEVEN REMEDIES. Elecampane [Inula helenium L.], too, chewed fasting, has the effect of strengthening the teeth, provided that from the moment it is pulled it is not allowed to touch the ground; a confection made from it is a remedy for cough. The juice of the root, when boiled, expels intestinal tapeworm; and when dried in the shade and reduced to powder, the root is curative in cases of cough, convulsions, flatulence, and affections of the trachea [windpipe]. It is also useful for the bites of venomous animals; and the leaves steeped in wine are applied topically for pains in the loins.

CHAPTER 20. ONIONS: TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES. There is no such thing as a wild onion. The cultivated onion is used to treat dimness of sight, the patient being made to smell it until tears come to the eyes; it is [text continues beyond this segment].

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment begins mid-chapter title with a likely OCR corruption ('BILE, OB IIARTWORT' for 'SILE, OR HARTWORT') which has been silently corrected throughout. The word 'CHIP.' for 'CHAP.' in the elecampane heading is also corrected. 2. Opisthotony / opisthotonos: a severe form of hypertonic muscular spasm causing the body to arch backward; retained as a period technical term. 3. Strangury: painful drop-by-drop urination, a classical medical term retained. 4. The footnote numbering (96–99, 1–3) and footnote text are preserved in the cleaned original as part of the scholarly apparatus. 5. The reference to 'roes' (does/female deer) eating hartwort leaves before parturition is flagged by Pliny's own annotator as an absurd story. 6. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence in Chapter 20; this reflects the chunk boundary, not a lacuna in Pliny.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Onion and Cutleek (Pliny, Natural History, Chapters 20–21)
results.original_text:
end of a couple of days. Applied, too, in the same way, they are good for healing excoriations. Roasted in hot ashes, many persons have applied them topically, with barley meal, for defluxions of the eyes and ulcerations of the genitals. The juice, too, is employed as an ointment for sores of the eyes, albugo, and argema. Mixed with honey, it is used as a liniment for the stings of serpents, and all kinds of ulcerous sores. In combination with woman's milk, it is employed for affections of the ears; and in cases of singing in the ears and hardness of hearing, it is injected into those organs with goose-grease or honey. In cases where persons have been suddenly struck dumb, it has been administered to them to drink, mixed with water. In cases, too, of toothache, it is sometimes introduced into the mouth as a gargle for the teeth; it is an excellent remedy also for all kinds of wounds made by animals, scorpions more particularly. In cases of alopecy and itch-scab, bruised onions are rubbed on the parts affected: they are also given boiled to persons afflicted with dysentery or lumbago. Onion peelings, burnt to ashes and mixed with vinegar, are employed topically for stings of serpents and multipedes. In other respects, there are remarkable differences of opinion among medical men. The more modern writers have stated that onions are good for the thoracic organs and the digestion, but that they are productive of flatulency and thirst. The school of Asclepiades maintains that, used as an aliment, onions impart a florid colour to the complexion, and that, taken fasting every day, they are promoters of robustness and health; that as a diet, too, they are good for the stomach by acting upon the spirits, and have the effect of relaxing the bowels. He says, too, that, employed as a suppository, onions disperse piles, and that the juice of them, taken in combination with juice of fennel, is wonderfully beneficial in cases of incipient dropsy. It is said, too, that the juice, taken with rue and honey, is good for quinsy, and has the effect of dispelling lethargy. Varro assures us that onions, pounded with salt and vinegar and then dried, will never be attacked by worms.

CHAP. 21. (6.) — CUTLEEK: THIRTY-TWO REMEDIES.
Cutleek has the effect of stanching bleeding at the nose.

end of a couple of days. Applied in the same manner, they are also good for healing excoriations [superficial skin abrasions]. Roasted in hot ashes, many persons have applied them topically, mixed with barley meal, for defluxions [discharges/inflammations] of the eyes and ulcerations of the genitals. The juice is also employed as an ointment for sores of the eyes, albugo [a whitening or opacity of the cornea], and argema [a white speck within the pupil of the eye]. Mixed with honey, it is used as a liniment for the stings of serpents and all kinds of ulcerous sores. Combined with woman's milk, it is employed for affections of the ears; and in cases of tinnitus [ringing in the ears] and hardness of hearing, it is injected into the ears with goose-grease or honey. In cases where persons have been suddenly struck dumb, it has been administered as a drink mixed with water. In cases of toothache, it is sometimes introduced into the mouth as a gargle; it is also held to be an excellent remedy for all kinds of wounds inflicted by animals, and especially by scorpions. In cases of alopecia [fox-evil; hair loss] and itch-scab, bruised onions are rubbed on the affected parts; they are also given boiled to persons suffering from dysentery or lumbago. Onion peelings, burnt to ashes and mixed with vinegar, are applied topically for stings of serpents and millipedes. In other respects, there are remarkable differences of opinion among physicians. He also states that, employed as a suppository, onions disperse piles, and that their juice, taken in combination with the juice of fennel, is wonderfully beneficial in cases of incipient dropsy. It is also said that the juice, taken with rue and honey, is good for quinsy [peritonsillar abscess/throat inflammation], and has the effect of dispelling lethargy. Varro assures us that onions, pounded with salt and vinegar and then dried, will never be attacked by worms.

entities_mentioned.name: Onion
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated as remedy for excoriations, eye afflictions (albugo, argema), serpent stings, ear disorders, sudden mutism, toothache, scorpion wounds, alopecia, itch-scab, dysentery, lumbago, flatulence, piles, dropsy, quinsy, and lethargy. Preparations include topical application roasted with barley meal, juice as ointment, mixed with honey, woman's milk, goose-grease, water, vinegar, rue, and fennel juice, as well as boiled for internal use and used as a suppository.

entities_mentioned.name: Inula Helenium (elecampane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in footnote 2 as Inula Helenium of botanists; see Book XIX, chapter 29. The active principle is a camphor-like compound (now identified as inulin/alantolactone).

entities_mentioned.name: Cutleek (Porrum sectivum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ampeloprasum var. porrum (leek) or Allium porrum, cut/sectile variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Cutleek ('porrum sectivum') is a repeatedly cut or leaf leek as distinct from the headed leek. Mentioned here as a styptic for nosebleed; thirty-two remedies attributed to it in the chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Barley meal
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an excipient in topical application with roasted onion for eye defluxions and genital ulcerations.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose-grease
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for ear injections along with onion juice or honey.

entities_mentioned.name: Fennel
entities_mentioned.notes: Juice combined with onion juice for incipient dropsy.

entities_mentioned.name: Rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with onion juice and honey for quinsy and lethargy.

entities_mentioned.name: Albugo
entities_mentioned.notes: A disease by which the cornea contracts a whiteness; treated with onion juice as ointment.

entities_mentioned.name: Argema
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corneal ulcer or leucoma involving the pupillary area
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a white speck within the black (pupil) of the eye; treated with onion juice.

results.translator_notes: This segment is already in English (a 19th-century translation of Pliny's Latin Natural History, likely Bostock & Riley). The task here is therefore to present a cleaned, annotated version. OCR errors corrected: 'E-oasted' → 'Roasted'; 'Iiiula' → 'Inula'; 'lias' → 'has'; 'winch' → 'which'. Footnotes are retained and integrated as bracketed clarifications or endnotes where relevant. The editorial footnotes (numbered 2–14) are from the original translator and have been preserved. Fee's critical annotations (noting that certain claimed virtues are erroneous) are preserved as translator context. The text begins mid-sentence, suggesting continuation from a previous chunk dealing with onion (Allium cepa). Chapter 21 on cutleek begins at the end of the segment and is only partially represented.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Leeks (Cut and Bulbed) and Garlic — Pliny's Natural History
results.original_text:
known also as 'syce,' situate in the corner of the eye, from which there is a continual running: some persons, however, give this name to livid pustules, which cause great restlessness in the night. Other kinds of ulcers, too, are treated with leeks beaten up with honey: used with vinegar, they are extensively employed also for the bites of wild beasts, as well as of serpents and other venomous creatures. Mixed with goats' gall, or else honied wine in equal proportions, they are used for affections of the ears, and, combined with woman's milk, for singing in the ears. In cases of head-ache, the juice is injected into the nostrils, or else into the ear at bed-time, two spoonfuls of juice to one of honey. This juice is taken too with pure wine, for the stings of serpents and scorpions, and, mixed with a semi-sextarius of wine, for lumbago. The juice, or the leek itself, eaten as a food, is very beneficial to persons troubled with spitting of blood, phthisis, or inveterate catarrhs; in cases also of jaundice or dropsy, and for nephretic pains, it is taken in barley-water, in doses of one acetabulum of juice. The same dose, too, mixed with honey, effectually purges the uterus. Leeks are eaten, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi, and are applied topically to wounds: they act also as an aphrodisiac, allay thirst, and dispel the effects of drunkenness; but they have the effect of weakening the sight and causing flatulency, it is said, though, at the same time, they are not injurious to the stomach, and act as an aperient. Leeks impart a remarkable clearness to the voice.

CHAP. 22. BULBED LEEK: THIRTY-NINE REMEDIES.
Bulbed leek produces the same effects as cut-leek, but in a more powerful degree. To persons troubled with spitting of blood, the juice of it is given, with powdered nut-galls or frankincense, or else gum acacia. Hippocrates, however, prescribes it without being mixed with anything else, and expressed himself of opinion that it has the property of opening the uterus when contracted, and that taken as an aliment by females, it is a great promoter of fecundity. Beaten up and mixed with honey, it cleanses ulcerous sores. It is good for the cure of coughs, catarrhs, and all affections of the lungs and of the trachea, whether given in the form of a ptisan, or eaten raw, the head excepted: it must be taken, however, without bread, and upon alternate days, and this even if there should be purulent expectorations. Taken in this form, it greatly improves the voice, and acts as an aphrodisiac, and as a promoter of sleep. The heads, boiled in a couple of waters, arrest looseness of the bowels, and fluxes of long standing; and a decoction of the outer coat acts as a dye upon grey hair.

CHAP. 23. GARLIC: SIXTY-ONE REMEDIES.
Garlic has very powerful properties, and is of great utility to persons on changes of water or locality. The very smell of it drives away serpents and scorpions, and, according to what some persons say, it is a cure for wounds made by [text breaks off].
results.translation:
The condition known also as 'syce' [from Greek σῦκον, 'fig'], situated in the corner of the eye, causes a continual running discharge; some authors, however, apply this name to livid pustules that cause great restlessness at night. Other kinds of ulcers are likewise treated with leeks beaten up with honey. Used with vinegar, leeks are also extensively employed for the bites of wild beasts, serpents, and other venomous creatures. Mixed with goats' gall, or alternatively with honeyed wine [mulsum] in equal proportions, they are used for affections of the ears; combined with woman's milk, they are used for ringing in the ears. In cases of headache, the juice is injected into the nostrils or into the ear at bedtime — two spoonfuls of juice to one of honey. This juice is also taken with undiluted wine [merum] for the stings of serpents and scorpions, and mixed with a semi-sextarius of wine for lumbago. The juice, or the leek itself eaten as food, is very beneficial to persons troubled with spitting of blood, phthisis [pulmonary consumption], or chronic catarrhs; in cases of jaundice or dropsy, and for nephritic pains, it is taken in barley-water in doses of one acetabulum of juice. The same dose, mixed with honey, effectually purges the uterus. Leeks are also eaten in cases of poisoning by fungi — though they would be of no real utility for this — and are applied topically to wounds. They also act as an aphrodisiac (a claim considered unfounded), allay thirst, and dispel the effects of drunkenness; but they are said to weaken the eyesight and cause flatulence, though at the same time they are not injurious to the stomach and act as an aperient [mild laxative]. Leeks impart a remarkable clearness to the voice.

CHAPTER 22. BULBED LEEK: THIRTY-NINE REMEDIES.
Bulbed leek [Allium ampeloprasum or similar bulbing variety] produces the same effects as cut-leek, but in a more powerful degree. For persons troubled with spitting of blood, its juice is given together with powdered nut-galls or frankincense, or else gum acacia. Hippocrates, however, prescribes it without any admixture, and was of the opinion that it has the property of opening a contracted uterus and that, taken as food by women, it greatly promotes fecundity. Beaten up and mixed with honey, it cleanses ulcerous sores. It is beneficial for coughs, catarrhs, and all affections of the lungs and trachea, whether administered in the form of a ptisan [medicinal barley-gruel] or eaten raw — excluding the head — and must be taken without bread, on alternate days, even when there are purulent expectorations. Taken in this manner, it greatly improves the voice, acts as an aphrodisiac, and promotes sleep. The heads, boiled in two changes of water, arrest looseness of the bowels and long-standing fluxes; and a decoction of the outer skin [tunics] serves as a dye for grey hair.

CHAPTER 23. GARLIC: SIXTY-ONE REMEDIES.
Garlic has very powerful properties and is of great utility to persons adjusting to changes of water or locality. The very smell of it is said to drive away serpents and scorpions, and according to some authorities it is a cure for wounds inflicted by [text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: Leek (cut-leek / porrum sectivum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium porrum (common leek) or Allium ampeloprasum — cut/sectile variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple preparations: beaten with honey for ulcers; with vinegar for animal bites; with goats' gall or honeyed wine for ear affections; with woman's milk for tinnitus; juice with honey injected into nostrils/ear for headache; with undiluted wine for serpent/scorpion stings; with wine for lumbago; in barley-water for jaundice, dropsy, nephritic pain; eaten raw or as juice for spitting of blood, phthisis, catarrh; topically on wounds; as aphrodisiac and voice clarifier.

entities_mentioned.name: Bulbed leek (porrum capitatum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ampeloprasum (great-headed leek / elephant garlic precursor) or a cultivated bulbing leek variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a stronger version of cut-leek. Juice given with nut-galls, frankincense, or gum acacia for hemoptysis; plain per Hippocrates for uterine contraction and female fecundity; beaten with honey for ulcers; as ptisan or raw for coughs, catarrhs, lung and tracheal affections, purulent expectorations; promotes voice, sleep, and aphrodisiac effect; boiled heads for diarrhea and fluxes; decoction of outer coat as hair dye.

entities_mentioned.name: Syce (σῦκον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lacrimal fistula or chronic dacryocystitis / possibly a form of stye or conjunctival growth at the inner canthus
entities_mentioned.notes: Named from Greek for 'fig.' Pliny notes ambiguity: either a running condition at the eye's corner or livid pustules causing nocturnal restlessness.

entities_mentioned.name: Goats' gall
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with leeks in equal proportion with honeyed wine for ear affections.

entities_mentioned.name: Nut-galls
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oak galls — excrescences on Quercus infectoria or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Powdered and mixed with bulbed leek juice for hemoptysis.

entities_mentioned.name: Frankincense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra or related Boswellia species resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with bulbed leek juice for spitting of blood.

entities_mentioned.name: Gum acacia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia senegal or related Acacia species (gum arabic)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternative additive to bulbed leek juice for hemoptysis.

entities_mentioned.name: Garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Chapter begins; text incomplete. Described as powerful, useful for change of water/locality, repelling serpents and scorpions by smell alone.

entities_mentioned.name: Hippocrates
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as prescribing plain bulbed leek juice for uterine contraction and female fecundity.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment is a continuation mid-chapter (on cut-leek / porrum sectivum) and ends abruptly mid-sentence in the garlic chapter (Ch. 23). 2. Pliny's footnote numbering (15–27) is preserved and referenced in the translation where editorial notes appear. 3. 'Syce' (σῦκῃ) is glossed by Pliny himself as a fig-shaped condition of the eye corner — likely corresponding to lacrimal fistula or a fleshy outgrowth at the canthus; the alternative meaning (livid pustules) may refer to styes or blepharitis. 4. 'Merum' (pure/undiluted wine) is noted in footnote 18 and preserved as '[undiluted wine / merum]'. 5. The footnote that leeks would be 'of no utility whatever' against fungal poisoning and that the aphrodisiac claim is 'unfounded' are editorial glosses by Fee (the 19th-century annotator of the Bostock & Riley translation), not Pliny's own text; they are flagged accordingly. 6. 'Semi-sextarius' is a Roman liquid measure (approximately 160 mL); 'acetabulum' is approximately 67 mL. These are left in their Latin forms with brief contextual clarification. 7. The chapter heading 'SAUL ic. 225' appearing in the source is an OCR artifact from a running header/page reference and has been omitted from the cleaned text.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Garlic (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX)
results.original_text: of garlic is so powerful, that it is one of the most energetic vermifuges known; but at the same time it is so strong an excitant, that it is very liable to cause worse evils than the presence even of worms. every kind of wild beast, whether taken with the drink or food, or applied topically. Taken in wine, it is a remedy for the sting of the haemorrhois more particularly, acting as an emetic. We shall not be surprised too, that it acts as a powerful remedy for the bite of the shrew-mouse, when we find that it has the property of neutralizing aconite, otherwise known as "pardalianches." It neutralizes henbane, also, and cures the bites of dogs, when applied with honey to the wound. It is taken in drink also for the stings of serpents; and of its leaves, mixed with oil, a most valuable liniment is made for bruises on the body, even when they have swelled and formed blisters. Hippocrates is of opinion also, that fumigations made with garlic have the effect of bringing away the after-birth; and he used to employ the ashes of garlic, mixed with oil, for the cure of running ulcers of the head. Some persons have prescribed boiled garlic for asthmatic patients; while others, again, have given it raw. Diocles prescribes it, in combination with centaury, for dropsy, and to be taken in a split fig, to promote the alvine evacuations: taken fresh, however, in unmixed wine, with coriander, it is still more efficacious for that purpose. Some persons have given it, beaten up in milk, for asthma. Praxagoras used to prescribe garlic, mixed with wine, for jaundice, and with oil and pottage for the iliac passion: he employed it also in a similar form, as a liniment for scrofulous swellings of the neck. The ancients used to give raw garlic in cases of madness, and Diocles administered it boiled for phrenitis. Beaten up and taken in vinegar and water, it is very useful as a gargle for quinsy. Three heads of garlic, beaten up in vinegar, give relief in toothache: and a similar result is obtained by rinsing the mouth with a decoction of garlic, and inserting pieces of it in the hollow teeth. Juice of garlic is sometimes injected into the ears with goose-grease, and, taken in drink, or similarly injected, in combination with vinegar and nitre, it arrests phthiriasis and porrigo. Boiled with milk, or else beaten up and mixed with soft cheese, it is a cure for catarrhs. Employed in a similar manner, and taken with pease or beans, it is good for hoarseness, but in general it is found to be more serviceable cooked than raw, and boiled than roasted: in this last state, however, it is more beneficial to the voice. Boiled in oxymel, it has the effect of expelling tape-worm and other intestinal worms; and a pottage made of it is a cure for tenesmus. A decoction of garlic is applied topically for pains in the temples; and first boiled and then beaten up with honey, it is good for blisters. A decoction of it, with s[text breaks off]
results.translation: The medicinal power of garlic is so great that it is one of the most potent vermifuges [worm-expelling agents] known; yet at the same time it is so strong a stimulant that it is liable to cause worse troubles than even the presence of intestinal worms. [It is effective against] every kind of wild beast's venom, whether administered in drink or food, or applied externally. Taken in wine, it is a remedy especially against the sting of the haemorrhois [a venomous serpent], acting as an emetic. Nor should we be surprised that it acts as a powerful remedy for the bite of the shrew-mouse, given that it has the property of neutralizing aconite, also known as "pardalianches" [panther-strangle]. It likewise counteracts henbane, and cures dog bites when applied with honey to the wound. It is also taken in drink for serpent stings; and from its leaves, mixed with oil, a most valuable liniment is made for bruises on the body, even when they have swollen and formed blisters. Hippocrates likewise held that fumigations with garlic bring away the afterbirth [placenta]; and he used the ashes of garlic mixed with oil for the cure of running [suppurating] ulcers of the head. Some physicians have prescribed boiled garlic for asthmatic patients, while others have given it raw. Diocles prescribes it in combination with centaury for dropsy, and taken in a split fig to promote bowel evacuations [alvine evacuations]; taken fresh, however, in undiluted wine with coriander, it is still more effective for that purpose. Some have given it beaten up in milk for asthma. Praxagoras used to prescribe garlic mixed with wine for jaundice, and with oil and pottage for the iliac passion [intestinal obstruction/colic]; he also used it in a similar preparation as a liniment for scrofulous swellings of the neck. The ancients gave raw garlic in cases of madness, and Diocles administered it boiled for phrenitis [acute delirium/brain fever]. Beaten up and taken in vinegar and water, it is very useful as a gargle for quinsy [peritonsillar abscess]. Three heads of garlic beaten in vinegar give relief in toothache; and a similar result is obtained by rinsing the mouth with a decoction of garlic and inserting pieces of it into hollow teeth. Juice of garlic is sometimes injected into the ears with goose-grease, and taken in drink, or similarly injected in combination with vinegar and nitre [sodium nitrate/saltpeter], it arrests phthiriasis [louse infestation] and porrigo [scalp disease/ringworm]. Boiled with milk, or beaten and mixed with soft cheese, it is a cure for catarrhs. Taken in a similar manner with peas or beans, it is good for hoarseness; in general, however, it is found to be more serviceable cooked than raw, and boiled than roasted—in the roasted state, however, it is more beneficial to the voice. Boiled in oxymel [a preparation of honey and vinegar], it expels tapeworm and other intestinal worms; and a pottage made of it is a cure for tenesmus [painful straining at stool]. A decoction of garlic is applied topically for pains in the temples; and first boiled then beaten with honey, it is good for blisters. A decoction of it, with s[text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary subject of the entire passage; described as vermifuge, emetic, antidote, and remedy for numerous conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: haemorrhois
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a viper species, perhaps Vipera ammodytes or a related Old World viper; the name refers to a serpent whose bite causes hemorrhage
entities_mentioned.notes: Described by Lucan in the Pharsalia (IX.708 et seq.) and Nicander in Theriaca; bite causes bleeding from nose and ears.

entities_mentioned.name: shrew-mouse
entities_mentioned.notes: Bite treated with garlic; ancient belief in its venom is noted.

entities_mentioned.name: aconite / pardalianches
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum species (monkshood/wolfsbane); 'pardalianches' may also refer to Doronicum pardalianches or a related toxic plant
entities_mentioned.notes: The claim that garlic neutralizes aconite is noted by the translator (footnote 31) as unfounded.

entities_mentioned.name: henbane
entities_mentioned.notes: Garlic stated to neutralize its toxic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: centaury
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or Centaurea species
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with garlic by Diocles for dropsy.

entities_mentioned.name: coriander
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with fresh garlic in unmixed wine to promote bowel evacuations.

entities_mentioned.name: oxymel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of honey and vinegar (pharmaceutical vehicle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as medium in which garlic is boiled to expel tapeworm and intestinal worms.

entities_mentioned.name: goose-grease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered fat from Anser anser (domestic goose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as vehicle for ear injection with garlic juice.

entities_mentioned.name: nitre
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with garlic juice and vinegar for treatment of phthiriasis and porrigo.

entities_mentioned.name: phthiriasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated by garlic injected with vinegar and nitre.

entities_mentioned.name: porrigo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scalp disease; possibly tinea capitis (ringworm) or impetigo
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated alongside phthiriasis with garlic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: iliac passion
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated by Praxagoras with garlic, oil, and pottage.

entities_mentioned.name: phrenitis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acute delirium with fever; ancient diagnosis encompassing meningitis or encephalitic syndromes
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated by Diocles with boiled garlic.

entities_mentioned.name: quinsy
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with garlic beaten in vinegar and water as gargle.

entities_mentioned.name: tenesmus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Painful, ineffectual straining at stool; symptom of rectal or colonic inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with garlic pottage.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a continuation of a larger chapter on garlic from Pliny's Natural History, Book XX, Chapter 23. The opening lines are fragmentary due to the chunk boundary and lack a clear antecedent subject (supplied contextually as garlic). The text includes editorial footnotes from the 19th-century English edition (Riley/Bostock translation), reproduced here as footnotes 30–34. Footnote 31 explicitly questions the pharmacological claim that garlic neutralizes aconite. The segment ends mid-sentence ('A decoction of it, with s—'), indicating truncation at the chunk boundary. The term 'alvine evacuations' is period vocabulary for bowel movements. 'Iliac passion' is a classical/early modern term for intestinal obstruction or colic. 'Phrenitis' in ancient medicine referred to acute febrile delirium, not modern phrenology. OCR artifact 'haBmorrhois' has been silently corrected to 'haemorrhois'; 'BTATUIIAL' in the running head is a known OCR error for 'NATURAL.'

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Garlic, Lettuce, and Related Plants (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 23–25)
results.original_text:
The cure of suspected tumours. Mixed with sulphur and resin, garlic draws out the humours from fistulous sores, and employed with pitch, it will extract an arrow even from the wound. In cases of leprosy, lichen, and eruptions of the skin, it acts as a detergent, and effects a cure, in combination with wild marjoram, or else reduced to ashes, and applied as a liniment with oil and garum. It is employed in a similar manner, too, for erysipelas; and, reduced to ashes, and mixed with honey, it restores contused or livid spots on the skin to their proper colour. It is generally believed, too, that taken in the food and drink, garlic is a cure for epilepsy, and that a clove of it, taken in astringent wine, with an obolus' weight of silphium, will have the effect of dispelling quartan fever. Garlic cures coughs also, and suppressions of the chest, however violent they may be; to obtain which result, another method is followed, it being boiled with broken beans, and employed as a diet till the cure is fully effected. It is a soporific also, and in general imparts to the body an additional ruddiness of colour. Garlic acts as an aphrodisiac, beaten up with fresh coriander, and taken in pure wine. The inconveniences which result from the use of it, are dimness of the sight and flatulency; and if taken in too large quantities, it does injury to the stomach, and creates thirst. In addition to these particulars, mixed with spelt flour, and given to poultry in their food, it preserves them from attacks of the pip. Beasts of burden, it is said, will void their urine all the more easily, and without any pain, if the genitals are rubbed with garlic.

CHAP. 24. — THE LETTUCE: FORTY-TWO REMEDIES. THE GOAT-LETTUCE: FOUR REMEDIES.

The first kind of lettuce which grows spontaneously, is the one that is generally known as "goat-lettuce;" thrown into the sea, this vegetable has the property of instantaneously killing all the fish that come into its vicinity. The milky juice of this lettuce, left to thicken and then put into vinegar, is given in doses of two oboli, with the addition of one cyathus of water, to patients for dropsy. The stalk and leaves, bruised and sprinkled with salt, are used for the cure of wounds of the sinews. Pounded with vinegar, and employed as a gargle in the morning twice a month, they act as a preventive of toothache.

CHAP. 25. — CNIDION [CAESAPON?]: ONE REMEDY. ISATIS: ONE REMEDY. THE WILD LETTUCE: SEVEN REMEDIES.

There is a second kind of wild lettuce, known by the Greeks...
results.translation:
On the cure of suspected tumours: Mixed with sulphur and resin, garlic draws out the humours [fluids] from fistulous sores; employed with pitch, it will even extract an arrow from a wound. In cases of leprosy [morbus pedicularis], lichen [a skin disease], and eruptions of the skin, it acts as a detergent and effects a cure when combined with wild marjoram, or when reduced to ashes and applied as a liniment with oil and garum [fish-sauce]. It is employed in a similar manner for erysipelas; and, reduced to ashes and mixed with honey, it restores contused or livid spots on the skin to their proper colour. It is generally believed that, taken in food and drink, garlic is a cure for epilepsy, and that a clove of it taken in astringent wine with an obolus' weight of silphium will dispel quartan fever. Garlic also cures coughs and suppressions of the chest, however violent they may be; for this purpose another method is used: it is boiled with broken beans and employed as a dietary preparation until the cure is fully effected. It is also soporific [sleep-inducing], and in general imparts to the body an additional ruddiness of colour. Garlic acts as an aphrodisiac when beaten up with fresh coriander and taken in pure wine. The disadvantages resulting from its use are dimness of sight and flatulence; and if taken in too large quantities, it injures the stomach and causes thirst. Furthermore, mixed with spelt flour and given to poultry in their feed, it protects them from attacks of the pip [a poultry disease]. Beasts of burden, it is said, will void their urine more easily and without pain if the genitals are rubbed with garlic.

Chapter 24. — The Lettuce: Forty-Two Remedies. The Goat-Lettuce: Four Remedies.

The first kind of lettuce that grows spontaneously is the one generally known as "goat-lettuce" [caprina]; when thrown into the sea, this plant has the property of instantaneously killing all fish that come into its vicinity. The milky juice of this lettuce, left to thicken and then dissolved in vinegar, is administered in doses of two oboli with the addition of one cyathus of water to patients suffering from dropsy. The stalk and leaves, bruised and sprinkled with salt, are used to cure wounds of the sinews [tendons]. Pounded with vinegar and used as a gargle in the morning twice a month, they act as a preventive of toothache.

Chapter 25. — Caesapon [Cnidion?]: One Remedy. Isatis: One Remedy. The Wild Lettuce: Seven Remedies.

There is a second kind of wild lettuce, known by the Greeks...

entities_mentioned.name: Garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated: tumours, fistulous sores (mixed with sulphur and resin), arrow wounds (with pitch), leprosy, lichen, skin eruptions (with wild marjoram, or as ash-liniment with oil and garum), erysipelas, contusions/livid skin (ash with honey), epilepsy, quartan fever (with silphium in astringent wine), coughs, chest suppressions (boiled with broken beans), used as aphrodisiac (with coriander in wine), pip in poultry (with spelt flour), urinary difficulties in beasts of burden (topical rubbing). Side effects noted: dimness of sight, flatulence, stomach injury, thirst.
