entities_mentioned.name: Goat-lettuce (caprina)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lactuca scariola or a Euphorbia/tithymalus species; Pliny elsewhere calls it 'tithymalum', suggesting it may be a spurge rather than a true lettuce
entities_mentioned.notes: Reported to kill fish when thrown in the sea. Milky juice thickened in vinegar given orally (2 oboli + 1 cyathus water) for dropsy. Stalk and leaves bruised with salt for sinew wounds. Pounded with vinegar as a gargle twice monthly to prevent toothache. Fee doubts it is a true lettuce; Hardouin conjectures it is tithymalum (spurge).

entities_mentioned.name: Isatis
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in chapter heading with one remedy ascribed; text of that remedy is not included in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild lettuce (second kind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lactuca virosa or related wild Lactuca species
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as known by the Greeks; text is incomplete at segment end. Seven remedies attributed.

entities_mentioned.name: Broken beans
entities_mentioned.notes: Garlic boiled with broken beans as a dietary cure for coughs and chest suppressions.

results.translator_notes: 1. The footnote numbering in the source (33–40) is OCR artefact from the original Bostock & Riley translation; footnotes have been retained and clarified in context. 2. 'Morbus pedicularis' (footnote 33) is glossed by Pliny's translator as leprosy; it may refer to phthiriasis (louse-disease) rather than what modern medicine calls leprosy (Hansen's disease). 3. Footnote 35 records a textual variant: Pintianus proposed 'hirudines' (leeches) rather than 'arundines' (arrows/reeds), but the arrow reading is supported by Plinius Valerianus and Marcellus Empiricus. 4. 'CMSA PON' in the chapter heading (Chapter 25) is clearly an OCR error; the Latin text likely reads 'Caesapon' or 'Cnidion' — left as '[Caesapon/Cnidion?]' in the translation. 5. 'ISATT8' is an OCR error for 'ISATIS'. 6. Measures: obolus = small Greek weight (~0.72 g); cyathus = small liquid measure (~45 ml). 7. The segment is incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence in Chapter 25.

results.title_inferred: Hawk-Weed and Wild Lettuce Varieties: Properties and Remedies
results.original_text:
HAWK-WEED. The leaves of this lettuce, applied as a liniment with polenta, are used for the cure of ulcerous sores. This plant is found growing in the fields. A third kind, again, grows in the woods; the name given to it is "isatis." The leaves of this last, beaten up and applied with polenta, are very useful for the cure of wounds. A fourth kind is used by dyers of wool; in the leaves it would resemble wild lapathum, were it not that they are more numerous and darker. This lettuce has the property of stanching blood, and of healing phagedaenic sores and putrid spreading ulcers, as well as tumours before suppuration. Both the root as well as the leaves are good, too, for erysipelas; and a decoction of it is drunk for affections of the spleen. Such are the properties peculiar to each of these varieties.

CHAP. 26. — HAWK-WEED: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES.

The properties which are common to all the wild varieties are whiteness, a stem sometimes as much as a cubit in length, and a roughness upon the stalk and leaves. Among these plants there is one with round, short leaves, known to some persons as "hieracion;" from the circumstance that the hawk tears it open and sprinkles its eyes with the juice, and so dispels any dimness of sight of which it is apprehensive. The juice of all these plants is white, and in its properties resembles that of the poppy. It is collected at harvest-time, by making incisions in the stalk, and is kept in new earthen vessels, being renowned as a remedy for numerous maladies. Mixed with woman's milk, it is a cure for all diseases of the eyes, such as argema for instance, films on the eyes, scars and inflammations of all kinds, and dimness of the sight more particularly. It is applied to the eyes, too, in wool, as a remedy for defluxions of those organs. This juice also purges the bowels, taken in doses of two oboli in vinegar and water. Drunk in wine it is a cure for the stings of serpents, and the leaves and stalk of the plant are pounded and taken in vinegar. They are employed also as a liniment for wounds, the sting of the scorpion more particularly; combined, too, with oil and vinegar, they are similarly applied for the bite of the phalangium. They have the effect, also, of neutralizing other [poisons or injuries, text continues].
results.translation:
HAWK-WEED. The leaves of this lettuce [caesapon], applied as a liniment together with pearl-barley meal [polenta], are used for the cure of ulcerous sores. This plant is found growing in the fields. A third kind, moreover, grows in the woods; its name is 'isatis.' The leaves of this last variety, beaten up and applied with polenta, are very useful for the healing of wounds. A fourth kind is used by wool-dyers; its leaves would resemble those of wild lapathum [dock], were they not more numerous and darker in color. This lettuce has the property of stanching blood, and of healing phagedaenic [rapidly spreading, ulcerating] sores, putrid spreading ulcers, and tumors before suppuration. Both the root and the leaves are likewise beneficial for erysipelas; and a decoction of it is drunk for affections of the spleen. Such are the properties peculiar to each of these varieties.

CHAPTER 26. — HAWK-WEED: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES.

The characteristics common to all the wild varieties [of lettuce] are: a white [milky juice], a stem sometimes reaching as much as a cubit in length, and roughness upon both the stalk and leaves. Among these plants there is one with round, short leaves, known to some persons as 'hieracion' [hawk-weed]; so called from the belief that the hawk tears it open and sprinkles its eyes with the juice, thereby dispelling any dimness of vision it fears. The juice of all these plants is white, and in its medicinal properties resembles that of the poppy. It is collected at harvest-time by making incisions in the stalk, and is stored in new earthenware vessels, being renowned as a remedy for numerous ailments. Mixed with woman's milk, it is a cure for all diseases of the eyes — such as argema [corneal ulcer or white spot], films on the eyes, scars, and inflammations of every kind — and especially for dimness of sight. It is also applied to the eyes by means of wool, as a remedy for fluxions [discharges] affecting those organs. This juice likewise purges the bowels when taken in doses of two oboli [a small measure] dissolved in vinegar and water. Drunk in wine, it is a cure for the stings of serpents; the leaves and stalk of the plant are also pounded and taken in vinegar for the same purpose. They are moreover employed as a liniment for wounds, and especially for the sting of the scorpion; combined with oil and vinegar, they are applied in the same manner for the bite of the phalangium [a venomous spider]. They also have the effect of neutralizing other [venoms or noxious agents — text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: caesapon (a kind of wild lettuce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Sonchus sp. or Hieracium sp. (sow-thistle or hawkweed family, Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Footnote by Féé suggests searching among Sonchus or Hieracium varieties. Used as a liniment with polenta for ulcerous sores.

entities_mentioned.name: isatis (third kind, growing in woods)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis tinctoria L. (woad) in wild state
entities_mentioned.notes: Féé identifies this with Isatis tinctoria (woad) in its wild form. Leaves beaten and applied with polenta for wound healing. Féé notes that woad lacks milky juice, making Pliny's classification among the Lactuceae botanically problematic.

entities_mentioned.name: fourth kind (dyer's lettuce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Isatis tinctoria L. (woad) in cultivated form
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by wool-dyers. Leaves resemble wild lapathum but more numerous and darker. Properties: stanches blood, heals phagedaenic sores and spreading ulcers, reduces pre-suppurative tumors, treats erysipelas, and (as decoction) treats splenic ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: hieracion (hawk-weed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Crepis sp. or Hieracium sp. (Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named from Greek 'hierax' (hawk). Féé suggests varieties of genus Crepis. Round, short leaves. Milky white juice resembling poppy juice. Harvested by incision of stalk and stored in earthenware.

entities_mentioned.name: lapathum (wild dock)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a comparator for leaf morphology of the dyer's lettuce variety.

entities_mentioned.name: phalangium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A venomous spider, possibly Latrodectus sp. or Pholcus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term for a category of venomous spider; exact species uncertain. Bite treated with hawk-weed juice combined with oil and vinegar.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a nineteenth-century English translation of Pliny's Naturalis Historia (Book XX), not a non-English source requiring fresh translation; however, the task has been completed by producing a clean, scholarly rendering that corrects OCR errors (e.g., 'heaten' → 'beaten', 'kin d' → 'kind', 'NATUKAL' → 'NATURAL', 'tho Lactuoee' → 'the Lactuceae'), preserves technical Latin and Greek terminology in brackets, and extracts all materia medica entities. The footnotes embedded in the OCR text (numbered 41–50) have been used to inform entity identification but are not reproduced in the translation body. The phrase 'two oboli' is retained as a period measure of weight/volume. 'Argema' is retained as a technical ophthalmological term (corneal opacity/ulcer). 'Phagedaenic' is retained as a period medical term denoting a rapidly spreading, gangrenous ulceration. The segment ends mid-sentence due to chunking.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Properties of Lettuce (Cultivated and Wild) and Hawkweed
results.original_text: it to be given to dropsical patients, in doses of two oboli, with vinegar and one cyathus of wine. Some persons collect the juice of the cultivated lettuce as well, but it is not so efficacious as the other. We have already made mention, to some extent, of the peculiar properties of the cultivated lettuce, such as promoting sleep, allaying the sexual passions, cooling the body when heated, purging the stomach, and making blood. In addition to these, it possesses no few properties besides; for it has the effect of removing flatulency, and of dispelling eructations, while at the same time it promotes the digestion, without ever being indigestible itself. Indeed, there is no article of diet known that is a greater stimulant to the appetite, or which tends in a greater degree to modify it; it being the extent, either way, to which it is eaten that promotes these opposite results. In the same way, too, lettuces eaten in too large quantities are laxative, but taken in moderation they are binding. They have the effect, also, of attenuating the tough, viscous, phlegm, and, according to what some persons say, of sharpening the senses. They are extremely serviceable, too, to debilitated stomachs; for which purpose [ILLEGIBLE: dose in oboli] of sour sauce is added to them, the sharpness of which is modified by the application of sweet wine, to make it of the same strength as vinegar-sauce. If, again, the phlegm with which the patient is troubled is extremely tough and viscous, wine of squills or of wormwood is employed; and if there is any cough perceptible, hyssop wine is mixed as well. Lettuces are given with wild endive for coeliac affections, and for obstructions of the thoracic organs. White lettuces, too, are prescribed in large quantities for melancholy and affections of the bladder. Praxagoras recommends them for dysentery. Lettuces are good, also, for recent burns, before blisters have made their appearance: in such cases they are applied with salt. They arrest spreading ulcers, being applied at first with saltpetre, and afterwards with wine. Beaten up, they are applied topically for erysipelas; and the stalks, beaten up with polenta, and applied with cold water, are soothing for luxations of the limbs and spasmodic contractions; used, too, with wine and polenta, they are good for pimples and eruptions. For cholera lettuces have been given, cooked in the saucepan, in which case it is those with the largest stalk and bitter that are the best: some persons administer them, also, as an injection, in milk. These stalks boiled, are remarkably good, it is said, for the stomach: the summer lettuce, too, more particularly, and the bitter, milky lettuce, of which we have already made mention as the "meconis," have a soporific effect. This juice, in combination with woman's milk, is said to be extremely beneficial to the eyesight, if applied to the head.
results.translation: It [the juice of wild lettuce] is to be given to dropsical patients in doses of two oboli [a small unit of weight], with vinegar and one cyathus [a small cup measure] of wine. Some persons also collect the juice of the cultivated lettuce, but it is not so efficacious as that of the wild variety. We have already mentioned, to some extent, the peculiar properties of the cultivated lettuce: that it promotes sleep, allays sexual passion, cools the body when overheated, purges the stomach, and produces blood. Beyond these, it possesses many further properties: it removes flatulence and dispels eructations [belching], while at the same time it aids digestion without itself being indigestible. Indeed, there is no article of diet better known for stimulating the appetite or for modifying it; and it is the quantity consumed, in either direction, that produces these opposite effects. Similarly, lettuces eaten in too large a quantity are laxative, but taken in moderation they are binding. They also have the effect of thinning tough, viscous phlegm, and, as some say, of sharpening the senses. They are extremely serviceable to debilitated stomachs; for this purpose [a certain number of] oboli of sour sauce [oxygarum] is added to them, the sharpness of which is moderated by the addition of sweet wine, so as to reduce it to the strength of vinegar-sauce [hydrogarum]. If, moreover, the phlegm troubling the patient is extremely tough and viscous, wine of squills or of wormwood is employed; and if any cough is perceptible, hyssop wine is added as well. Lettuces are given together with wild endive for coeliac [bowel] complaints and for obstructions of the thoracic organs. White lettuces are also prescribed in large quantities for melancholy and for affections of the bladder. Praxagoras recommends them for dysentery. Lettuces are also beneficial for recent burns, before blisters have appeared: in such cases they are applied externally with salt. They arrest spreading ulcers, being applied first with saltpetre [nitre] and afterwards with wine. When beaten up, they are applied topically for erysipelas; and the stalks, beaten up with polenta [coarse grain meal] and applied with cold water, are soothing for dislocations of the limbs and spasmodic contractions. Used with wine and polenta, they are good for pimples and skin eruptions. For cholera, lettuces have been given cooked in a saucepan, those with the largest stalk and the most bitter flavour being considered the best for this purpose; some persons also administer them as an enema in milk. The boiled stalks are said to be remarkably good for the stomach. The summer lettuce especially, and the bitter, milky lettuce previously mentioned under the name "meconis," have a soporific effect. The juice of this plant, combined with woman's milk, is said to be extremely beneficial for the eyesight when applied to the head.

entities_mentioned.name: wild lettuce (juice)
entities_mentioned.notes: The inspissated milky juice, called 'lactucarium,' was used as a mild anodyne substitute for opium. Pliny's footnote 48 acknowledges modern doubt about many of its attributed effects.

entities_mentioned.name: cultivated lettuce
entities_mentioned.notes: Pliny attributes numerous medicinal properties to it; modern botany and pharmacology confirm only some (mild sedative, digestive aid). Claims of blood-making and purging are noted as erroneous in footnotes.

entities_mentioned.name: meconis (bitter, milky lettuce)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lactuca virosa or a related opium-bearing species
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'meconis' relates to poppy-like (opiate) properties; cross-referenced by Pliny to an earlier discussion. Possibly the same as wild lettuce or a distinct bitter variety.

entities_mentioned.name: wild endive
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with lettuce for coeliac affections and thoracic obstructions.

entities_mentioned.name: wine of squills
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (sea squill) macerated in wine
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical expectorant and diuretic preparation used for tough phlegm.

entities_mentioned.name: wine of wormwood
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to treat tough viscous phlegm; well-attested in classical and medieval pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: hyssop wine
entities_mentioned.notes: Added when cough is present alongside phlegmatic complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: polenta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coarsely ground grain meal (barley or emmer wheat flour)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical vehicle/paste medium for external applications of lettuce preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: saltpetre (nitre)
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied to spreading ulcers in the first stage of treatment before wine is used.

entities_mentioned.name: sour sauce (oxygarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxygarum: mixture of garum (fish sauce) and vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: A Roman condiment-medicine used as a digestive stimulant.

entities_mentioned.name: Praxagoras
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek physician of the 4th century BCE, pupil of Nicomachus, teacher of Herophilus; cited here as recommending lettuce for dysentery.

results.translator_notes: This segment is sourced from Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia, dealing with lettuce (Lactuca spp.) and transitioning toward a chapter on hawkweed. The source text includes original editorial footnotes (numbered 48–57) that were part of an earlier English edition translation and commentary, which have been retained in the cleaned original text for scholarly context. The dose reading '[ILLEGIBLE: dose in oboli]' reflects a gap marked by asterisks ('* *54') in the source, indicating a lacuna in the Latin manuscript at that point. The claim that lettuce 'makes blood' and acts as a purgative is flagged by Pliny's own annotators as erroneous by modern standards. The term 'cholera' here likely refers to a gastrointestinal flux condition rather than Vibrio cholerae infection, which was not identified until the 19th century.
